pull request updates the documentation related to floating-point precision in NumPy, specifically addressing the issue of incorrect determinant calculations for certain matrices.

- Added a note on how the determinant of the matrix np.array([[5, 5, 6], [7, 7, 5], [4, 4, 8]]) is expected to be zero, highlighting the observed precision issues.

- Included a code example demonstrating how to compute the determinant and check if it is close to zero using np.isclose().

- Ensured consistency in formatting and improved clarity throughout the documentation.

This change aims to provide users with a better understanding of floating-point arithmetic in NumPy and how to handle potential precision errors.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1809095763","pull_request_review_id":2382565431,"id":1809095763,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85r1JxT","diff_hunk":"@@ -342,6 +342,35 @@ range of possible values.
     >>> np.power(100, 100, dtype=np.float64)
     1e+200
 
+Floating point precision
+========================
+
+Many functions in NumPy, especially those in `numpy.linalg`, involve floating-point
+arithmetic, which can introduce small inaccuracies due to the way computers 
+represent decimal numbers. For instance, when computing the determinant of the 
+following matrix:
+
+    >>> import numpy as np
+    >>> a = np.array([[5, 5, 6], [7, 7, 5], [4, 4, 8]])
+    >>> print(np.linalg.det(a)) # Result is close to 0 due to floating-point precision
+    -3.1974423109204565e-14
+    
+
+One may receive a result like ``-3.1974423109204565e-14`` instead of ``0``. This is
+a behavior common to all frameworks that use floating point arithmetic.
+
+To handle such cases, it's advisable to set a threshold for comparison when checking
+if a determinant is effectively zero. For example:
+
+    >>> np.isclose(np.linalg.det(a), 0)  # Check for closeness to 0
+    True","path":"doc/source/user/basics.types.rst","commit_id":"2d9ae53afade1165caa767df207539ed82b2422b","original_commit_id":"8fd9bb75abf478e4be9b25382e4094098dffb3f4","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"While that example is important, it demonstrates just one issue with floating point arithmetic (not exactly representing the base-10 decimals used in the literals).

pull request updates the documentation related to floating-point precision in NumPy, specifically addressing the issue of incorrect determinant calculations for certain matrices.

- Added a note on how the determinant of the matrix np.array([[5, 5, 6], [7, 7, 5], [4, 4, 8]]) is expected to be zero, highlighting the observed precision issues.

- Included a code example demonstrating how to compute the determinant and check if it is close to zero using np.isclose().

- Ensured consistency in formatting and improved clarity throughout the documentation.

This change aims to provide users with a better understanding of floating-point arithmetic in NumPy and how to handle potential precision errors.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1809166393","pull_request_review_id":2382684356,"id":1809166393,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85r1bA5","diff_hunk":"@@ -342,6 +342,35 @@ range of possible values.
     >>> np.power(100, 100, dtype=np.float64)
     1e+200
 
+Floating point precision
+========================
+
+Many functions in NumPy, especially those in `numpy.linalg`, involve floating-point
+arithmetic, which can introduce small inaccuracies due to the way computers 
+represent decimal numbers. For instance, when computing the determinant of the 
+following matrix:
+
+    >>> import numpy as np
+    >>> a = np.array([[5, 5, 6], [7, 7, 5], [4, 4, 8]])
+    >>> print(np.linalg.det(a)) # Result is close to 0 due to floating-point precision
+    -3.1974423109204565e-14
+    
+
+One may receive a result like ``-3.1974423109204565e-14`` instead of ``0``. This is
+a behavior common to all frameworks that use floating point arithmetic.
+
+To handle such cases, it's advisable to set a threshold for comparison when checking
+if a determinant is effectively zero. For example:
+
+    >>> np.isclose(np.linalg.det(a), 0)  # Check for closeness to 0
+    True","path":"doc/source/user/basics.types.rst","commit_id":"2d9ae53afade1165caa767df207539ed82b2422b","original_commit_id":"8fd9bb75abf478e4be9b25382e4094098dffb3f4","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"It's not really algorithm-specific concerning just `det()`, which is why I recommended a general note here. - Added a note on how the determinant of the matrix np.array([[5, 5, 6], [7, 7, 5], [4, 4, 8]]) is expected to be zero, highlighting the observed precision issues.

- Included a code example demonstrating how to compute the determinant and check if it is close to zero using np.isclose().

- Ensured consistency in formatting and improved clarity throughout the documentation.

This change aims to provide users with a better understanding of floating-point arithmetic in NumPy and how to handle potential precision errors.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1809170867","pull_request_review_id":2382696546,"id":1809170867,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85r1cGz","diff_hunk":"@@ -342,6 +342,30 @@ range of possible values.
     >>> np.power(100, 100, dtype=np.float64)
     1e+200
 
+Floating point precision
+========================
+
+Many functions in NumPy, especially those in `numpy.linalg`, involve floating-point
+arithmetic, which can introduce small inaccuracies due to the way computers 
+represent decimal numbers. For instance, when performing basic arithmetic operations 
+involving floating-point numbers:
+
+    >>> 0.3 - 0.2 - 0.1  # This does not equal 0 due to floating-point precision
+    -2.7755575615628914e-17
+
+To handle such cases, it's advisable to use functions like `np.isclose` to compare 
+values, rather than checking for exact equality:
+
+    >>> np.isclose(0.3 - 0.2 - 0.1, 0, rtol=1e-05)  # Check for closeness to 0","path":"doc/source/user/basics.types.rst","commit_id":"2d9ae53afade1165caa767df207539ed82b2422b","original_commit_id":"2d9ae53afade1165caa767df207539ed82b2422b","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"From the docs:

"""
For finite values, isclose uses the following equation to test whether
two floating point values are equivalent.

 absolute(`a` - `b`) <= (`atol` + `rtol` * absolute(`b`))
"""

Because `b==0`, setting the value of `rtol` won't change anything. In order to demonstrate controlling the tolerance in this case, you must use `atol` in this example and talk about absolute tolerances, not relative tolerances.","created_at":"2024-10-21T16:56:22Z","updated_at":"2024-10-21T16:56:22Z","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27602#discussion_r1809170867","pull_request_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27602","author_association":"MEMBER","_links":{"self":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1809170867"},"html":{"href":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27602#discussion_r1809170867"},"pull_request":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27602"}},"reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1809170867/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"start_line":null,"original_start_line":null,"start_side":null,"line":359,"original_line":359,"side":"RIGHT","in_reply_to_id":1809085405,"original_position":18,"position":18,"subject_type":"line"},"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27602","id":2134154776,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAA3dP85_NJ4Y","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27602","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27602.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27602.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27602","number":27602,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"Update {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1809208842","pull_request_review_id":2382794451,"id":1809208842,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85r1lYK","diff_hunk":"@@ -342,6 +342,35 @@ range of possible values.
     >>> np.power(100, 100, dtype=np.float64)
     1e+200
 
+Floating point precision
+========================
+
+Many functions in NumPy, especially those in `numpy.linalg`, involve floating-point
+arithmetic, which can introduce small inaccuracies due to the way computers 
+represent decimal numbers. For instance, when computing the determinant of the 
+following matrix:
+
+    >>> import numpy as np
+    >>> a = np.array([[5, 5, 6], [7, 7, 5], [4, 4, 8]])
+    >>> print(np.linalg.det(a)) # Result is close to 0 due to floating-point precision
+    -3.1974423109204565e-14
+    
+
+One may receive a result like ``-3.1974423109204565e-14`` instead of ``0``. This is
+a behavior common to all frameworks that use floating point arithmetic.
+
+To handle such cases, it's advisable to set a threshold for comparison when checking
+if a determinant is effectively zero. For example:
+
+    >>> np.isclose(np.linalg.det(a), 0)  # Check for closeness to 0
+    True","path":"doc/source/user/basics.types.rst","commit_id":"2d9ae53afade1165caa767df207539ed82b2422b","original_commit_id":"8fd9bb75abf478e4be9b25382e4094098dffb3f4","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Here.","created_at":"2024-10-21T17:18:07Z","updated_at":"2024-10-21T17:18:07Z","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27602#discussion_r1809208842","pull_request_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27602","author_association":"MEMBER","_links":{"self":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1809208842"},"html":{"href":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27602#discussion_r1809208842"},"pull_request":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27602"}},"reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1809208842/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"start_line":null,"original_start_line":362,"start_side":"RIGHT","line":null,"original_line":366,"side":"RIGHT","in_reply_to_id":1808558330,"original_position":25,"position":null,"subject_type":"line"},"pull_request":{ pull request updates the documentation related to floating-point precision in NumPy, specifically addressing the issue of incorrect determinant calculations for certain matrices.

- Added a note on how the determinant of the matrix np.array([[5, 5, 6], [7, 7, 5], [4, 4, 8]]) is expected to be zero, highlighting the observed precision issues.

- Included a code example demonstrating how to compute the determinant and check if it is close to zero using np.isclose().

- Ensured consistency in formatting and improved clarity throughout the documentation.

This change aims to provide users with a better understanding of floating-point arithmetic in NumPy and how to handle potential precision errors.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/18124","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/18124/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/18124/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/18124/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/18124","id":779322657,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU3NzkzMjI2NTc=","number":18124,"title":"BUG: Multiprocessing issue for .npz files","user":{"login":"Th-Fo","id":42180212,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQyMTgwMjEy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/42180212?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo","html_url":"https://github.com/Th-Fo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":12,"created_at":"2021-01-05T17:24:34Z","updated_at":"2024-10-21T18:17:53Z","closed_at":"2024-05-30T14:01:29Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Accessing the entries of a NpzFile object can raise a BadZipFile error (for an intact .npz file) under the following circumstances:
* First, `np.load('my_file.npz')` is called in some process.
* Afterwards, this process is forked, e.g. via multiprocessing module.
* Several of these processes (either multiple child processes, or the parent plus >=1 child processes) access its entries after forking.
The occurrence of this error depends on the .npz file, on the order in which the entries are read out, and apparently also on the machine (on the RAM size?), but seems deterministic for a certain configuration. It happens on all Python and NumPy versions I have tested, including Python 3.9.1 and NumPy 1.19.2 (newest versions available for me). It is not clear to me whether the problem is on the level of Numpy, zipfile, or somewhere else.

Minimal example:

a) Generating the dataset. This part is a bit "tricky" because different .npz files are needed to trigger the bug on different machines; however, for all machines I have tried, I was able to find a .npz file which reproduced the bug (the Python/NumPy version didn't play a role then).

Among the easiest configurations with a high chance to trigger the bug are

```
# especially for machines with <=32 GB RAM
import numpy as np
np.savez(
		'data.npz',
		item_0=np.random.randn(10000),
		item_1=np.random.randn(200),
		item_2=np.random.randn(10000)
)
```

and

```
# especially for machines with >150 GB RAM
import numpy as np
np.savez(
		'data.npz',
		item_0=np.random.randn(100000),
		item_1=np.random.randn(200000)
)
```

If this doesn't work, try

```
import collections
import numpy as np

typical_size = 1000 # also try 10000 or 100000
sizes = np.round(-typical_size*np.log(np.random.rand(20))).astype(int)

np.savez(
		'data.npz',
		**collections.OrderedDict([
			('item_%d'%idx, np.random.randn(size))
			for idx, size in enumerate(sizes)
		])
)
```

b) apply the "recipe" described in the beginning:

```
import multiprocessing
import numpy as np
import time

def func(data):
	for _ in data.values(): # accessing the .npz file from the child processes
		time.sleep(0.2) # to make the access order deterministic

data = np.load('data.npz') # loading the .npz file in the parent process
for _ in range(2):
	# launching child processes inside this loop, passing data as an argument to the target function
	subproc = multiprocessing.Process(target=func, args=[data])
	subproc.start()
	time.sleep(0.1) # to make the access order deterministic
```

result:

```
Process Process-2:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/multiprocessing/process.py", line 258, in _bootstrap
    self.run()
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/multiprocessing/process.py", line 93, in run
    self._target(*self._args, **self._kwargs)
  File "minimal_example.py", line 6, in func
    for _ in data.values():
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/_collections_abc.py", line 762, in __iter__
    yield self._mapping[key]
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/site-packages/numpy/lib/npyio.py", line 256, in __getitem__
    magic = bytes.read(len(format.MAGIC_PREFIX))
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/zipfile.py", line 872, in read
    data = self._read1(n)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/zipfile.py", line 962, in _read1
    self._update_crc(data)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/zipfile.py", line 890, in _update_crc
    raise BadZipFile("Bad CRC-32 for file %r" % self.name)
zipfile.BadZipFile: Bad CRC-32 for file 'item_1.npy'
```

Remarks:
* The error does not occur if np.load is called within each child process, or if the parent process passes an own NpzFile instance to each child process. This might serve as a quick fix, but at least doubles the runtime for calling np.load if both the parent and the child process need to access the considered file, and might be complicated to implement for applications into which multiprocessing is deeply built in.

* Dependent on the .npz file and the machine, slightly different errors can occur, including:

  zipfile.BadZipFile: Bad magic number for file header
  zipfile.BadZipFile: Truncated file header
  zipfile.BadZipFile: File name in directory 'item_2.npy' and header b'it' differ.
  EOFError

* For a fixed order in which the entries are read out, the entries whose readout cause an error are deterministic. If the exception is caught and readout is repeated, accessing an entry succeeds or fails always like it did for the first time (I guess the reason for this is in the caching). This behavior can be seen, for example, by replacing func in the minimal example with

```
import traceback
def func(data):
	for key in 3*list(data.keys()):
		try:
			data[key]
		except Exception:
			traceback.print_exc()
		time.sleep(0.2) # to make access order deterministic
		pass
```

pull request updates the documentation related to floating-point precision in NumPy, specifically addressing the issue of incorrect determinant calculations for certain matrices.

- Added a note on how the determinant of the matrix np.array([[5, 5, 6], [7, 7, 5], [4, 4, 8]]) is expected to be zero, highlighting the observed precision issues.

- Included a code example demonstrating how to compute the determinant and check if it is close to zero using np.isclose().

- Ensured consistency in formatting and improved clarity throughout the documentation.

This change aims to provide users with a better understanding of floating-point arithmetic in NumPy and how to handle potential precision errors.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/comments/1809958343","pull_request_review_id":2383935860,"id":1809958343,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOAA3dP85r4cXH","diff_hunk":"@@ -342,6 +342,35 @@ range of possible values.
     >>> np.power(100, 100, dtype=np.float64)
     1e+200
 
+Floating point precision
+========================
+
+Many functions in NumPy, especially those in `numpy.linalg`, involve floating-point
+arithmetic, which can introduce small inaccuracies due to the way computers 
+represent decimal numbers. For instance, when computing the determinant of the 
+following matrix:
+
+    >>> import numpy as np
+    >>> a = np.array([[5, 5, 6], [7, 7, 5], [4, 4, 8]])
+    >>> print(np.linalg.det(a)) # Result is close to 0 due to floating-point precision
+    -3.1974423109204565e-14
+    
+
+One may receive a result like ``-3.1974423109204565e-14`` instead of ``0``. This is
+a behavior common to all frameworks that use floating point arithmetic.
+
+To handle such cases, it's advisable to set a threshold for comparison when checking
+if a determinant is effectively zero. For example:
+
+    >>> np.isclose(np.linalg.det(a), 0)  # Check for closeness to 0
+    True","path":"doc/source/user/basics.types.rst","commit_id":"2d9ae53afade1165caa767df207539ed82b2422b","original_commit_id":"8fd9bb75abf478e4be9b25382e4094098dffb3f4","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Not regarding `det()` per se. pull request updates the documentation related to floating-point precision in NumPy, specifically addressing the issue of incorrect determinant calculations for certain matrices.

- Added a note on how the determinant of the matrix np.array([[5, 5, 6], [7, 7, 5], [4, 4, 8]]) is expected to be zero, highlighting the observed precision issues.

- Included a code example demonstrating how to compute the determinant and check if it is close to zero using np.isclose().

- Ensured consistency in formatting and improved clarity throughout the documentation.

This change aims to provide users with a better understanding of floating-point arithmetic in NumPy and how to handle potential precision errors.

LinearOperator dtype determination broken","user":{"login":"lobpcg","id":42650045,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQyNjUwMDQ1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/42650045?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/lobpcg","html_url":"https://github.com/lobpcg","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lobpcg/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lobpcg/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lobpcg/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lobpcg/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lobpcg/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lobpcg/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lobpcg/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lobpcg/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/lobpcg/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":37334597,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzMzNDU5Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/defect","name":"defect","color":"e11d21","default":false,"description":"A clear bug or issue that prevents SciPy from being installed or used as expected"},{"id":37340854,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MDg1NA==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/scipy.sparse.linalg","name":"scipy.sparse.linalg","color":"0366d6","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2023-09-07T23:03:03Z","updated_at":"2024-10-24T00:39:39Z","closed_at":"2024-10-24T00:39:39Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe your issue.

Calling the `LinearOperator` without specifying the dtype activates the dtype determination in ```scipy/sparse/linalg/_interface.py", line 182, in _init_dtype
        self.dtype = np.asarray(self.matvec(v)).dtype```
that looks strange. 

In `/scipy/sparse/linalg/_interface.py` the code in question is
```
    def _init_dtype(self):
        """Called from subclasses at the end of the __init__ routine.
        """
        if self.dtype is None:
            v = np.zeros(self.shape[-1])
            self.dtype = np.asarray(self.matvec(v)).dtype
```

 `v.dtype` is `np.float64` so `self.dtype` will be at least  `np.float64`, like in the docstring example:
```
    >>> import numpy as np
    >>> from scipy.sparse.linalg import LinearOperator
    >>> def mv(v):
    ...     return np.array([2*v[0], 3*v[1]])
    ...
    >>> A = LinearOperator((2,2), matvec=mv)
    >>> A
    <2x2 _CustomLinearOperator with dtype=float64>
```
even though it only multiplies by `int`, so should not   `self.dtype` be `int` in this example?
Naively, is not `self.dtype` suppose to match the `dtype` of the underlying matrix implicitly used for the product? Maybe use `v = np.zeros(self.shape[-1], type=np.int8)` i.e. with the "smallest" `dtype`?

Reference issue
Toward gh-15928

#### What does this implement/fix?
SciPy's `foldnorm` and `foldcauchy` are examples of folded distributions, which "fold" the PDF from the left of the origin to the right. In terms of random variables, this corresponds with taking the absolute value. `halfnorm`, `halfgennorm`, `halflogistic`, and `halfcauchy` are are all special cases of folded distributions.

This PR adds a class that represents folded distributions and a function `abs` that takes the absolute value of a random variable. For example, if `X = stats.Normal()`, `Y = abs(X)` is distributed according to the folded normal. While I was at it, I went ahead and implemented the `__abs__` operator. We should probably still have our own `abs` function for the same reasons that NumPy includes `abs` in addition to overriding `__abs__` (probably documentation and discoverability). 

```python3
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats

X = abs(stats.Normal() + 1.2)
X0 = stats.foldnorm(1.2)

x = np.linspace(-1, 5, 300)
pdf = X.pdf(x)
pdf0 = X0.pdf(x)
plt.plot(x, pdf, label='PDF underlying `X`')
plt.plot(x, pdf0, '--', label='PDF underlying `X0`')
plt.legend()
plt.title("PDF of a folded normal distribution")
plt.show()
```

![{AD726DA4-D326-4FA3-883F-CB6C885F70F5}](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1365d315-8a79-451a-9fc9-1b3487ea2ef0)

#### Additional information
Together with gh-21746, this will allow us to implement `__pow__` for even values of `other`. I think gh-21746 alone is enough to implement `__rpow__`. These methods can be added in a follow-up PR.

 * PEP 3118 buffer protocol

 * Implementing PEP 3118 is somewhat convoluted because of the desirata:

#include "dtypemeta.h"

/*************************************************************************
 ****************   Implement Buffer Protocol ****************************
 *************************************************************************/

/*************************************************************************
 * PEP 3118 buffer protocol
 *
 * Implementing PEP 3118 is somewhat convoluted because of the desirata:
 *
 * - Don't add new members to ndarray or descr structs, to preserve binary
 *   compatibility. (Also, adding the items is actually not very useful,
 *   since mutability issues prevent an 1 to 1 relationship between arrays
 *   and buffer views.)
 *
 * - Don't use bf_releasebuffer, because it prevents PyArg_ParseTuple("s#", ...
 *   from working. Breaking this would cause several backward compatibility
 *   issues already on Python 2.6.
 *
 * - Behave correctly when array is reshaped in-place, or it's dtype is
 *   altered.
 *
 * The solution taken below is to manually track memory allocated for
 * Py_buffers.
 *************************************************************************/

might be eliminated by regenerating those files with a newer lapack version

however note the following (current release is lapack-3.12.0)

```
$ tail -n 6 numpy/numpy/linalg/lapack_lite/README.rst
The output C files in git use the LAPACK source from the LAPACK_ page, using
version 3.2.2. Unfortunately, newer versions use newer FORTRAN features, which
are increasingly not supported by ``f2c``. As these are found, the patch files
will need to be changed to re-express new constructs with legacy constructs.

.. _LAPACK: https://netlib.org/lapack/index.html
$

`SeedSequence` supports `None`, `int`, `Sequence[int]` for entropy. It turns out that anythign with `len(obj)==0` in the leading dimension will be accepted and is identical to `0`. This includes `ndarrays` with any number of dimensions. 

This might be more of a FR than a bug, but the lack of standardization could increase end users error risk.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

np.random.SeedSequence(0).generate_state(1)
np.random.SeedSequence([]).generate_state(1)
np.random.SeedSequence([[],[]]).generate_state(1)
np.random.SeedSequence(np.empty((0,10,10))).generate_state(1)
np.random.SeedSequence(np.empty((0,10,10), dtype=float)).generate_state(1)
```


### Error message:
No error. The output of the above is

array([2968811710], dtype=uint32)
array([2968811710], dtype=uint32)
array([2968811710], dtype=uint32)
array([2968811710], dtype=uint32)
```



### Python and NumPy Versions:

Describe your issue.

Looks like the `L D L^T` decompositions provided by `scipy` are wrong. See a simple example below.

The attached code yields:
```
array([[1. , 0. , 0. ],
       [1.4, 1. , 0. ],
       [1.2, 0.5, 1. ]])
```
The right output would be:
```
array([[1. , 0. , 0. ],
       [1.2, 1. , 0. ],
       [1.4, 2.0, 1. ]])
```
Note that the correct D is:
```
array([4.5e+01, 1.2e+00, 0.0e0])
```

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np

from scipy.linalg import ldl

M = np.arange(9).reshape([3, 3])
A = M.T @ M
lu, d, perm = ldl(A)

print(lu[perm,:])
```


### Error message

```shell
N/A (not an error, just an incorrect result)
```

Pivoting gets used to avoid numerical difficulties, resulting in the nontrivial `perm` permutation. In an LDL decomposition, the `L` and `D` matrices of the pivoted problem in general will not match up with the `L` and `D` matrices of the unpivoted problem. The result you are expecting comes from an unpivoted implementation. The `lu`, `d`, and `perm` outputs all obey the properties demanded of the LDL decomposition (`lu @ d @ lu.T` recovers `A` within numerical error, `lu[perm, :]` is lower triangular with unit diagonals, and `d` is diagonal). I don't think there is a bug here.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2348017990/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Describe your issue.

Looks like the `L D L^T` decompositions provided by `scipy` are wrong. See a simple example below.

The attached code yields:
```
array([[1. , 0. , 0. ],
       [1.4, 1. , 0. ],
       [1.2, 0.5, 1. ]])
```
The right output would be:
```
array([[1. , 0. , 0. ],
       [1.2, 1. , 0. ],
       [1.4, 2.0, 1. ]])
```
Note that the correct D is:
```
array([4.5e+01, 1.2e+00, 0.0e0])
```

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np

from scipy.linalg import ldl

M = np.arange(9).reshape([3, 3])
A = M.T @ M
lu, d, perm = ldl(A)

print(lu[perm,:])
```


### Error message

```shell
N/A (not an error, just an incorrect result)
```

If you look at the signature for [`np.multiply`](https://numpy.org/doc/2.1/reference/generated/numpy.multiply.html), you will see that it can take an optional `out=` argument that will be the array to write the data to.

When you do `a[0] = a[0] * 1.5`, this gets turned into, roughly, `a[0] = np.multiply(a[0], 1.5)`. No `out=` argument specified. So it only looks at the dtypes of the inputs to decide which dtype-specific implementation to dispatch to. For our purposes, there are 2 implementations to look at, one that does `int64 * int64 -> int64` and one that does `float64 * float64 -> float64`. Since the inputs are mixed, we try our casting rules and see that `int64` can be upcast to `float64`, so the integer input is cast to a `float64` and the `float64 * float64 -> float64` implementation gets used. The result of that gets downcast back to `int64` when `a[0] = ...` goes to assign the value back into the array.

When you do `a[0] *= 1.5`, however, something a little different happens. Since the output destination is now known (`a[0]` is an `int64` array), the implementation of the inplace multiplication operator essentially does this: `np.multiply(a[0], 1.5, out=a[0])`. This causes the ufunc to look for a loop that can deal with `int64 * float64 -> int64`. Since our rules decide based on the inputs that `float64 * float64 -> float64` is the only implementation that we can use, it then checks to see if the `float64` output can be downcast back to the `int64` output that is fixed. Our default rules dictate that we cannot safely downcast that way (for ufunc resolution).

It *is* a good question why the downcast is allowed during assignment (e.g. `a[0] = 4.5`) but not during inplace arithmetic. Allowing the downcast during assignment is a deliberate design decision that allows us to more comfortably work with low-precision arrays and still use plain Python `int`s and `float`s to manipulate them without having to first convert them all to the low-precision scalar types. We can't really do that in the inplace arithmetic because the ufuncs don't know that they are being called in that context. We know that this is a kind of an inconsistency, but it's one that we are generally comfortable with.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2351039063/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

With the pending closure of https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20964, PRIMA will be integrated with SciPy, and while the primary goal of that PR and the issue it closes is to update COBYLA, PRIMA contains 4 other algorithms for derivative free optimization. They are:

* LINCOA - LINearly Constrained Optimization algorithm - for problems with linear constraints and bound constraints (bot no nonlinear constraints)
* BOBYQA - Bound Optimization BY Quadratic Approximation - for problems with bound constraints, but no linear/nonlinear constraints
* UOBYQA - Unconstrained Optimization BY Quadratic Approximation - for unconstrained problems
* NEWUOA - Newer algorithm for unconstrained problems.

More information can be found at @zaikunzhang's website: https://www.zhangzk.net/software.html

Since PRIMA will already be integrated in SciPy with the above PR, exposing these should be rather simple. I could do these myself, and I would propose to add them one by one to smooth out the review process, but these could also be a nice set of "good first issue" for folks interested in contributing to SciPy. I wonder if the maintainers have someone in mind who wants to contribute and can add these, or perhaps we can mark this issue as a good first issue and leave it up for a month or so to see if we have any bites?

Like I said I'd be happy to get this done myself and I'd even be willing to take responsibility for maintaining it in the future, but I thought it might be prudent to pause and ask if there are others who would like to take this opportunity to contribute.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

dumped directory strings exposed a few typos

scipy/signal/windows/_windows.py

fixed typo in the following and changed to lowercase

```diff
-     The Kaiser window is a very good approximation to the Digital Prolate
-     Spheroidal Sequence, or Slepian window, which is the transform which
+     The Kaiser window is a very good approximation to the discrete prolate
+     spheroidal sequence, or Slepian window, which is the transform which
```

Is this comment correct, i.e collection not a sequence but sequences?

```
$ sed -n '2035,2044p' scipy/scipy/signal/windows/_windows.py
    # Here we want to set up an optimization problem to find a sequence
    # whose energy is maximally concentrated within band [-W,W].
    # Thus, the measure lambda(T,W) is the ratio between the energy within
    # that band, and the total energy. This leads to the eigen-system
    # (A - (l1)I)v = 0, where the eigenvector corresponding to the largest
    # eigenvalue is the sequence with maximally concentrated energy. The
    # collection of eigenvectors of this system are called Slepian
    # sequences, or discrete prolate spheroidal sequences (DPSS). Only the
    # first K, K = 2NW/dt orders of DPSS will exhibit good spectral
    # concentration
$
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21617/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21617/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2372216261","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21617#issuecomment-2372216261","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21617","id":2372216261,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6NZSXF","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-24T19:36:50Z","updated_at":"2024-09-24T19:36:50Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"The language there is fine. noted in #27461, when an array is summarized, the `repr` no longer makes it clear what the shape is. This is a bit odd, since for the case of a zero-sized array, the shape is shown when it is not clear what it would be (i.e., when the size is zero but the shape multi-dimensional). Similarly, if the dtype is not obvious, it is shown. So, this PR also shows the shape when summaries are made:
```
In [2]: np.arange(1001)
Out[2]: array([   0,    1,    2, ...,  998,  999, 1000], shape=(1001,))
In [3]: np.arange(1001, dtype="i2")
Out[3]: 
array([   0,    1,    2, ...,  998,  999, 1000],
      dtype=int16, shape=(1001,))
```
One can get the old behaviour by using `legacy='2.1'` - I'm not sure this is really required; happy to remove it if desired.

Fixes #27461

p.s. The first commit reorganizes the routine to make it easier to add the shape, while the second has the actual enhancement and tests.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27482/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27482/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2383655238","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27482#issuecomment-2383655238","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27482","id":2383655238,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86OE7FG","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-09-30T16:25:14Z","updated_at":"2024-09-30T16:25:14Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"We're not going to change the default representation without a wider discussion on [the mailing list](https://mail.python.org/mailman3/lists/numpy-discussion.python.org/) and a NEP. This will cause a ton of churn for docstring examples in all downstream projects, so we won't pull the trigger on this without a significant consensus from a lot of stakeholders. I have my doubts that we'll get that kind of strong agreement at this time. [NEP 51](https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0051-scalar-representation.html) was the last change of this kind, and it was significantly smaller in scope.

In any case, since there is no `shape=` argument to `np.array()`, this particular choice for `__repr__` is probably not going to happen.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2383655238/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Reference issue
gh-15928
Closes gh-4538
Closes gh-8053
Closes gh-9321
Closes gh-10106 [^1]
Closes gh-10328
Closes gh-11465 [^2]
Closes gh-11746 [^3]
Closes gh-12192 [^3]
Closes gh-13504
Closes gh-13986
Closes gh-14895
Closes gh-16877
Closes gh-16998 [^4]
Closes gh-17307 [^5]
Closes gh-17620
Closes gh-18782 [^6]
Closes gh-19524 [^4]

[^1]: Additional improvements are possible, but random sampling in this infrastructure is still much faster due to vectorization of numerical integration and inversion.
[^2]: Added methods for inverse of the logarithm of the CDF
[^3]: Already closed for the old infrastructure, but addressed in the new infrastructure.
[^4]: Actually we still have an equivalent issue. Because instantiating a `Generator` is very slow, `ContinuousDistribution` does not convert the default `rng=None`into a PRNG until the first time it is used. If `None` were converted to the global `RandomState`, this would avoid the original issue exactly as suggested in gh-19524. However, `None` gets converted to a new `Generator` (`np.random.default_rng()`), which assumes an unpredicatable state. Therefore, if a random variable is copied before `sample` is called, copies will get different `Generator`s, whereas if it is copied *after* `sample` is called, copies will get the same `Generator`. There are several possible solutions; we need to decide which approach to take.
[^5]: It is the _policy_ of this infrastructure that errors will not be raised, but NaNs will be produced. This is more appropriate in a vectorized context: an error due to one bad element of an array should not cause the rest to fail.
[^6]: Infrastructure converts all inputs to consistent, at least float dtype.

#### What does this implement/fix?
See gh-15928. Compared to the branch reviewed in that RFC, there are only a few new commits here, beginning with 5fdb881d6d76d5bddae041a9b3d2f028c5df290f, that respond to some of the comments in the RFC as summarized by @tupui.

#### Additional information
There are many follow-up PRs planned before 1.15.0 (e.g. array API support, various distribution classes, test improvements, accuracy benchmarking and documentatino, tutorials, `fit`, other `TransformedDistrbution` subclasses, `DiscreteDistribution`, etc.). I certainly missed them, or at least missed that they entailed discussions that I wouldn't be able to opt into as they are convened. I did not miss when you've [waved interested parties off](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/15928#issuecomment-1905514881) from taking a look at what you were working on, saying that you were "not ready for receiving external comments nor review of our design and implementation". You will have to forgive me for coming to the conclusion that there was a division between "internal" deliberations among this "stats" team with the rest of the scipy team being "external".

There's a lot to be gained by having high-bandwidth online voice meetings to discuss things among a more focused group of attendees, but [like the NumPy Documentation Team](https://mail.python.org/archives/list/numpy-discussion@python.org/thread/7LR7LCGGI3PDIG6GJDKF46J5EAB4X7SQ/), we announce each one on the mailing list/Discourse, keep the attendance open to the whole development team, and report the minutes of each meeting as they happen. I can also understand why this subgroup may want to do this work without that amount of immediate transparency, with very good intentions, but that's [not the way SciPy develops](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/15928#issuecomment-2148014752).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2264134781/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
https://discuss.scientific-python.org/t/inclusion-of-aaa-rational-approximation-in-scipy/1282
#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR implements the core AAA algorithm for rational approximation. For more background please see the mailing list post linked above. There are a number of extra features that would be good to include however I would prefer to leave these as follow ups to keep the diff manageable.

Features:

- Computes near-optimal rational approximation for provided data which can then be evaluated
- Can compute the roots, poles and residues of the rational approximation

Planned Features:

- Cleanup of spurious poles
-  Differentiation of approximation
- Lawson iteration for optimal approximation

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
https://discuss.scientific-python.org/t/inclusion-of-aaa-rational-approximation-in-scipy/1282
#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
This PR implements the core AAA algorithm for rational approximation. For more background please see the mailing list post linked above. There are a number of extra features that would be good to include however I would prefer to leave these as follow ups to keep the diff manageable.

Features:

- Computes near-optimal rational approximation for provided data which can then be evaluated
- Can compute the roots, poles and residues of the rational approximation

Planned Features:

- Cleanup of spurious poles
-  Differentiation of approximation
- Lawson iteration for optimal approximation

Describe your issue.

In `scipy.odr`, the order of the coefficients (`beta`) are inconsistent. Using the `odr.unilinear` model, the offset, or y-intercept, is `beta[1]`, in `odr.multilinear`, the offset is `beta[0]`.

This then also changes the order of `output.cov_beta`, obviously.

This is really confusing as one can also use the multilinear model as a unilinear one. I wanted to update a generic model to be able to handle multiple variables, so I updated the unilinear to multilinear, thinking it would work. But their definitions are swapped:

**Multilinear:**
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/87c46641a8b3b5b47b81de44c07b840468f7ebe7/scipy/odr/_models.py#L10-L14

**Unilinear:**
https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/87c46641a8b3b5b47b81de44c07b840468f7ebe7/scipy/odr/_models.py#L214-L215


In my opinion, the unilinear definition should use the one from multilinear:
```python
def _unilin(B, x):
    return x*B[1] + B[0]


def _unilin_fjd(B, x):
    return np.ones(x.shape, float) * B[1]


def _unilin_fjb(B, x):
    _ret = np.concatenate((np.ones(x.shape, float), x))
    _ret.shape = (2,) + x.shape

    return _ret
```


I'm not sure why both version even exist, considering the multilinear model can be used for unilinear problems.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import numpy as np
from scipy import odr

x = np.linspace(0, 10)
y = 0.1 + 3 * x
data = odr.Data(x, y)

out1 = odr.ODR(data, odr.unilinear).run()
out2 = odr.ODR(data, odr.multilinear).run()

print(out1.beta)
print(out2.beta)

print(out1.cov_beta)
print(out2.cov_beta)
```


### Error message

```shell
[3.  0.1]
[0.1 3. ]
[[ 0.02305822 -0.11529155]
 [-0.11529155  0.77646333]]
[[ 0.77647742 -0.11529665]
 [-0.11529665  0.02305943]]
```

It was one of the first libraries I built for public consumption and still holds a special place in my heart.

For reasons that I go into [on the ODRPACK95 thread](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/7107#issuecomment-2198805027), I consider the design of `scipy.odr` (especially the parts touched by the issues you've opened) to be largely a dead end. They were maybe an interesting experiment 20 years ago, but the rest of scipy moved in a different, better direction since then. I would love to see this energy applied to making a `scipy.optimize.odr` function that is designed using the modern `scipy.optimize` conventions. Building it off of ODRPACK95 (the main new feature being bound constraints) gives us the problemtunity to use the `scipy.optimize` machinery to express bound constraints.

So for a change like this, which would involve some deprecation and code breakage, I am not enthusiastic about. The only code breakage I'll readily support right now is the eventual deprecation of `scipy.odr` as a whole in favor of a reimplimentation of the functionality in `scipy.optimize`.

I know it's frustrating to propose a small change only to have it thrown back at you with "why don't you make this much bigger change instead!" But I don't know what your enthusiasm budget actually is, so I wanted to throw out this opportunity and see if that budget _is_ big enough to feed the more impactful (IMO) changes. Thank you!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2266735067/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Pickling most of the `scipy.odr` objects is not possible, as the `__getattr__` method is called when loading the object. At this point, the object does not have any attributes attached.

As the current implementation assumes that `self.meta` exists. As it does not, it enters an infinite loop.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

I would like pickling to work.
I would suggest catching this edge-case by checking if the `meta` attribute is requested in `__getattr__`, and raising an `AttributeError`

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

The current `odr.polynomial` model does not seem to support multiple input values (meaning `x` has the shape *(m, n)*).

This is probably true for some of the other models as well.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

I think the built-in models should support a few more cases. Of course, not everything can be included and it is easy to use your own model. But I think this is still useful for enough people.

I would like the `polynomial` and possibly the other models to support multiple inputs.

pickling","user":{"login":"TiborVoelcker","id":72939048,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcyOTM5MDQ4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/72939048?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker","html_url":"https://github.com/TiborVoelcker","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The `__getattr__` method of the `odr` objects led to a recursion error when unpickling. This is because, when unpickling, the instance is checked for a `__setstate__` method, before the any attributes are added.
The implementation before assumed that the attributes are already set.

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
Closes gh-21306.

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
Fixes pickling behavior of the `scipy.odr` objects. To be specific, this fixes an issue while unpickling: The instance is checked for a `__setstate__` method. As the odr object don't have this method, the `__getattr__` method is called. At this stage, the instance does not have any attributes attached yet.
This enters an infinite recursion loop, as the `__getattr__` implementation assumes that all attributes are present and accesses the `meta` attribute. As this does not exist yet, the `__getattr__` method is called with again, to find the `meta` attribute, entering the recursion loop.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
Only the pickling and unpickling of the most important odr objects is tested. The other should pickle normally anyway.
Also the tests only confirm the pickling and unpickling works without errors and don't check the correctness of the resulting object. I did not see a reason to test for pickling","user":{"login":"TiborVoelcker","id":72939048,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcyOTM5MDQ4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/72939048?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker","html_url":"https://github.com/TiborVoelcker","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The `__getattr__` method of the `odr` objects led to a recursion error when unpickling. This is because, when unpickling, the instance is checked for a `__setstate__` method, before the any attributes are added.
The implementation before assumed that the attributes are already set.

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
Closes gh-21306.

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
Fixes pickling behavior of the `scipy.odr` objects. To be specific, this fixes an issue while unpickling: The instance is checked for a `__setstate__` method. As the odr object don't have this method, the `__getattr__` method is called. At this stage, the instance does not have any attributes attached yet.
This enters an infinite recursion loop, as the `__getattr__` implementation assumes that all attributes are present and accesses the `meta` attribute. As this does not exist yet, the `__getattr__` method is called with again, to find the `meta` attribute, entering the recursion loop.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
Only the pickling and unpickling of the most important odr objects is tested. The other should pickle normally anyway.
Also the tests only confirm the pickling and unpickling works without errors and don't check the correctness of the resulting object. I did not see a reason to test for pickling","user":{"login":"TiborVoelcker","id":72939048,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcyOTM5MDQ4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/72939048?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker","html_url":"https://github.com/TiborVoelcker","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/TiborVoelcker/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"The `__getattr__` method of the `odr` objects led to a recursion error when unpickling. This is because, when unpickling, the instance is checked for a `__setstate__` method, before the any attributes are added.
The implementation before assumed that the attributes are already set.

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
Closes gh-21306.

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
Fixes pickling behavior of the `scipy.odr` objects. To be specific, this fixes an issue while unpickling: The instance is checked for a `__setstate__` method. As the odr object don't have this method, the `__getattr__` method is called. At this stage, the instance does not have any attributes attached yet.
This enters an infinite recursion loop, as the `__getattr__` implementation assumes that all attributes are present and accesses the `meta` attribute. As this does not exist yet, the `__getattr__` method is called with again, to find the `meta` attribute, entering the recursion loop.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
Only the pickling and unpickling of the most important odr objects is tested. The other should pickle normally anyway.
Also the tests only confirm the pickling and unpickling works without errors and don't check the correctness of the resulting object. I did not see a reason to test for #### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
Closes gh-21306.

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
Fixes pickling behavior of the `scipy.odr` objects. To be specific, this fixes an issue while unpickling: The instance is checked for a `__setstate__` method. As the odr object don't have this method, the `__getattr__` method is called. At this stage, the instance does not have any attributes attached yet.
This enters an infinite recursion loop, as the `__getattr__` implementation assumes that all attributes are present and accesses the `meta` attribute. As this does not exist yet, the `__getattr__` method is called with again, to find the `meta` attribute, entering the recursion loop.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
Only the pickling and unpickling of the most important odr objects is tested. The other should pickle normally anyway.
Also the tests only confirm the pickling and unpickling works without errors and don't check the correctness of the resulting object. #### What does this implement/fix?

`__getattr__` method of the `odr` objects led to a recursion error when unpickling. This is because, when unpickling, the instance is checked for a `__setstate__` method, before the any attributes are added.
The implementation before assumed that the attributes are already set.

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
Closes gh-21306.

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
Fixes pickling behavior of the `scipy.odr` objects. To be specific, this fixes an issue while unpickling: The instance is checked for a `__setstate__` method. As the odr object don't have this method, the `__getattr__` method is called. At this stage, the instance does not have any attributes attached yet.
This enters an infinite recursion loop, as the `__getattr__` implementation assumes that all attributes are present and accesses the `meta` attribute. As this does not exist yet, the `__getattr__` method is called with again, to find the `meta` attribute, entering the recursion loop.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
Only the pickling and unpickling of the most important odr objects is tested. The other should pickle normally anyway.
Also the tests only confirm the pickling and unpickling works without errors and don't check the correctness of the resulting object. I did not see a reason to test for `__getattr__` method of the `odr` objects led to a recursion error when unpickling. This is because, when unpickling, the instance is checked for a `__setstate__` method, before the any attributes are added.
The implementation before assumed that the attributes are already set.

#### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
Closes gh-21306.

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
Fixes pickling behavior of the `scipy.odr` objects. To be specific, this fixes an issue while unpickling: The instance is checked for a `__setstate__` method. As the odr object don't have this method, the `__getattr__` method is called. At this stage, the instance does not have any attributes attached yet.
This enters an infinite recursion loop, as the `__getattr__` implementation assumes that all attributes are present and accesses the `meta` attribute. As this does not exist yet, the `__getattr__` method is called with again, to find the `meta` attribute, entering the recursion loop.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
Only the pickling and unpickling of the most important odr objects is tested. The other should pickle normally anyway.
Also the tests only confirm the pickling and unpickling works without errors and don't check the correctness of the resulting object. #### Reference issue
<!--Example: Closes gh-WXYZ.-->
Closes gh-21306.

#### What does this implement/fix?
<!--Please explain your changes.-->
Fixes pickling behavior of the `scipy.odr` objects. To be specific, this fixes an issue while unpickling: The instance is checked for a `__setstate__` method. As the odr object don't have this method, the `__getattr__` method is called. At this stage, the instance does not have any attributes attached yet.
This enters an infinite recursion loop, as the `__getattr__` implementation assumes that all attributes are present and accesses the `meta` attribute. As this does not exist yet, the `__getattr__` method is called with again, to find the `meta` attribute, entering the recursion loop.

#### Additional information
<!--Any additional information you think is important.-->
Only the pickling and unpickling of the most important odr objects is tested. The other should pickle normally anyway.
Also the tests only confirm the pickling and unpickling works without errors and don't check the correctness of the resulting object. I did not see a reason to test for Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.

Pickling most of the `scipy.odr` objects is not possible, as the `__getattr__` method is called when loading the object. At this point, the object does not have any attributes attached.

As the current implementation assumes that `self.meta` exists. As it does not, it enters an infinite loop.

### Describe the solution you'd like.

I would like pickling to work.
I would suggest catching this edge-case by checking if the `meta` attribute is requested in `__getattr__`, and raising an `AttributeError`

### Describe alternatives you've considered.

+---
+title: "SPEC 7 — Seeding pseudo-random number generation"
+date: 2023-04-19
+author:
+  - "Stéfan van der Walt <stefanv@berkeley.edu>"
+  - "Sebastian Berg <sebastianb@nvidia.com>"
+  - "Pamphile Roy <roy.pamphile@gmail.com>"
+  - "Matt Haberland <mhaberla@calpoly.edu>"
+  - Other participants in the discussion <not.yet@named.org>"
+discussion: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/14322
+endorsed-by:
+---
+
+## Description
+
+Currently, libraries across the ecosystem provide various APIs for seeding random number generation.
+This SPEC suggests a unified, pragmatic API, taking into account technical and historical factors.
+Adopting such a uniform API will simplify the user experience, especially for those who rely on multiple projects.
+
+We recommend:
+
+- avoiding the use of global state and legacy bitstream generators, and
+- standardizing the usage and interpretation of an `rng` keyword for seeding.
+
+We suggest implementing these principles by:
+
+- deprecating the use of `numpy.random.seed` to control the random state,
+- deprecating the use of `random_state`/`seed` arguments in favor of a consistent `rng` argument, and
+- using `numpy.random.default_rng` to validate the `rng` argument and instantiate a `Generator`.
+
+Note that `numpy.random.default_rng` does not accept instances of `RandomState`, so use of `RandomState` to control the seed is effectively deprecated, too.","path":"spec-0007/index.md","commit_id":"044ff761350bbeb3672bd63827fc1681ff38e752","original_commit_id":"044ff761350bbeb3672bd63827fc1681ff38e752","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"1. We intended to add the coercion of `RandomState` to `default_rng()` (following our experience with SciPy's QMC version of `check_random_state()` which coerces things to `Generator`s) but never got around to it. It's a good idea, but a little bit optional. Once people are passing objects to `rng=` keywords, they probably won't be doing so with `RandomState` objects. But it'll be a good corner case to patch up. This is on NumPy.

2. The use case of `RandomState` inside of unit tests is about calling `RandomState` methods explicitly inside of unit tests to create data arrays. The use case is not passing `RandomState` instances to the functions under test. Those functions will soon all be using `Generator`s inside their internals, obliterating any of the frozen functionality of `RandomState` Describe your issue.

`stats.geom.rvs` returns negative values when `p` is small.  The support is strictly positive and should never return these values. 

When validating this in R, I am able to get valid values for substantially smaller values of `p` and this should mirror that kind of performance.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy.stats import geom

# This returns a valid output
geom.rvs(0.0000000001, random_state = 1234) #(2125986401)
# Adding an extra zero will cause it to fail as will any smaller number
geom.rvs(0.0000000001, random_state = 1234) #(-2147483648)
```


### Error message

```shell
-2147483648
```

I still think it's acceptable to do nothing except document the behavior (Matt's findings concerning the parameter space is very informative to include, as well as an example of how one can check for saturation oneself).

But if we do want to have a warning, let me make one more pitch for doing it based on the passed input parameters rather than observing a saturated output. My main principle when it comes to warnings is that they need to be actionable for the user of the function. If the user can't change anything under their control to avoid triggering the warning, don't warn. As a kind of corollary, I think that when users make choices under their control that would lead to the warning-worthy outcomes depending on the things that are outside of their control, we should usually still warn them. Otherwise, we get heisenbugs that are hard to track down. A coder uses `geom.rvs()` with these extreme parameters and it happens to work fine with the PRNG state they tested it, but then in actual use, there are a bunch of warnings. But only sometimes.

There are two things that determine whether or not this `rvs()` method saturates the output: the parameters and the PRNG state. Practically speaking, the user only controls the parameters, not the PRNG state (certainly not in a way to control whether or not it saturates, except in contrived, party-trick scenarios). Every PRNG state is equally valid (or invalid), so I think it behooves us to think in terms of the ensemble of possible results when deciding to warn, not just when a particular PRNG state happens to push the result to the saturation limit. Our finite arithmetic always distorts the probability distribution, even aside from saturation. Under this rubric, we consider it warning-worthy when the distortion is high enough (for some value of "enough").  Even when we have very reasonable `p` values like 0.3, we're distorting the distribution. AFAICT, when `p==0.3`, our algorithm can _never_ generate the maximum `int64` integer (or even much smaller integers); it is _not_ actually true that every parameter choice has a finite chance of saturating. We just don't care about that particular distortion because the true probabilities were so low to begin with that the amount of distortion to the distribution is small. Working through the ziggurat constants, I think it's really only _possible_ to start saturating at around `p=1e-18`.

Also, more specific to this case, when `p<eps`, in addition to the saturation, we also start to observe wider and wider gaps between possible integer outputs below the saturation limit. That's an additional distortion that is only possible to detect by looking at the input parameters and not the result.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2282833401/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Reference issue
gh-18372

#### What does this implement/fix?
gh-18372 reported that `stats.geom.rvs` could produce negative values, which are not in the support of the distribution. This fixes it by following the convention of `Generator.geometric` in recent NumPy versions: replace negative values with the maximum integer.

```python3
import numpy as np
np.random.RandomState(294582935).geometric(1e-30)  # -9223372036854775808
np.random.default_rng(294582935).geometric(1e-30)  # 9223372036854775807
np.iinfo(np.int64).max  # 9223372036854775807
```

Currently, it does the work regardless of whether `random_state` is a `Generator` or `RandomState`, but if there is a follow-up to emit the warning per the suggesition in gh-18372, I can check the `random_state` type (and NumPy version, if need be) before determining the maximum integer and doing `where`.

Describe your issue.

`stats.geom.rvs` returns negative values when `p` is small.  The support is strictly positive and should never return these values. 

When validating this in R, I am able to get valid values for substantially smaller values of `p` and this should mirror that kind of performance.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy.stats import geom

# This returns a valid output
geom.rvs(0.0000000001, random_state = 1234) #(2125986401)
# Adding an extra zero will cause it to fail as will any smaller number
geom.rvs(0.0000000001, random_state = 1234) #(-2147483648)
```


### Error message

```shell
-2147483648
```

We often don't have nearly enough information about the user's context to suggest the right place to intervene, which might be far upstream of our library function. People solve linear systems for lots of reasons in lots of different domains, and how the ill-conditioned matrix comes into play varies. The root cause might be because the mesh in the PDE discretization is less than ideal. It might be something that could be resolved by numerical preconditioning (possibly with a different kind of solver) regardless of where the matrix comes from.

No warning message has to be comprehensive, in fact, being short and internet-searchable can be more valuable because people can find documentation and discussions that help them explore their options with higher context.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2282924934/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Reference issue
gh-15928
Closes gh-4538

Closes gh-10106 [^1]
Closes gh-10328
Closes gh-11465 [^2]
Closes gh-11746 [^3]

Closes gh-13504
Closes gh-13986
