Closes gh-16998 [^4]
Closes gh-17307 [^5]
Closes gh-17620
Closes gh-18782 [^6]
Closes gh-19524 [^4]

[^1]: Additional improvements are possible, but random sampling in this infrastructure is still much faster due to vectorization of numerical integration and inversion.
[^2]: Added methods for inverse of the logarithm of the CDF
[^3]: Already closed for the old infrastructure, but addressed in the new infrastructure.
[^4]: Actually we still have an equivalent issue. Because instantiating a `Generator` is very slow, `ContinuousDistribution` does not convert the default `rng=None`into a PRNG until the first time it is used. If `None` were converted to the global `RandomState`, this would avoid the original issue exactly as suggested in gh-19524. However, `None` gets converted to a new `Generator` (`np.random.default_rng()`), which assumes an unpredicatable state. Therefore, if a random variable is copied before `sample` is called, copies will get different `Generator`s, whereas if it is copied *after* `sample` is called, copies will get the same `Generator`. There are several possible solutions; we need to decide which approach to take.
[^5]: It is the _policy_ of this infrastructure that errors will not be raised, but NaNs will be produced. This is more appropriate in a vectorized context: an error due to one bad element of an array should not cause the rest to fail.
[^6]: Infrastructure converts all inputs to consistent, at least float dtype.

#### What does this implement/fix?
See gh-15928. Compared to the branch reviewed in that RFC, there are only a few new commits here, beginning with 5fdb881d6d76d5bddae041a9b3d2f028c5df290f, that respond to some of the comments in the RFC as summarized by @tupui.

#### Additional information
There are many follow-up PRs planned before 1.15.0 (e.g. array API support, various distribution classes, test improvements, accuracy benchmarking and documentatino, tutorials, `fit`, other `TransformedDistrbution` subclasses, `DiscreteDistribution`, etc.). Have to start somewhere!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21050/reactions","total_count":5,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":5,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21050/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2284199579","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21050#issuecomment-2284199579","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21050","id":2284199579,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6IJh6b","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-12T14:52:06Z","updated_at":"2024-08-12T14:52:06Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> The way I imagine this working is that the `rng` that can be stored in an attribute specifies a default which is used if no `rng` is passed into `sample`. Passing an `rng` into `sample` overrides this default. (If one passes an `rng` into sample for a derived distribution, perhaps we could use this `rng` to generate seeds for constructing new `rngs` that are passed into the `sample` method of each "atomic" distribution that went into the construction of the derived distribution.) If this behavior is well documented, and it is clearly explained when passing an `rng` to `sample` should be preferred over storing `rng`s as attributes and vice versa, I think this arrangement could possibly be acceptable, although I'm not confident in this judgment, and would like to hear more opinions.

This is more or less what the current infrastructure does. It has [bugs](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/19524) that will be exacerbated by using `Generator` instead of `RandomState` (no cheap global instance to default to) as well as having a lot more instances (versus the global instances in the old infrastructure).

> My understanding is that having more fine grained control of the randomness when sampling from these derived distributions is what motivated @mdhaber to allow each "atomic" distribution to carry its own random state.

I'm not sure why you would want to do that. Generally, you'll want to use one PRNG instance passed to each of them to use serially. It's one of those rare times in a programmer's life that there really aren't tradeoffs: it's the safest, most performant, _and_ easiest-to-code option. Finally, you get to Pick Three. You want separate PRNG states for things like concurrency or time-travel (which is really just a form of concurrency) and avoid them otherwise.

There is nothing about treating an object as a random variable that requires it to bind to a PRNG upon instantiation. It's not the thing that turns a mere distribution object into a random variable object. And even for full-fledged RV objects (with the fancy composition functionality), you still want to bind to PRNG states late because the choice for what state to use happens best at the time that you sample from them and not when you instantiate them. It's just an orthogonal coding decision that should be reflected by an orthogonal API.

> If we do the simple thing of having the `rng` just be passed to `sample` now, I think it should be possible in the future to either update distributions to also function as random variables in a backwards compatible way, or to create a separate random variable class that could be used in a random variable API.

Yes, that would still be possible, though I expect that after experience of using the simple API, you won't find it necessary to add to it.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2284199579/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

gh-15928
Closes gh-4538

Closes gh-10328

Closes gh-11746 [^3]

Closes gh-13504

Closes gh-16998 [^4]
Closes gh-17307 [^5]
Closes gh-17620
Closes gh-18782 [^6]
Closes gh-19524 [^4]

[^1]: Additional improvements are possible, but random sampling in this infrastructure is still much faster due to vectorization of numerical integration and inversion.
[^2]: Added methods for inverse of the logarithm of the CDF
[^3]: Already closed for the old infrastructure, but addressed in the new infrastructure.
[^4]: Actually we still have an equivalent issue. Because instantiating a `Generator` is very slow, `ContinuousDistribution` does not convert the default `rng=None`into a PRNG until the first time it is used. If `None` were converted to the global `RandomState`, this would avoid the original issue exactly as suggested in gh-19524. However, `None` gets converted to a new `Generator` (`np.random.default_rng()`), which assumes an unpredicatable state. Therefore, if a random variable is copied before `sample` is called, copies will get different `Generator`s, whereas if it is copied *after* `sample` is called, copies will get the same `Generator`. There are several possible solutions; we need to decide which approach to take.
[^5]: It is the _policy_ of this infrastructure that errors will not be raised, but NaNs will be produced. This is more appropriate in a vectorized context: an error due to one bad element of an array should not cause the rest to fail.
[^6]: Infrastructure converts all inputs to consistent, at least float dtype.

#### What does this implement/fix?
See gh-15928. Compared to the branch reviewed in that RFC, there are only a few new commits here, beginning with 5fdb881d6d76d5bddae041a9b3d2f028c5df290f, that respond to some of the comments in the RFC as summarized by @tupui.

#### Additional information
There are many follow-up PRs planned before 1.15.0 (e.g. array API support, various distribution classes, test improvements, accuracy benchmarking and documentatino, tutorials, `fit`, other `TransformedDistrbution` subclasses, `DiscreteDistribution`, etc.). Have to start somewhere!","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21050/reactions","total_count":5,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":5,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21050/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2286325623","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21050#issuecomment-2286325623","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/21050","id":2286325623,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc6IRo93","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-13T13:57:24Z","updated_at":"2024-08-13T13:57:24Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> could the issues you've pointed out in the passage I've quoted be resolved by only allowing users to call `sample` without passing an `rng` if the `rng` attribute has been set?

No, they are problems with holding onto an `rng` attribute regardless of what else you do. There are many use cases where we want to do certain operations with distribution/RV state (like passing by reference or cloning) where we'd make a different choice for PRNG state. Consequently, encapsulating the two together in one object creates these problems.

> Late binding seems to work fine for examples like this too, but one thing to consider is that it seems like it makes it easier for implementation details to impact randomness, so that if something changes the order in which unique symbols are taken when generating internal samples, the values generated by the outer call to `sample` with the same `Generator` could change from one SciPy version to the next.

Intrinsic to any use of pseudorandomness, I'm afraid; I don't think early-/late-binding makes much of a difference. We make no guarantees about version-to-version stability in exact PRNG samples currently. This would be no different. In non-trivial downstream code, even other non-PRNG-related changes to `scipy` (e.g. an improvement to a function in `scipy.linalg`) would affect the fine details of the simulation too, so it's not worth much extra effort in the PRNG-related parts of `scipy`.

> Do you mean not adding to it in only in terms of how random state is controlled for sampling?

Yes.

> My main concern about passing a RNG to a method was that in the future we could have other methods in need of RNG and then have the need to pass it to every call of methods.

If that's the main concern, then I think that possible convenience is firmly outweighed by the problems that arise from early binding that we've documented here.

> For code that doesn’t care about reproducibility, it doesn’t have to pass an rng, and a method could simply use the “default” rng.

Right, the default of `rng=None` is always permitted. We use [the standard pattern](https://blog.scientific-python.org/numpy/numpy-rng/#passing-a-numpy-rng-around) of using `rng = np.random.default_rng(rng)` to coerce the input, just like is done here in the early-binding version. We don't have a global PRNG anymore (from our experience with `RandomState`, it causes too many problems; thread-locality only solves some of them), but a new one is created afresh with OS entropy. We don't need to invent new machinery.

```python
def sample(self, size=None, rng=None):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(rng)
    ...
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2286325623/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

distribution","user":{"login":"mdhaber","id":6570539,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY1NzA1Mzk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6570539?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber","html_url":"https://github.com/mdhaber","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":37340722,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MDcyMg==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/scipy.stats","name":"scipy.stats","color":"0366d6","default":false,"description":""},{"id":37340735,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzM0MDczNQ==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/enhancement","name":"enhancement","color":"bfe5bf","default":true,"description":"A improvement"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-08-14T19:44:09Z","updated_at":"2024-08-14T21:01:55Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/pulls/21389","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21389","diff_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21389.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21389.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"#### Reference issue
Closes gh-6528, gh-13644 (duplicate)
Supersedes gh-6739, gh-18109
https://mail.python.org/pipermail/scipy-dev/2020-May/024185.html

#### What does this implement/fix?
This implements the normal-inverse-gamma distribution. (The multivariate generalization can be a separate PR, probably implemented in an unrelated class.)

There were two earlier PRs for this distribution:
- gh-6739 was closed because it became inactive. It was also not vectorized, and there may have been some license-related concerns.
- gh-18109 was closed because it did not implement the (bivariate) normal-inverse-gamma distribution. 

This PR addresses these concerns: it implements the normal-inverse-gamma distribution with arguments $x$ and $\sigma^2$ and shapes $\mu$, $\lambda$, $\alpha$, and $\beta$, all of which can be broadcastable arrays.

#### Additional information
To addressed the points from the [closing comment](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/18109#issuecomment-1458993415) of gh-18109:

> It looks like the distribution is an oddball - it doesn't fit our univariate distribution infrastructure or the multivariate distribution infrastructure. 

There is essentially zero multivariate distribution infrastructure, so there are no conflicts with the infrastructure. This is the first of the multivariate distributions that is not arbitrary-dimensional, but that does not really present any complications. The more unusual thing is that the two dimensions - $x$ and $\sigma^2$ - are conceptually distinct. For instance, in the generalized version of this distribution, the normal-inverse-Wishart, $x$ becomes a vector and $\sigma^2$ becomes a matrix. As discussed in [Adding Normal-Inverse Wishart Distribution in scipy.stats module](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/scipy-dev/2020-May/024185.html), the question becomes whether we want to treat $x$ and $\sigma^2$ as separate inputs/outputs of `pdf`/`rvs` or whether we want to combine them. I've kept them separate, and normal broadcasting rules apply.

> Julia's [`ConjugatePriors.jl`](https://github.com/JuliaStats/ConjugatePriors.jl) package considers both `x` and `sig2` as random variates while R's [`PIGShift`](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/PIGShift/PIGShift.pdf) package considers only `x` as the random variate. Should SciPy follow the former or the latter convention?

The issues have requested the normal-inverse-gamma distribution, which is a bivariate distribution, so this PR follows the former convention. We could add methods that return related univariate distributions (e.g. for the marginal distributions).

> Would the implementation of the distribution be useful? The marginal distributions for `x` and `sig2` are already implemented in SciPy and it's easy to compute the full distribution using those.

It is relatively straightforward to generate random variates using `invgamma` and `norm`, but:
- users have requested this distribution anyway, and
- this distribution has other methods.

> The CDF on Wikipedia seems incorrect. Probably best to avoid implementing it before confirming the closed form is correct.

This PR does not implement the CDF.

*Example:*

<details>

```python3
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

mu, lmbda, a, b = 0, 1, 1, 1
x = np.linspace(-3, 3)
s2 = np.linspace(0, 4)[:, np.newaxis]

fig, axs = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9, 4), sharey=True)

for i, a in enumerate([1, 2, 4]):
    ax = axs[i]

    x_co = np.sqrt(lmbda / b) * (x - mu)
    y_co = s2 / b
    pdf = stats.normal_inverse_gamma.pdf(x, s2, mu, lmbda, a, b)

    ax.contour(x_co, y_co.ravel(), pdf, levels=np.logspace(-2, pdf.max(), 7))

    x_samp, s2_samp = stats.normal_inverse_gamma.rvs(mu, lmbda, a, b, size=50000)
    x_co = np.sqrt(lmbda / b) * (x_samp - mu)
    y_co = s2_samp / b

    ax.scatter(x_co, y_co, marker='.', alpha=0.01)
    ax.set_xlim((-3.1, 3.1))
    ax.set_ylim((0, 4))
    ax.set_xlabel(r'$\mu$')

axs[0].set_ylabel(r'$\sigma^2$')

plt.show()
```

</details>

improvement"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-08-14T19:44:09Z","updated_at":"2024-08-14T21:46:57Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/pulls/21389","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21389","diff_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21389.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/21389.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"#### Reference issue
Closes gh-6528, gh-13644 (duplicate)
Supersedes gh-6739, gh-18109
https://mail.python.org/pipermail/scipy-dev/2020-May/024185.html

#### What does this implement/fix?
This implements the normal-inverse-gamma distribution. (The multivariate generalization can be a separate PR, probably implemented in an unrelated class.)

There were two earlier PRs for this distribution:
- gh-6739 was closed because it became inactive. It was also not vectorized, and there may have been some license-related concerns.
- gh-18109 was closed because it did not implement the (bivariate) normal-inverse-gamma distribution. 

This PR addresses these concerns: it implements the normal-inverse-gamma distribution with arguments $x$ and $\sigma^2$ and shapes $\mu$, $\lambda$, $\alpha$, and $\beta$, all of which can be broadcastable arrays.

#### Additional information
To addressed the points from the [closing comment](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/18109#issuecomment-1458993415) of gh-18109:

> It looks like the distribution is an oddball - it doesn't fit our univariate distribution infrastructure or the multivariate distribution infrastructure. 

There is essentially zero multivariate distribution infrastructure, so there are no conflicts with the infrastructure. This is the first of the multivariate distributions that is not arbitrary-dimensional, but that does not really present any complications. The more unusual thing is that the two dimensions - $x$ and $\sigma^2$ - are conceptually distinct. For instance, in the generalized version of this distribution, the normal-inverse-Wishart, $x$ becomes a vector and $\sigma^2$ becomes a matrix. As discussed in [Adding Normal-Inverse Wishart Distribution in scipy.stats module](https://mail.python.org/pipermail/scipy-dev/2020-May/024185.html), the question becomes whether we want to treat $x$ and $\sigma^2$ as separate inputs/outputs of `pdf`/`rvs` or whether we want to combine them. I've kept them separate, and normal broadcasting rules apply.

> Julia's [`ConjugatePriors.jl`](https://github.com/JuliaStats/ConjugatePriors.jl) package considers both `x` and `sig2` as random variates while R's [`PIGShift`](https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/PIGShift/PIGShift.pdf) package considers only `x` as the random variate. Should SciPy follow the former or the latter convention?

The issues have requested the normal-inverse-gamma distribution, which is a bivariate distribution, so this PR follows the former convention. We could add methods that return related univariate distributions (e.g. for the marginal distributions).

> Would the implementation of the distribution be useful? The marginal distributions for `x` and `sig2` are already implemented in SciPy and it's easy to compute the full distribution using those.

It is relatively straightforward to generate random variates using `invgamma` and `norm`, but:
- users have requested this distribution anyway, and
- this distribution has other methods.

> The CDF on Wikipedia seems incorrect. Probably best to avoid implementing it before confirming the closed form is correct.

This PR does not implement the CDF.

*Example:*

<details>

```python3
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

mu, lmbda, a, b = 0, 1, 1, 1
x = np.linspace(-3, 3)
s2 = np.linspace(0, 4)[:, np.newaxis]

fig, axs = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9, 4), sharey=True)

for i, a in enumerate([1, 2, 4]):
    ax = axs[i]

    x_co = np.sqrt(lmbda / b) * (x - mu)
    y_co = s2 / b
    pdf = stats.normal_inverse_gamma.pdf(x, s2, mu, lmbda, a, b)

    ax.contour(x_co, y_co.ravel(), pdf, levels=np.logspace(-2, pdf.max(), 7))

    x_samp, s2_samp = stats.normal_inverse_gamma.rvs(mu, lmbda, a, b, size=50000)
    x_co = np.sqrt(lmbda / b) * (x_samp - mu)
    y_co = s2_samp / b

    ax.scatter(x_co, y_co, marker='.', alpha=0.01)
    ax.set_xlim((-3.1, 3.1))
    ax.set_ylim((0, 4))
    ax.set_xlabel(r'$\mu$')

axs[0].set_ylabel(r'$\sigma^2$')

plt.show()
```

</details>

This is an enhancement adding a `coeffs` property to polynomials, as discussed in https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26401.
The reason is that a lot of people get confused by `P.coef`, because this is not the coefficients they are looking for. This new property will offer them an easy access to `P.convert().coef`, i.e. what they are actually looking for, and explain the difference with `P.coef`.
Best regards,
Olivier

proposal"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":44,"created_at":"2024-02-12T13:49:51Z","updated_at":"2024-08-21T15:21:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"`linalg.kron` is a basic, data-movement intensive array operation. There is hardly any computation done other than scalar times array and quite some shuffling needed for higher order nd arrays. It is similar to a very special case of `np.einsum` in higher dimensions. Hence it doesn't make too much sense for us to carry around a suboptimal version of it since Numpy already supports batch array input https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.kron.html

For comparison we also have Khatri-Rao product which is a Kronecker-like product and it is specific enough for us to have it but kron is not in my opinion.

See #20072 and https://github.com/pymc-devs/pytensor/issues/640 for more context.

Describe your issue.

I have a time series of non-negative data values (which may include zero values).  When I use interp1d with kind='linear', and pass a time value exactly equal to an input time where the input value is zero, I expect it to return a value of zero, but the result is a small negative value.  This causes issues later on, when I pass the interpolated data to a routine which requires non-negative inputs.  In the example below, changing the values_input data type from np.float32 to np.float64 gives the expected output of 0.0.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
import scipy
import numpy as np
time_strings_input = np.array(['2018-07-01T13:02:16.892474880',
                               '2018-07-01T13:02:16.922475008',
                               '2018-07-01T13:02:16.952474880'])
values_input = np.array([0.028584518, 0., 0.013626526],dtype=np.float32)

input_times_npdt64 = np.array([np.datetime64(t) for t in time_strings_input])
interp_to_times_npdt64 = np.array(input_times_npdt64[1])

input_times_float64 = input_times_npdt64.astype(np.float64)
interp_to_time_float64 = interp_to_times_npdt64.astype(np.float64)

interpolator = scipy.interpolate.interp1d(input_times_float64, values_input, kind='linear')
result=interpolator(interp_to_time_float64)
print(result)
# Expected result: 0.0
# Actual result: -3.469446951953614e-18
```


### Error message

```shell
No error message
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27306","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27306/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27306/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27306/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27306","id":2493811740,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86UpIwc","number":27306,"title":"BUG: inverted sign in sin(arcsin())","user":{"login":"yonghakim","id":40263494,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQwMjYzNDk0","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/40263494?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/yonghakim","html_url":"https://github.com/yonghakim","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yonghakim/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yonghakim/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yonghakim/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yonghakim/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yonghakim/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yonghakim/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yonghakim/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yonghakim/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/yonghakim/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-08-29T08:40:28Z","updated_at":"2024-08-29T13:43:28Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

In sine of arcsin for complex number, the sign of imaginary part got inverted(see 32bit).
Is this overflow?

```python
import numpy as np
import jax
jax.config.update('jax_enable_x64', True)
import jax.numpy as jnp
import torch

num = 4+1E-8j

a = np.array(num, dtype=np.complex128)
b = np.array(num, dtype=np.complex64)
print(np.sin(np.arcsin(a)), np.sin(np.arcsin(b)))

a = jnp.array(num, dtype=jnp.complex128)
b = jnp.array(num, dtype=jnp.complex64)
print(jnp.sin(jnp.arcsin(a)), jnp.sin(jnp.arcsin(b)))

a = torch.tensor(num, dtype=torch.complex128)
b = torch.tensor(num, dtype=torch.complex64)
print(torch.sin(torch.arcsin(a)), torch.sin(torch.arcsin(b)))
```

Right ones are 32bits that has inverted imaginary sign.
```python
numpy:    (4.000000000000001+1.0000000033743736e-08j) (3.9999998-1.6929347e-07j)
Jax numpy: (4.000000000000001+1.0000000033743736e-08j) (3.9999995-1.6929346e-07j)
Torch:tensor(4.0000+1.0000e-08j, dtype=torch.complex128) tensor(4.0000+8.6029e-06j)
```

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
import jax
jax.config.update('jax_enable_x64', True)
import jax.numpy as jnp
import torch
num = 4+1E-8j
a = np.array(num, dtype=np.complex128)
b = np.array(num, dtype=np.complex64)
print(np.sin(np.arcsin(a)), np.sin(np.arcsin(b)))
a = jnp.array(num, dtype=jnp.complex128)
b = jnp.array(num, dtype=jnp.complex64)
print(jnp.sin(jnp.arcsin(a)), jnp.sin(jnp.arcsin(b)))
a = torch.tensor(num, dtype=torch.complex128)
b = torch.tensor(num, dtype=torch.complex64)
print(torch.sin(torch.arcsin(a)), torch.sin(torch.arcsin(b)))
```

The `.real` part in `complex128` arithmetic ends up `<np.pi/2` whereas in the `complex64` arithmetic, it ends up `>np.pi/2` because it exactly equals `np.float32(np.pi/2)` which is a little bit above `np.float64(np.pi/2)`. So not overflow, just the vicissitudes of rounding at these different precisions.

```python
>>> np.sin(np.nextafter(np.float32(np.pi/2), np.float32(0))+np.arcsin(b).imag*np.complex64(1j))
(3.9999995+2.924021e-07j)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2317700688/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/29315","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/29315/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/29315/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/29315/events","html_url":"https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/29315","id":2364488901,"node_id":"I_kwDOAAzd1s6M7zzF","number":29315,"title":"Using randomness","user":{"login":"thomasjpfan","id":5402633,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU0MDI2MzM=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5402633?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/thomasjpfan","html_url":"https://github.com/thomasjpfan","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thomasjpfan/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thomasjpfan/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thomasjpfan/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thomasjpfan/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thomasjpfan/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thomasjpfan/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thomasjpfan/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thomasjpfan/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/thomasjpfan/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":68750477,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2ODc1MDQ3Nw==","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/labels/API","name":"API","color":"bfd4f2","default":false,"description":null},{"id":1314220606,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMzE0MjIwNjA2","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/labels/Needs%20Decision","name":"Needs Decision","color":"ffd8c1","default":false,"description":"Requires decision"},{"id":1784583753,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxNzg0NTgzNzUz","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/labels/RFC","name":"RFC","color":"7fe299","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2024-06-20T13:42:07Z","updated_at":"2024-07-02T17:14:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"In SPEC7 https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180, has two goals:

1. Deprecate the use of `RandomState` and `np.random.seed`
2. Standardize the usage of `rng` for setting seeding.

For 1, according to [NEP19](https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0019-rng-policy.html#numpy-random), I do not think NumPy wants to deprecate `np.random.seed` because they see valid use cases.

For 2, the primary reason around using `rng` instead of `random_state` is that it is a "better name" for NumPy's [Random Generator](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/generator.html#random-generator). I am okay with keeping `random_state` and not have users go the pain of changing their code.

Currently, scikit-learn does not support generators because we tied it to https://github.com/scikit-learn/enhancement_proposals/pull/88. We wanted to use generators to cleanly switch to a different RNG behavior compared to RandomState. For me, I think they can be decoupled. If we tackle  https://github.com/scikit-learn/enhancement_proposals/pull/88, we can fix it for both RandomState and Generators.

@scikit-learn/core-devs What do you think of SPEC7's proposal?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/29315/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/29315/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/2203877889","html_url":"https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/29315#issuecomment-2203877889","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/29315","id":2203877889,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAAzd1s6DXIIB","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-02T17:14:24Z","updated_at":"2024-07-02T17:14:24Z","author_association":"NONE","body":"> For 1, according to [NEP19](https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0019-rng-policy.html#numpy-random), I do not think NumPy wants to deprecate `np.random.seed` because they see valid use cases.

To be quite clear, in NEP 19, I am very much staking the ground that `scikit-learn` is *not* one of the valid use cases for `np.random.seed()`. It is a blight upon you and your users (to be clear, one that *I* inflicted). As the NumPy project, per se, we are not going to remove `np.random.seed()`, but as the author of `np.random`, I vigorously encourage you to pretend that it does not exist.

> For 2, the primary reason around using `rng` instead of `random_state` is that it is a "better name" for NumPy's [Random Generator](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/generator.html#random-generator). I am okay with keeping `random_state` and not have users go the pain of changing their code.

SPEC7 does not recommend a name change just because we are dealing with `Generator` objects rather than `RandomState` objects. SPEC7 is recommending changing the *semantics* of what the default `random_state=None` does; therefore, one should probably go through a deprecation cycle with a name change to reflect the semantics change. SPEC7 recommends (in the eventual state) that one use `rng=None` keyword arguments, then doing `rng = np.random.default_rng(rng)` to coerce the argument to a `Generator`. `default_rng()` is analogous to `check_random_state()` *except*, crucially, that there is no default global `Generator` like there is a default global `RandomState`. `np.random.default_rng(None)` creates a completely new `Generator`. If there had been no semantics change, we would have recommended retaining the `random_state` name despite dropping `RandomState` instances.

The deprecation lifecycle proposed by SPEC7 has an intermediate state that is somewhat along the lines of "allow people to pass a `Generator` but don't forbid `RandomState` or break current code" but it does involve an `rng=` argument.

> I personally don't mind renaming `random_state` to `rng` IF it unlocks some backward compatibility concerns with _fixing_ `random_state`.

Yes, this is the case.

As for what features using `Generator`s (and `Generator`s alone) can buy you: https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/16988#issuecomment-1518037853","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/comments/2203877889/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

+---
+title: "SPEC 7 — Seeding pseudo-random number generation"
+date: 2023-04-19
+author:
+  - "Stéfan van der Walt <stefanv@berkeley.edu>"
+  - "Sebastian Berg <sebastianb@nvidia.com>"
+  - Other participants in the discussion <not.yet@named.org>"
+discussion: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/14322
+endorsed-by:
+---
+
+## Description
+
+<!--
+Briefly and clearly describe the proposal.
+Explain the general need and the advantages of this specific proposal.
+If relevant, include examples of how the new functionality would be used,
+intended use-cases, and pseudo-code illustrating its use.
+-->
+
+There is disparity in the APIs libraries provide to seed random number generation.
+This SPEC suggests a single, pragmatic API for the ecosystem, taking into account technical and historical factors.
+Adopting such a uniform API will simplify the user experience, especially for those who rely on multiple projects.
+
+Specifically, we recommend to:
+
+- Deprecate the use of `RandomState` and `np.random.seed`.
+- Standardize usage and interpretation of an `rng` keyword for seeding.
+
+### Concepts
+
+- `BitGenerator`: Generates a stream of pseudo-random bits. The default generator in NumPy (`np.random.default_rng`) uses PCG64.
+- `Generator`: Derives pseudo-random numbers from the bits produced by a `BitGenerator`.
+- `RandomState`: a [legacy object in NumPy](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/index.html), similar to `Generator`, that produces random numbers based on the Mersenne Twister.
+
+### Constraints
+
+NumPy, SciPy, scikit-learn, scikit-image, and NetworkX all implement pseudo-random seeding in slightly different ways.
+Common keyword arguments include `random_state` and `seed`.
+In practice, the seed is unfortunately also often controlled using `np.random.seed`.
+
+## Implementation
+
+<!--
+Discuss how this would be implemented.
+-->
+
+Legacy behavior in packages such as scikit-learn (`sklearn.utils.check_random_state`) typically handle `None` (use the global seed state), an int (convert to `RandomState`), or `RandomState` object.
+
+Two strong motivations for moving over to `Generator`s are:
+
+(1) they avoid naïve seeding strategies, such as using successive integers, via the underlying [SeedSequence](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/parallel.html#seedsequence-spawning);
+(2) they avoid using global state (from `np.random.mtrand._rand`).
+
+Our recommendation here is a deprecation strategy which does not in _all_ cases adhere to the Hinsen[^hinsen] principle.
+
+The [deprecation strategy](https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180#issuecomment-1515248009) is:
+
+1. Accept both `rng` and `random_state` keyword arguments.
+2. If `rng=None`, handle `random_state` as in legacy behavior (see above), except use a compatible Generator instead of RandomState.
+   A DeprecationWarning is raised to warn about a future change in behavior.
+3. After <X time>, use only `rng`, seeding with `default_rng(rng)`.
+   Raise an error if `random_state` is provided.
+4. At a time of the library's choosing, remove any machinery related to `random_state`.
+
+### Impact
+
+The following users will be affected:
+
+1. Those who use `np.random.seed`. The proposal will do away with that global seeding mechanism, meaning that code that relies on it will, after a certain deprecation period, start seeing a different stream of random numbers than before. To ensure that this does not go unnoticed, the library should raise a `FutureWarning` if `np.random.seed` was called earlier (we show how to do that further down).
+
+   Such code will, in effect, go from being seeded to being unseeded.
+   To avoid that from happening, the code will have to be modified to pass in explicitly an `rng` argument on each function call.
+
+2. Those who do not seed. Their code will, after the deprecation period, use the newly proposed default. Since they were already not requesting repeatable sequences, and since the underlying _distributions_ of pseudo-random numbers did not change, they should be unaffected.
+
+3. Users of `random_state=...`. Support for the `random_state` argument may be dropped eventually, but meanwhile we can provide clear guidance, via deprecation warnings and documentation, on how to migrate to the new `rng` keyword.
+
+[^hinsen]: The Hinsen principle states, loosely, that code should, whether executed now or in the future, return the same result, or raise an error.
+
+### Code
+
+As an example, consider how SciPy would transition from the `seed` to the `rng` keyword using a decorator.
+This is implemented using:
+1. A `check_random_state` function which normalizes either old (`seed`) or new (`rng`) input to a `Generator` object.
+   If neither `seed` nor `rng` was passed but the user has previously called `np.random.seed()`
+   this function gives a `FutureWarning` because the behavior will change as noted in
+   the Impact section point 1.
+2. A decorator to deal with the `seed` to `rng` keyword rename.  In future versions, this will deprecate the `seed` keyword. Meanwhile, it ensures that the documentation and auto-completion only advertises the new parameter name.
+   Delaying the deprecation ensures that downstream users can switch to `rng=` on all supported SciPy versions when the deprecation happens.
+
+```python
+_NoValue = object()  # singleton to indicate not explicitly passed
+
+
+def check_random_state(seed=_NoValue, rng=_NoValue):
+    if rng is not _NoValue and seed is not _NoValue:
+        raise TypeError("cannot pass both `rng=` and `random_state=` at the same time.")
+    if rng is not _NoValue:
+        return np.random.default_rng(rng)
+
+    if seed is _NoValue:
+        # If the user passed nothing, we have to reach into NumPy here:
+        # 1. If np.random.seed(None) was called (or never called), then we can
+        #    just use the default_rng (the result is random anyway).
+        # 2. If it was called, we must return the global random state object
+        #    and warn about future ignoring of seed!
+        if np.random.mtrand._rand._bit_generator._seed_seq is not None:
+            # The user did not seed, so no need to warn.
+            return np.random.default_rng()
+        warnings.warn(
+            "The NumPy global rng was seeded in call to np.random.seed() "
+            "in the future this function will ignore this seed and return "
+            "random values as if a new `np.random.default_rng()` was created.",
+            FutureWarning, stacklevel=5)
+        return np.random.mtrand._rand
+    if seed is None or seed is np.random:
+        return np.random.mtrand._rand
+    if isinstance(seed, (numbers.Integral, np.integer)):
+        return np.random.RandomState(seed)
+    if isinstance(seed, (np.random.RandomState, np.random.Generator)):
+        return seed
+
+    raise ValueError(f"'{seed}' cannot be used to seed a numpy.random.RandomState"
+                     " instance")
+
+
+def _prepare_rng(old_name, dep_version=None):
+    new_name = "rng"
+
+    def decorator(fun):
+        @functools.wraps(fun)
+        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
+            if old_name in kwargs:
+                if dep_version:
+                    end_version = dep_version.split('.')
+                    end_version[1] = str(int(end_version[1]) + 2)
+                    end_version = '.'.join(end_version)
+                    message = (f"Use of keyword argument `{old_name}` is "
+                               f"deprecated and replaced by `{new_name}`.  "
+                               f"Support for `{old_name}` will be removed "
+                               f"in SciPy {end_version}.")
+                    warnings.warn(message, DeprecationWarning, stacklevel=2)
+                if new_name in kwargs:
+                    message = (f"{fun.__name__}() got multiple values for "
+                               f"argument now known as `{new_name}`")
+                    raise TypeError(message)
+
+            kwargs[new_name] = check_random_state(
+                kwargs.pop(old_name, _NoValue),
+                rng=kwargs.pop(new_name, _NoValue)
+            )
+            return fun(*args, **kwargs)
+        return wrapper
+    return decorator
+
+
+@_prepare_rng("random_state")
+def library_function(/, rng=None):
+    # The decorated library function takes an `rng` argument which is
+    # guaranteed to be a either a Generator or a RandomState.
+    # `random_state=` is supported input (the old can be customized).
+    assert isinstance(rng, (np.random.Generator, {"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/specs/pulls/comments/1663217413","pull_request_review_id":2154951724,"id":1663217413,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOE4f9cs5jIq8F","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,183 @@
+---
+title: "SPEC 7 — Seeding pseudo-random number generation"
+date: 2023-04-19
+author:
+  - "Stéfan van der Walt <stefanv@berkeley.edu>"
+  - "Sebastian Berg <sebastianb@nvidia.com>"
+  - Other participants in the discussion <not.yet@named.org>"
+discussion: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/14322
+endorsed-by:
+---
+
+## Description
+
+<!--
+Briefly and clearly describe the proposal.
+Explain the general need and the advantages of this specific proposal.
+If relevant, include examples of how the new functionality would be used,
+intended use-cases, and pseudo-code illustrating its use.
+-->
+
+There is disparity in the APIs libraries provide to seed random number generation.
+This SPEC suggests a single, pragmatic API for the ecosystem, taking into account technical and historical factors.
+Adopting such a uniform API will simplify the user experience, especially for those who rely on multiple projects.
+
+Specifically, we recommend to:
+
+- Deprecate the use of `RandomState` and `np.random.seed`.
+- Standardize usage and interpretation of an `rng` keyword for seeding.
+
+### Concepts
+
+- `BitGenerator`: Generates a stream of pseudo-random bits. The default generator in NumPy (`np.random.default_rng`) uses PCG64.
+- `Generator`: Derives pseudo-random numbers from the bits produced by a `BitGenerator`.
+- `RandomState`: a [legacy object in NumPy](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/index.html), similar to `Generator`, that produces random numbers based on the Mersenne Twister.
+
+### Constraints
+
+NumPy, SciPy, scikit-learn, scikit-image, and NetworkX all implement pseudo-random seeding in slightly different ways.
+Common keyword arguments include `random_state` and `seed`.
+In practice, the seed is unfortunately also often controlled using `np.random.seed`.
+
+## Implementation
+
+<!--
+Discuss how this would be implemented.
+-->
+
+Legacy behavior in packages such as scikit-learn (`sklearn.utils.check_random_state`) typically handle `None` (use the global seed state), an int (convert to `RandomState`), or `RandomState` object.
+
+Two strong motivations for moving over to `Generator`s are:
+
+(1) they avoid naïve seeding strategies, such as using successive integers, via the underlying [SeedSequence](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/parallel.html#seedsequence-spawning);
+(2) they avoid using global state (from `np.random.mtrand._rand`).
+
+Our recommendation here is a deprecation strategy which does not in _all_ cases adhere to the Hinsen[^hinsen] principle.
+
+The [deprecation strategy](https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180#issuecomment-1515248009) is:
+
+1. Accept both `rng` and `random_state` keyword arguments.
+2. If `rng=None`, handle `random_state` as in legacy behavior (see above), except use a compatible Generator instead of RandomState.
+   A DeprecationWarning is raised to warn about a future change in behavior.
+3. After <X time>, use only `rng`, seeding with `default_rng(rng)`.
+   Raise an error if `random_state` is provided.
+4. At a time of the library's choosing, remove any machinery related to `random_state`.
+
+### Impact
+
+The following users will be affected:
+
+1. Those who use `np.random.seed`. The proposal will do away with that global seeding mechanism, meaning that code that relies on it will, after a certain deprecation period, start seeing a different stream of random numbers than before. To ensure that this does not go unnoticed, the library should raise a `FutureWarning` if `np.random.seed` was called earlier (we show how to do that further down).
+
+   Such code will, in effect, go from being seeded to being unseeded.
+   To avoid that from happening, the code will have to be modified to pass in explicitly an `rng` argument on each function call.
+
+2. Those who do not seed. Their code will, after the deprecation period, use the newly proposed default. Since they were already not requesting repeatable sequences, and since the underlying _distributions_ of pseudo-random numbers did not change, they should be unaffected.
+
+3. Users of `random_state=...`. Support for the `random_state` argument may be dropped eventually, but meanwhile we can provide clear guidance, via deprecation warnings and documentation, on how to migrate to the new `rng` keyword.
+
+[^hinsen]: The Hinsen principle states, loosely, that code should, whether executed now or in the future, return the same result, or raise an error.
+
+### Code
+
+As an example, consider how SciPy would transition from the `seed` to the `rng` keyword using a decorator.
+This is implemented using:
+1. A `check_random_state` function which normalizes either old (`seed`) or new (`rng`) input to a `Generator` object.
+   If neither `seed` nor `rng` was passed but the user has previously called `np.random.seed()`
+   this function gives a `FutureWarning` because the behavior will change as noted in
+   the Impact section point 1.
+2. A decorator to deal with the `seed` to `rng` keyword rename.  In future versions, this will deprecate the `seed` keyword. Meanwhile, it ensures that the documentation and auto-completion only advertises the new parameter name.
+   Delaying the deprecation ensures that downstream users can switch to `rng=` on all supported SciPy versions when the deprecation happens.
+
+```python
+_NoValue = object()  # singleton to indicate not explicitly passed
+
+
+def check_random_state(seed=_NoValue, rng=_NoValue):
+    if rng is not _NoValue and seed is not _NoValue:
+        raise TypeError("cannot pass both `rng=` and `random_state=` at the same time.")
+    if rng is not _NoValue:
+        return np.random.default_rng(rng)
+
+    if seed is _NoValue:
+        # If the user passed nothing, we have to reach into NumPy here:
+        # 1. If np.random.seed(None) was called (or never called), then we can
+        #    just use the default_rng (the result is random anyway).
+        # 2. If it was called, we must return the global random state object
+        #    and warn about future ignoring of seed!
+        if np.random.mtrand._rand._bit_generator._seed_seq is not None:
+            # The user did not seed, so no need to warn.
+            return np.random.default_rng()
+        warnings.warn(
+            "The NumPy global rng was seeded in call to np.random.seed() "
+            "in the future this function will ignore this seed and return "
+            "random values as if a new `np.random.default_rng()` was created.",
+            FutureWarning, stacklevel=5)
+        return np.random.mtrand._rand
+    if seed is None or seed is np.random:
+        return np.random.mtrand._rand
+    if isinstance(seed, (numbers.Integral, np.integer)):
+        return np.random.RandomState(seed)
+    if isinstance(seed, (np.random.RandomState, np.random.Generator)):
+        return seed
+
+    raise ValueError(f"'{seed}' cannot be used to seed a numpy.random.RandomState"
+                     " instance")
+
+
+def _prepare_rng(old_name, dep_version=None):
+    new_name = "rng"
+
+    def decorator(fun):
+        @functools.wraps(fun)
+        def wrapper(*args, **kwargs):
+            if old_name in kwargs:
+                if dep_version:
+                    end_version = dep_version.split('.')
+                    end_version[1] = str(int(end_version[1]) + 2)
+                    end_version = '.'.join(end_version)
+                    message = (f"Use of keyword argument `{old_name}` is "
+                               f"deprecated and replaced by `{new_name}`.  "
+                               f"Support for `{old_name}` will be removed "
+                               f"in SciPy {end_version}.")
+                    warnings.warn(message, DeprecationWarning, stacklevel=2)
+                if new_name in kwargs:
+                    message = (f"{fun.__name__}() got multiple values for "
+                               f"argument now known as `{new_name}`")
+                    raise TypeError(message)
+
+            kwargs[new_name] = check_random_state(
+                kwargs.pop(old_name, _NoValue),
+                rng=kwargs.pop(new_name, _NoValue)
+            )
+            return fun(*args, **kwargs)
+        return wrapper
+    return decorator
+
+
+@_prepare_rng("random_state")
+def library_function(/, rng=None):
+    # The decorated library function takes an `rng` argument which is
+    # guaranteed to be a either a Generator or a RandomState.
+    # `random_state=` is supported input (the old can be customized).
+    assert isinstance(rng, (np.random.Generator, As I was proofing some other changes, I noticed that the Copyright line at the end of the published NEPS is hardcoded to 2017-2018.
![image](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/assets/6098961/8a115ada-a2ac-4b15-8e7c-a919eca21b89)

The main documentation more appropriately has a Copyright line that goes from 2008 to 2024.
![image](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/assets/6098961/29ef7e4c-94e6-4186-bfee-e2ba0bc70618)

This output is controlled by a copyright variable in the applicable conf.py file.

The NEPs' copyright variable in `doc/neps/conf.py1` is
`copyright = '2017-2018, NumPy Developers'`

I believe it should either be changed to be the same as the copyright variable in `doc/source/conf.py` which is:
`copyright = '2008-2024, NumPy Developers'`

OR, to simplify maintenance, it should be made dynamic using syntax something like:
```
import datetime
year = datetime.datetime.now().year
copyright = f'© 2008-{year}, NumPy Developers.'
```
(I haven't tested the above lines; they are adapted from ChatGPT code.)

I'd be happy to try making the dynamic date version of the change to the NEP conf.py since I'm working on those files now. It looks as if the appropriate start year for NEPs would be 2017. That's the current value and   appears to be the publication year of NEP 0.

There are lots of things that a copyright notice can accomplish. It is true that under the Berne Convention, no copyright notice is needed for our copyright *to exist* on the work in Berne Convention countries; that exists as soon as the work is created. But the presence of a correctly-formatted copyright notice does have a legal effect in US courts. It removes the ability of the defendant to claim in court "I didn't know that it was validly copyrighted". By the letter of the [US Code](https://www.copyright.gov/title17/92chap4.html#401), this only applies to correctly-formatted copyright notices with a date, not dateless ones.

And for the record, because this is a common point of confusion reading this statute in the context of open source software, "the date of first publication" in a continually updated work is the date of the last substantive modification that you want to claim copyright on, not the unchanging first year. Each time we make substantive modifications, it's like making a new edition of a textbook, not just a new printing of a text unchanged from a decade ago. You include the date of publication of the new edition.

Since this is about the NEPs (sub-)website and not the NEPs themselves, the `copyright = f'© 2017-{year}, NumPy Developers.'` is probably sufficient, IMO.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2206884781/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The representation of float is meant to be disambiguated in numpy 2.

While this is a tad annoying for codes assuming that float64 should be represented as natural numbers, I understand it is a design choice. However, the representation of floats returns a string of the kind "np.float64(...)", rather than just "float64(...)".

This creates a nasty side effect when one tries to evaluate number representations using function `eval`.

Below is a screen capture of an `ipython` console.
Note that the `numpy.zeros(3)` returns `array([0., 0., 0.])`, not `np.array([0., 0., 0.])`.

![image](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/assets/84327932/1a301cc3-2d07-4b7b-b2b6-9625c2d37100)


### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy

print(repr(numpy.zeros(3)))  # expected: "array([0., 0., 0.])" - OK

print(repr(numpy.cos(0.0)))
# expected: "float64(1.0)"
# actual: "np.float64(1.0)", with "np" hard-coded!

# Consequence:
eval(repr(numpy.zeros(3)), {"array": numpy.array})  # works

eval(repr(numpy.cos(0.0)), {"cos": numpy.cos})  # raises NameError: 'np' is not defined
```


### Error message:

_No response_

### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.12.4
Numpy 2.0.0

_No response_

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/specs/pulls/comments/1667125573","pull_request_review_id":2161241649,"id":1667125573,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOE4f9cs5jXlFF","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,183 @@
+---
+title: "SPEC 7 — Seeding pseudo-random number generation"
+date: 2023-04-19
+author:
+  - "Stéfan van der Walt <stefanv@berkeley.edu>"
+  - "Sebastian Berg <sebastianb@nvidia.com>"
+  - Other participants in the discussion <not.yet@named.org>"
+discussion: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/14322
+endorsed-by:
+---
+
+## Description
+
+<!--
+Briefly and clearly describe the proposal.
+Explain the general need and the advantages of this specific proposal.
+If relevant, include examples of how the new functionality would be used,
+intended use-cases, and pseudo-code illustrating its use.
+-->
+
+There is disparity in the APIs libraries provide to seed random number generation.
+This SPEC suggests a single, pragmatic API for the ecosystem, taking into account technical and historical factors.
+Adopting such a uniform API will simplify the user experience, especially for those who rely on multiple projects.
+
+Specifically, we recommend to:
+
+- Deprecate the use of `RandomState` and `np.random.seed`.
+- Standardize usage and interpretation of an `rng` keyword for seeding.
+
+### Concepts
+
+- `BitGenerator`: Generates a stream of pseudo-random bits. The default generator in NumPy (`np.random.default_rng`) uses PCG64.
+- `Generator`: Derives pseudo-random numbers from the bits produced by a `BitGenerator`.
+- `RandomState`: a [legacy object in NumPy](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/index.html), similar to `Generator`, that produces random numbers based on the Mersenne Twister.
+
+### Constraints
+
+NumPy, SciPy, scikit-learn, scikit-image, and NetworkX all implement pseudo-random seeding in slightly different ways.
+Common keyword arguments include `random_state` and `seed`.
+In practice, the seed is unfortunately also often controlled using `np.random.seed`.
+
+## Implementation
+
+<!--
+Discuss how this would be implemented.
+-->
+
+Legacy behavior in packages such as scikit-learn (`sklearn.utils.check_random_state`) typically handle `None` (use the global seed state), an int (convert to `RandomState`), or `RandomState` object.
+
+Two strong motivations for moving over to `Generator`s are:
+
+(1) they avoid naïve seeding strategies, such as using successive integers, via the underlying [SeedSequence](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/parallel.html#seedsequence-spawning);
+(2) they avoid using global state (from `np.random.mtrand._rand`).
+
+Our recommendation here is a deprecation strategy which does not in _all_ cases adhere to the Hinsen[^hinsen] principle.
+
+The [deprecation strategy](https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180#issuecomment-1515248009) is:
+
+1. Accept both `rng` and `random_state` keyword arguments.
+2. If `rng=None`, handle `random_state` as in legacy behavior (see above), except use a compatible Generator instead of RandomState.
+   A DeprecationWarning is raised to warn about a future change in behavior.
+3. After <X time>, use only `rng`, seeding with `default_rng(rng)`.
+   Raise an error if `random_state` is provided.
+4. At a time of the library's choosing, remove any machinery related to `random_state`.
+
+### Impact
+
+The following users will be affected:
+
+1. Those who use `np.random.seed`. The proposal will do away with that global seeding mechanism, meaning that code that relies on it will, after a certain deprecation period, start seeing a different stream of random numbers than before. To ensure that this does not go unnoticed, the library should raise a `FutureWarning` if `np.random.seed` was called earlier (we show how to do that further down).
+
+   Such code will, in effect, go from being seeded to being unseeded.
+   To avoid that from happening, the code will have to be modified to pass in explicitly an `rng` argument on each function call.
+
+2. Those who do not seed. Their code will, after the deprecation period, use the newly proposed default. Since they were already not requesting repeatable sequences, and since the underlying _distributions_ of pseudo-random numbers did not change, they should be unaffected.
+
+3. Users of `random_state=...`. Support for the `random_state` argument may be dropped eventually, but meanwhile we can provide clear guidance, via deprecation warnings and documentation, on how to migrate to the new `rng` keyword.
+
+[^hinsen]: The Hinsen principle states, loosely, that code should, whether executed now or in the future, return the same result, or raise an error.
+
+### Code
+
+As an example, consider how SciPy would transition from the `seed` to the `rng` keyword using a decorator.
+This is implemented using:
+1. A `check_random_state` function which normalizes either old (`seed`) or new (`rng`) input to a `Generator` object.
+   If neither `seed` nor `rng` was passed but the user has previously called `np.random.seed()`
+   this function gives a `FutureWarning` because the behavior will change as noted in
+   the Impact section point 1.
+2. A decorator to deal with the `seed` to `rng` keyword rename.  In future versions, this will deprecate the `seed` keyword. Meanwhile, it ensures that the documentation and auto-completion only advertises the new parameter name.
+   Delaying the deprecation ensures that downstream users can switch to `rng=` on all supported SciPy versions when the deprecation happens.
+
+```python
+_NoValue = object()  # singleton to indicate not explicitly passed
+
+
+def check_random_state(seed=_NoValue, rng=_NoValue):
+    if rng is not _NoValue and seed is not _NoValue:
+        raise TypeError("cannot pass both `rng=` and `random_state=` at the same time.")
+    if rng is not _NoValue:
+        return np.random.default_rng(rng)","path":"spec-0007/index.md","commit_id":"3fd8fc73c3e822fbb121523dce57a201d00db679","original_commit_id":"3fd8fc73c3e822fbb121523dce57a201d00db679","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"It was part of the discussion when we designed `np.random.default_rng()`. We wanted to have a replacement for third-party `check_random_state()` functions inside of `np.random` itself. SPEC7 is about coordinating the deprecation strategy for getting people to move from the pattern of `random_state = check_random_state(random_state)` to `rng = np.random.default_rng(rng)`, not really really re-envisioning much else. This pattern from `check_random_state()` (in it's various flavors) has been battle-tested since its original incarnation in `scikit-learn` for over a decade. Another possibility that was considered was having `default_rng()` only take seeds (or `None`) while also including a `check_rng(rng)` that only takes `Generator`s, `None`, and legacy `RandomState`s, but no one then had the appetite for multiple functions like this, given the success of `check_random_state()`. If you really want to relitigate the issue, the proper venue would be an enhancement proposal for numpy, not a SPEC.

In documentation, I do highly encourage emphasizing that we expect people to pass around `Generator`s primarily, but allowing for a seed (of various kinds) or legacy `RandomState`s are permitted for rarer cases. We don't always know what the entrypoint our user will use in an API. While creating a single `Generator` at the top of a program and passing it all the way through everything is frequently the right thing to do, especially in ML, you come across cases where you will need to rerun one thing (like a CV splitter) multiple times and have it do the same thing (possibly to multiple sequences) separate from the main PRNG stream. Allowing for straight static seeds helps with that (though I do admit there is little loss in requiring people to create the `Generator` from the seed separately).","created_at":"2024-07-05T20:39:34Z","updated_at":"2024-07-05T20:39:34Z","html_url":"https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180#discussion_r1667125573","pull_request_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/specs/pulls/180","author_association":"NONE","_links":{"self":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/specs/pulls/comments/1667125573"},"html":{"href":"https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180#discussion_r1667125573"},"pull_request":{"href":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/specs/pulls/180"}},"reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/specs/pulls/comments/1667125573/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"start_line":null,"original_start_line":null,"start_side":null,"line":100,"original_line":100,"side":"RIGHT","in_reply_to_id":1667086334,"original_position":100,"position":100,"subject_type":"line"},"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/specs/pulls/180","id":1320346063,"node_id":"PR_kwDOE4f9cs5OsuHP","html_url":"https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180","diff_url":"https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/specs/issues/180","number":180,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Add +---
+title: "SPEC 7 — Seeding pseudo-random number generation"
+date: 2023-04-19
+author:
+  - "Stéfan van der Walt <stefanv@berkeley.edu>"
+  - "Sebastian Berg <sebastianb@nvidia.com>"
+  - Other participants in the discussion <not.yet@named.org>"
+discussion: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/14322
+endorsed-by:
+---
+
+## Description
+
+<!--
+Briefly and clearly describe the proposal.
+Explain the general need and the advantages of this specific proposal.
+If relevant, include examples of how the new functionality would be used,
+intended use-cases, and pseudo-code illustrating its use.
+-->
+
+There is disparity in the APIs libraries provide to seed random number generation.
+This SPEC suggests a single, pragmatic API for the ecosystem, taking into account technical and historical factors.
+Adopting such a uniform API will simplify the user experience, especially for those who rely on multiple projects.
+
+Specifically, we recommend to:
+
+- Deprecate the use of `RandomState` and `np.random.seed`.
+- Standardize usage and interpretation of an `rng` keyword for seeding.
+
+### Concepts
+
+- `BitGenerator`: Generates a stream of pseudo-random bits. The default generator in NumPy (`np.random.default_rng`) uses PCG64.
+- `Generator`: Derives pseudo-random numbers from the bits produced by a `BitGenerator`.
+- `RandomState`: a [legacy object in NumPy](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/index.html), similar to `Generator`, that produces random numbers based on the Mersenne Twister.
+
+### Constraints
+
+NumPy, SciPy, scikit-learn, scikit-image, and NetworkX all implement pseudo-random seeding in slightly different ways.
+Common keyword arguments include `random_state` and `seed`.
+In practice, the seed is unfortunately also often controlled using `np.random.seed`.
+
+## Implementation
+
+<!--
+Discuss how this would be implemented.
+-->
+
+Legacy behavior in packages such as scikit-learn (`sklearn.utils.check_random_state`) typically handle `None` (use the global seed state), an int (convert to `RandomState`), or `RandomState` object.
+
+Two strong motivations for moving over to `Generator`s are:
+
+(1) they avoid naïve seeding strategies, such as using successive integers, via the underlying [SeedSequence](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/parallel.html#seedsequence-spawning);
+(2) they avoid using global state (from `np.random.mtrand._rand`).
+
+Our recommendation here is a deprecation strategy which does not in _all_ cases adhere to the Hinsen[^hinsen] principle.
+
+The [deprecation strategy](https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180#issuecomment-1515248009) is:
+
+1. Accept both `rng` and `random_state` keyword arguments.
+2. If `rng=None`, handle `random_state` as in legacy behavior (see above), except use a compatible Generator instead of RandomState.
+   A DeprecationWarning is raised to warn about a future change in behavior.
+3. After <X time>, use only `rng`, seeding with `default_rng(rng)`.
+   Raise an error if `random_state` is provided.
+4. At a time of the library's choosing, remove any machinery related to `random_state`.
+
+### Impact
+
+The following users will be affected:
+
+1. Those who use `np.random.seed`. The proposal will do away with that global seeding mechanism, meaning that code that relies on it will, after a certain deprecation period, start seeing a different stream of random numbers than before. To ensure that this does not go unnoticed, the library should raise a `FutureWarning` if `np.random.seed` was called earlier (we show how to do that further down).
+
+   Such code will, in effect, go from being seeded to being unseeded.
+   To avoid that from happening, the code will have to be modified to pass in explicitly an `rng` argument on each function call.
+
+2. Those who do not seed. Their code will, after the deprecation period, use the newly proposed default. Since they were already not requesting repeatable sequences, and since the underlying _distributions_ of pseudo-random numbers did not change, they should be unaffected.
+
+3. Users of `random_state=...`. Support for the `random_state` argument may be dropped eventually, but meanwhile we can provide clear guidance, via deprecation warnings and documentation, on how to migrate to the new `rng` keyword.
+
+[^hinsen]: The Hinsen principle states, loosely, that code should, whether executed now or in the future, return the same result, or raise an error.
+
+### Code

+---
+title: "SPEC 7 — Seeding pseudo-random number generation"
+date: 2023-04-19
+author:
+  - "Stéfan van der Walt <stefanv@berkeley.edu>"
+  - "Sebastian Berg <sebastianb@nvidia.com>"
+  - Other participants in the discussion <not.yet@named.org>"
+discussion: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/14322
+endorsed-by:
+---
+
+## Description
+
+<!--
+Briefly and clearly describe the proposal.
+Explain the general need and the advantages of this specific proposal.
+If relevant, include examples of how the new functionality would be used,
+intended use-cases, and pseudo-code illustrating its use.
+-->
+
+There is disparity in the APIs libraries provide to seed random number generation.
+This SPEC suggests a single, pragmatic API for the ecosystem, taking into account technical and historical factors.
+Adopting such a uniform API will simplify the user experience, especially for those who rely on multiple projects.
+
+Specifically, we recommend to:
+
+- Deprecate the use of `RandomState` and `np.random.seed`.
+- Standardize usage and interpretation of an `rng` keyword for seeding.
+
+### Concepts
+
+- `BitGenerator`: Generates a stream of pseudo-random bits. The default generator in NumPy (`np.random.default_rng`) uses PCG64.
+- `Generator`: Derives pseudo-random numbers from the bits produced by a `BitGenerator`.
+- `RandomState`: a [legacy object in NumPy](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/index.html), similar to `Generator`, that produces random numbers based on the Mersenne Twister.
+
+### Constraints
+
+NumPy, SciPy, scikit-learn, scikit-image, and NetworkX all implement pseudo-random seeding in slightly different ways.
+Common keyword arguments include `random_state` and `seed`.
+In practice, the seed is unfortunately also often controlled using `np.random.seed`.
+
+## Implementation
+
+<!--
+Discuss how this would be implemented.
+-->
+
+Legacy behavior in packages such as scikit-learn (`sklearn.utils.check_random_state`) typically handle `None` (use the global seed state), an int (convert to `RandomState`), or `RandomState` object.
+
+Two strong motivations for moving over to `Generator`s are:
+
+(1) they avoid naïve seeding strategies, such as using successive integers, via the underlying [SeedSequence](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/parallel.html#seedsequence-spawning);
+(2) they avoid using global state (from `np.random.mtrand._rand`).
+
+Our recommendation here is a deprecation strategy which does not in _all_ cases adhere to the Hinsen[^hinsen] principle.
+
+The [deprecation strategy](https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180#issuecomment-1515248009) is:
+
+1. Accept both `rng` and `random_state` keyword arguments.
+2. If `rng=None`, handle `random_state` as in legacy behavior (see above), except use a compatible Generator instead of RandomState.
+   A DeprecationWarning is raised to warn about a future change in behavior.
+3. After <X time>, use only `rng`, seeding with `default_rng(rng)`.
+   Raise an error if `random_state` is provided.
+4. At a time of the library's choosing, remove any machinery related to `random_state`.
+
+### Impact
+
+The following users will be affected:
+
+1. Those who use `np.random.seed`. The proposal will do away with that global seeding mechanism, meaning that code that relies on it will, after a certain deprecation period, start seeing a different stream of random numbers than before. To ensure that this does not go unnoticed, the library should raise a `FutureWarning` if `np.random.seed` was called earlier (we show how to do that further down).
+
+   Such code will, in effect, go from being seeded to being unseeded.
+   To avoid that from happening, the code will have to be modified to pass in explicitly an `rng` argument on each function call.
+
+2. Those who do not seed. Their code will, after the deprecation period, use the newly proposed default. Since they were already not requesting repeatable sequences, and since the underlying _distributions_ of pseudo-random numbers did not change, they should be unaffected.
+
+3. Users of `random_state=...`. Support for the `random_state` argument may be dropped eventually, but meanwhile we can provide clear guidance, via deprecation warnings and documentation, on how to migrate to the new `rng` keyword.
+
+[^hinsen]: The Hinsen principle states, loosely, that code should, whether executed now or in the future, return the same result, or raise an error.
+
+### Code
