{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/specs/pulls/comments/1667389979","pull_request_review_id":2161550917,"id":1667389979,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOE4f9cs5jYlob","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,183 @@
+---
+title: "SPEC 7 — Seeding pseudo-random number generation"
+date: 2023-04-19
+author:
+  - "Stéfan van der Walt <stefanv@berkeley.edu>"
+  - "Sebastian Berg <sebastianb@nvidia.com>"
+  - Other participants in the discussion <not.yet@named.org>"
+discussion: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/14322
+endorsed-by:
+---
+
+## Description
+
+<!--
+Briefly and clearly describe the proposal.
+Explain the general need and the advantages of this specific proposal.
+If relevant, include examples of how the new functionality would be used,
+intended use-cases, and pseudo-code illustrating its use.
+-->
+
+There is disparity in the APIs libraries provide to seed random number generation.
+This SPEC suggests a single, pragmatic API for the ecosystem, taking into account technical and historical factors.
+Adopting such a uniform API will simplify the user experience, especially for those who rely on multiple projects.
+
+Specifically, we recommend to:
+
+- Deprecate the use of `RandomState` and `np.random.seed`.
+- Standardize usage and interpretation of an `rng` keyword for seeding.
+
+### Concepts
+
+- `BitGenerator`: Generates a stream of pseudo-random bits. The default generator in NumPy (`np.random.default_rng`) uses PCG64.
+- `Generator`: Derives pseudo-random numbers from the bits produced by a `BitGenerator`.
+- `RandomState`: a [legacy object in NumPy](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/index.html), similar to `Generator`, that produces random numbers based on the Mersenne Twister.
+
+### Constraints
+
+NumPy, SciPy, scikit-learn, scikit-image, and NetworkX all implement pseudo-random seeding in slightly different ways.
+Common keyword arguments include `random_state` and `seed`.
+In practice, the seed is unfortunately also often controlled using `np.random.seed`.
+
+## Implementation
+
+<!--
+Discuss how this would be implemented.
+-->
+
+Legacy behavior in packages such as scikit-learn (`sklearn.utils.check_random_state`) typically handle `None` (use the global seed state), an int (convert to `RandomState`), or `RandomState` object.
+
+Two strong motivations for moving over to `Generator`s are:
+
+(1) they avoid naïve seeding strategies, such as using successive integers, via the underlying [SeedSequence](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/parallel.html#seedsequence-spawning);
+(2) they avoid using global state (from `np.random.mtrand._rand`).
+
+Our recommendation here is a deprecation strategy which does not in _all_ cases adhere to the Hinsen[^hinsen] principle.
+
+The [deprecation strategy](https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180#issuecomment-1515248009) is:
+
+1. Accept both `rng` and `random_state` keyword arguments.
+2. If `rng=None`, handle `random_state` as in legacy behavior (see above), except use a compatible Generator instead of RandomState.
+   A DeprecationWarning is raised to warn about a future change in behavior.
+3. After <X time>, use only `rng`, seeding with `default_rng(rng)`.
+   Raise an error if `random_state` is provided.
+4. At a time of the library's choosing, remove any machinery related to `random_state`.
+
+### Impact
+
+The following users will be affected:
+
+1. Those who use `np.random.seed`. The proposal will do away with that global seeding mechanism, meaning that code that relies on it will, after a certain deprecation period, start seeing a different stream of random numbers than before. To ensure that this does not go unnoticed, the library should raise a `FutureWarning` if `np.random.seed` was called earlier (we show how to do that further down).
+
+   Such code will, in effect, go from being seeded to being unseeded.
+   To avoid that from happening, the code will have to be modified to pass in explicitly an `rng` argument on each function call.
+
+2. Those who do not seed. Their code will, after the deprecation period, use the newly proposed default. Since they were already not requesting repeatable sequences, and since the underlying _distributions_ of pseudo-random numbers did not change, they should be unaffected.
+
+3. Users of `random_state=...`. Support for the `random_state` argument may be dropped eventually, but meanwhile we can provide clear guidance, via deprecation warnings and documentation, on how to migrate to the new `rng` keyword.
+
+[^hinsen]: The Hinsen principle states, loosely, that code should, whether executed now or in the future, return the same result, or raise an error.
+
+### Code
+
+As an example, consider how SciPy would transition from the `seed` to the `rng` keyword using a decorator.
+This is implemented using:
+1. A `check_random_state` function which normalizes either old (`seed`) or new (`rng`) input to a `Generator` object.
+   If neither `seed` nor `rng` was passed but the user has previously called `np.random.seed()`
+   this function gives a `FutureWarning` because the behavior will change as noted in
+   the Impact section point 1.
+2. A decorator to deal with the `seed` to `rng` keyword rename.  In future versions, this will deprecate the `seed` keyword. Meanwhile, it ensures that the documentation and auto-completion only advertises the new parameter name.
+   Delaying the deprecation ensures that downstream users can switch to `rng=` on all supported SciPy versions when the deprecation happens.
+
+```python
+_NoValue = object()  # singleton to indicate not explicitly passed
+
+
+def check_random_state(seed=_NoValue, rng=_NoValue):
+    if rng is not _NoValue and seed is not _NoValue:
+        raise TypeError("cannot pass both `rng=` and `random_state=` at the same time.")
+    if rng is not _NoValue:
+        return np.random.default_rng(rng)","path":"spec-0007/index.md","commit_id":"3fd8fc73c3e822fbb121523dce57a201d00db679","original_commit_id":"3fd8fc73c3e822fbb121523dce57a201d00db679","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"I'm not saying there shouldn't be a discussion. +---
+title: "SPEC 7 — Seeding pseudo-random number generation"
+date: 2023-04-19
+author:
+  - "Stéfan van der Walt <stefanv@berkeley.edu>"
+  - "Sebastian Berg <sebastianb@nvidia.com>"
+  - Other participants in the discussion <not.yet@named.org>"
+discussion: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/14322
+endorsed-by:
+---
+
+## Description
+
+<!--
+Briefly and clearly describe the proposal.
+Explain the general need and the advantages of this specific proposal.
+If relevant, include examples of how the new functionality would be used,
+intended use-cases, and pseudo-code illustrating its use.
+-->
+
+There is disparity in the APIs libraries provide to seed random number generation.
+This SPEC suggests a single, pragmatic API for the ecosystem, taking into account technical and historical factors.
+Adopting such a uniform API will simplify the user experience, especially for those who rely on multiple projects.
+
+Specifically, we recommend to:
+
+- Deprecate the use of `RandomState` and `np.random.seed`.
+- Standardize usage and interpretation of an `rng` keyword for seeding.
+
+### Concepts
+
+- `BitGenerator`: Generates a stream of pseudo-random bits. The default generator in NumPy (`np.random.default_rng`) uses PCG64.
+- `Generator`: Derives pseudo-random numbers from the bits produced by a `BitGenerator`.
+- `RandomState`: a [legacy object in NumPy](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/index.html), similar to `Generator`, that produces random numbers based on the Mersenne Twister.
+
+### Constraints
+
+NumPy, SciPy, scikit-learn, scikit-image, and NetworkX all implement pseudo-random seeding in slightly different ways.
+Common keyword arguments include `random_state` and `seed`.
+In practice, the seed is unfortunately also often controlled using `np.random.seed`.
+
+## Implementation
+
+<!--
+Discuss how this would be implemented.
+-->
+
+Legacy behavior in packages such as scikit-learn (`sklearn.utils.check_random_state`) typically handle `None` (use the global seed state), an int (convert to `RandomState`), or `RandomState` object.
+
+Two strong motivations for moving over to `Generator`s are:
+
+(1) they avoid naïve seeding strategies, such as using successive integers, via the underlying [SeedSequence](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/parallel.html#seedsequence-spawning);
+(2) they avoid using global state (from `np.random.mtrand._rand`).
+
+Our recommendation here is a deprecation strategy which does not in _all_ cases adhere to the Hinsen[^hinsen] principle.
+
+The [deprecation strategy](https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180#issuecomment-1515248009) is:
+
+1. Accept both `rng` and `random_state` keyword arguments.
+2. If `rng=None`, handle `random_state` as in legacy behavior (see above), except use a compatible Generator instead of RandomState.
+   A DeprecationWarning is raised to warn about a future change in behavior.
+3. After <X time>, use only `rng`, seeding with `default_rng(rng)`.
+   Raise an error if `random_state` is provided.
+4. At a time of the library's choosing, remove any machinery related to `random_state`.
+
+### Impact
+
+The following users will be affected:
+
+1. Those who use `np.random.seed`. The proposal will do away with that global seeding mechanism, meaning that code that relies on it will, after a certain deprecation period, start seeing a different stream of random numbers than before. To ensure that this does not go unnoticed, the library should raise a `FutureWarning` if `np.random.seed` was called earlier (we show how to do that further down).
+
+   Such code will, in effect, go from being seeded to being unseeded.
+   To avoid that from happening, the code will have to be modified to pass in explicitly an `rng` argument on each function call.
+
+2. Those who do not seed. Their code will, after the deprecation period, use the newly proposed default. Since they were already not requesting repeatable sequences, and since the underlying _distributions_ of pseudo-random numbers did not change, they should be unaffected.
+
+3. Users of `random_state=...`. Support for the `random_state` argument may be dropped eventually, but meanwhile we can provide clear guidance, via deprecation warnings and documentation, on how to migrate to the new `rng` keyword.
+
+[^hinsen]: The Hinsen principle states, loosely, that code should, whether executed now or in the future, return the same result, or raise an error.
+
+### Code
+
+As an example, consider how SciPy would transition from the `seed` to the `rng` keyword using a decorator.
+This is implemented using:
+1. A `check_random_state` function which normalizes either old (`seed`) or new (`rng`) input to a `Generator` object.
+   If neither `seed` nor `rng` was passed but the user has previously called `np.random.seed()`
+   this function gives a `FutureWarning` because the behavior will change as noted in
+   the Impact section point 1.
+2. A decorator to deal with the `seed` to `rng` keyword rename.  In future versions, this will deprecate the `seed` keyword. Meanwhile, it ensures that the documentation and auto-completion only advertises the new parameter name.
+   Delaying the deprecation ensures that downstream users can switch to `rng=` on all supported SciPy versions when the deprecation happens.
+
+```python
+_NoValue = object()  # singleton to indicate not explicitly passed
+
+
+def check_random_state(seed=_NoValue, rng=_NoValue):
+    if rng is not _NoValue and seed is not _NoValue:
+        raise TypeError("cannot pass both `rng=` and `random_state=` at the same time.")
+    if rng is not _NoValue:
+        return np.random.default_rng(rng)","path":"spec-0007/index.md","commit_id":"3fd8fc73c3e822fbb121523dce57a201d00db679","original_commit_id":"3fd8fc73c3e822fbb121523dce57a201d00db679","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"My point is precisely that such a change *should* be a NEP and not a SPEC. Enhancement","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-07-05T16:21:27Z","updated_at":"2024-07-08T13:31:42Z","closed_at":"2024-07-08T13:31:42Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

The representation of float is meant to be disambiguated in numpy 2.

While this is a tad annoying for codes assuming that float64 should be represented as natural numbers, I understand it is a design choice. However, the representation of floats returns a string of the kind "np.float64(...)", rather than just "float64(...)".

This creates a nasty side effect when one tries to evaluate number representations using function `eval`.

Below is a screen capture of an `ipython` console.
Note that the `numpy.zeros(3)` returns `array([0., 0., 0.])`, not `np.array([0., 0., 0.])`.

![image](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/assets/84327932/1a301cc3-2d07-4b7b-b2b6-9625c2d37100)


### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy

print(repr(numpy.zeros(3)))  # expected: "array([0., 0., 0.])" - OK

print(repr(numpy.cos(0.0)))
# expected: "float64(1.0)"
# actual: "np.float64(1.0)", with "np" hard-coded!

# Consequence:
eval(repr(numpy.zeros(3)), {"array": numpy.array})  # works

eval(repr(numpy.cos(0.0)), {"cos": numpy.cos})  # raises NameError: 'np' is not defined
```


### Error message:

_No response_

### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python 3.12.4
Numpy 2.0.0

_No response_

Using `eval()` at all isn't the best practice, either. In any case, you can make a dummy object with `float64` and the others explicitly listed as attributes and plop it into the namespace a `np`, if you want. It doesn't really change the security picture, though, if I recall all the clever jailbreaks you can do with just a `type` object. Again, we do not care much about roundtrippability through `eval()` per se.
2. That alias sure is convenient, though. Whether it should or shouldn't be in the repr is purely a matter of taste; there is no rule or guideline that says anything about it. As discussed in [NEP 51](https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0051-scalar-representation.html#alternatives), the kind of roundtrippability that we care (somewhat) about is users copy-pasting from the output into the input in an interactive prompt. And because we vigorously recommend that users `import numpy as np`, that means `np.`.
3. Yeah, it's inconsistent, and I pointed out why. As for what `type()` does, that's `type.__repr__` working (though I suspect you might be in IPython, in which case that gets overridden by IPython's pretty-printer), which we don't control (or care about being consistent with).

All this was litigated during the [NEP 51](https://numpy.org/neps/nep-0051-scalar-representation.html#alternatives) discussion. Since I don't think we're going to reopen that, so I'm going to go ahead and close this.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2214088068/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Describe your issue.

The new re-implementation of `nnls` in [SciPy 1.12](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/release/1.12.0-notes.html), which replaced the solid-rock classic implementation by [Lawson & Hanson 1995 (L&H)](http://dx.doi.org/10.1137/1.9781611971217), is a major step back and not robust enough for real-works applications.

The previous L&H `nnls` implementation was designed to deal with degenerate Jacobians and rounding errors. It solved the linear sub-problem using a least-squares method which allowed for degenerated Jacobians. 

The current one simply ignores ill-conditioned matrices and solves the linear sub-problem assuming no degeneracy.

The previous L&H implementation was designed to avoid explicitly forming the `J.T @ J` square matrix, where `J` is the problem Jacobian, to avoid increasing the ill-conditioning of the problem. Instead, the current re-implementation forms the `J.T @ J` matrix and uses that in the linear solution.

Overall, the current implementation is a major step back and not robust enough for real-world applications. It breaks (fails to converge) a number of my large applications that worked well with the L&H Scipy version.

A much more robust implementation of this same problem is provided by the SciPy [bvls](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/v1.14.0/scipy/optimize/_lsq/bvls.py) procedure, which is a more general version of `nnls`. The Scipy `bvls` already implemented (without credit) the equivalent for `bvls` problems of the so-called `fast nnls` modification. It could be stripped down to solve `nnls` problems. Or the L&H procedure could be retained.

* I can understand why you think "_these obfuscate the code rather than improve it_" and I don't disagree with you, but that's just an opinion based on your programming habits and exposure to Python code.

FWIW, list comprehensions are a commonly used example in the excellent [_The Programmer's Brain_](https://www.manning.com/books/the-programmers-brain) of a confusing construct. Generally speaking, it can sometimes aid readability in the simpler cases where you can transform a 4-line snippet with lots of repetition into a nice DRY one-liner. In Hermans' terms, this can reduce the _diffuseness_ of the code.

But in the more complicated cases, like many of these in the PR, especially those with `if` clauses, we're not really saving on the linecount, but maybe only winning a bit in the DRYness. Comprehensions are kind of backwards things. Our eyes first see the expression of the item being added to the container *before the variables are even defined*. Then we get the `for` loop that defines the variables and where they come from. *Then* we get the `if` clause that recontextualizes the whole purpose of the thing. In the `for` loop version, we get each of those things in the order our brain needs them.

As a rule, I'm probably going to use a comprehension if it gets me to a one-liner, but probably opt for a `for` loop if I'm going to end up splitting up the comprehension over the same number of lines anyways. I am quite happy to keep `PERF401` and `PERF403` turned off in our configuration and let authors decide what to use.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2223009885/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

taken, and no I don't have any specific reference for you about that particular point. Most eye-tracking studies can't resolve movements that small (but I will note that natural language is not an ideal comparison; we have specialized wetware for our native languages, natural language sentences are generally shorter, and context often overdetermines which way a sentence is going to fall out in any case). There are a number of studies ranking the general readability/understandability of Python list comprehensions lower than the corresponding `for` loop, though. Google Scholar finds them readily.\r\n\r\n`for` loops are also Python. No one is saying that comprehensions are forbidden. Generally, I'd be happy to accept either version of each of these in newly-authored code (but also resist changing from one to the other without specific reasons for each one). ### Proposed new feature or change:

As briefly discussed at SciPy2024 (with @ngoldbaum), forward compatible version of functions that take a "size" argument vs a "shape" would be welcome. Opening this here to ask for opinions before starting a PR.

# Idea

Some functions take a `shape` argument (`np.zeros`, as in the array API), others take a `size` (as np.random.... does), describing in their docs as `shape`. Other libraries like TensorFlow ([random.normal](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/random/uniform) take consistently a `shape` argument and the array API (afaik) is also tending towards this, albeit the randomness sector is hard to agree on (given the different generator designs) and no such formal decision has been made.
IMHO, `shape` will come and be consistent throughout in the array API and would anyway be the better (more consistent) choice.

# Proposal


Adding an additional `shape` keyword to functions that take a `size` as argument for forward compatibility.
If both are specified, the code errors. No other changes are intended internally (but open to if needed), it's meant to be a zero-cost PR.

## Goal

Provide a consistent naming as an input name for users. Getting this in ASAP means that less legacy code is being written, future upgrades will be easier and it's simply less burden to remember where to use `size` vs `shape` for users.


## No-goals

No deprecation or breaking changes are planned, no behavioral changes either. Simply use a consistent naming.

If there are special cases where `size` maybe fits better, this can be left untouched.

## Future

In the future, and with the API standard adapting this formally, `size` could be depricated, internals could be changed. But that's explicitly not part of the PR.

# Compatibility

This is meant as a forward compatibility feature and should not pose any problems. The only way this could backfire seems to be if another name would be adopted in the array API standard, this seems to me to unlikely to rule out. Even if it were, I would still argue that numpy should allow `shape`. But it seems like an unrealistic scenario.


# Actions

If people agree on this, I'll gladly open a PR and we'll move it there for the more detailed discussion.

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/specs/pulls/comments/1676415592","pull_request_review_id":2175653384,"id":1676415592,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOE4f9cs5j7BJo","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,183 @@
+---
+title: "SPEC 7 — Seeding pseudo-random number generation"
+date: 2023-04-19
+author:
+  - "Stéfan van der Walt <stefanv@berkeley.edu>"
+  - "Sebastian Berg <sebastianb@nvidia.com>"
+  - Other participants in the discussion <not.yet@named.org>"
+discussion: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/14322
+endorsed-by:
+---
+
+## Description
+
+<!--
+Briefly and clearly describe the proposal.
+Explain the general need and the advantages of this specific proposal.
+If relevant, include examples of how the new functionality would be used,
+intended use-cases, and pseudo-code illustrating its use.
+-->
+
+There is disparity in the APIs libraries provide to seed random number generation.
+This SPEC suggests a single, pragmatic API for the ecosystem, taking into account technical and historical factors.
+Adopting such a uniform API will simplify the user experience, especially for those who rely on multiple projects.
+
+Specifically, we recommend to:
+
+- Deprecate the use of `RandomState` and `np.random.seed`.
+- Standardize usage and interpretation of an `rng` keyword for seeding.
+
+### Concepts
+
+- `BitGenerator`: Generates a stream of pseudo-random bits. The default generator in NumPy (`np.random.default_rng`) uses PCG64.
+- `Generator`: Derives pseudo-random numbers from the bits produced by a `BitGenerator`.
+- `RandomState`: a [legacy object in NumPy](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/index.html), similar to `Generator`, that produces random numbers based on the Mersenne Twister.
+
+### Constraints
+
+NumPy, SciPy, scikit-learn, scikit-image, and NetworkX all implement pseudo-random seeding in slightly different ways.
+Common keyword arguments include `random_state` and `seed`.
+In practice, the seed is unfortunately also often controlled using `np.random.seed`.
+
+## Implementation
+
+<!--
+Discuss how this would be implemented.
+-->
+
+Legacy behavior in packages such as scikit-learn (`sklearn.utils.check_random_state`) typically handle `None` (use the global seed state), an int (convert to `RandomState`), or `RandomState` object.
+
+Two strong motivations for moving over to `Generator`s are:
+
+(1) they avoid naïve seeding strategies, such as using successive integers, via the underlying [SeedSequence](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/parallel.html#seedsequence-spawning);
+(2) they avoid using global state (from `np.random.mtrand._rand`).
+
+Our recommendation here is a deprecation strategy which does not in _all_ cases adhere to the Hinsen[^hinsen] principle.
+
+The [deprecation strategy](https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180#issuecomment-1515248009) is:
+
+1. Accept both `rng` and `random_state` keyword arguments.
+2. If `rng=None`, handle `random_state` as in legacy behavior (see above), except use a compatible Generator instead of RandomState.
+   A DeprecationWarning is raised to warn about a future change in behavior.
+3. After <X time>, use only `rng`, seeding with `default_rng(rng)`.
+   Raise an error if `random_state` is provided.
+4. At a time of the library's choosing, remove any machinery related to `random_state`.
+
+### Impact
+
+The following users will be affected:
+
+1. Those who use `np.random.seed`. The proposal will do away with that global seeding mechanism, meaning that code that relies on it will, after a certain deprecation period, start seeing a different stream of random numbers than before. To ensure that this does not go unnoticed, the library should raise a `FutureWarning` if `np.random.seed` was called earlier (we show how to do that further down).
+
+   Such code will, in effect, go from being seeded to being unseeded.
+   To avoid that from happening, the code will have to be modified to pass in explicitly an `rng` argument on each function call.
+
+2. Those who do not seed. Their code will, after the deprecation period, use the newly proposed default. Since they were already not requesting repeatable sequences, and since the underlying _distributions_ of pseudo-random numbers did not change, they should be unaffected.
+
+3. Users of `random_state=...`. Support for the `random_state` argument may be dropped eventually, but meanwhile we can provide clear guidance, via deprecation warnings and documentation, on how to migrate to the new `rng` keyword.
+
+[^hinsen]: The Hinsen principle states, loosely, that code should, whether executed now or in the future, return the same result, or raise an error.
+
+### Code
+
+As an example, consider how SciPy would transition from the `seed` to the `rng` keyword using a decorator.
+This is implemented using:
+1. A `check_random_state` function which normalizes either old (`seed`) or new (`rng`) input to a `Generator` object.
+   If neither `seed` nor `rng` was passed but the user has previously called `np.random.seed()`
+   this function gives a `FutureWarning` because the behavior will change as noted in
+   the Impact section point 1.
+2. A decorator to deal with the `seed` to `rng` keyword rename.  In future versions, this will deprecate the `seed` keyword. Meanwhile, it ensures that the documentation and auto-completion only advertises the new parameter name.
+   Delaying the deprecation ensures that downstream users can switch to `rng=` on all supported SciPy versions when the deprecation happens.
+
+```python
+_NoValue = object()  # singleton to indicate not explicitly passed
+
+
+def check_random_state(seed=_NoValue, rng=_NoValue):
+    if rng is not _NoValue and seed is not _NoValue:
+        raise TypeError("cannot pass both `rng=` and `random_state=` at the same time.")
+    if rng is not _NoValue:

+---
+title: "SPEC 7 — Seeding pseudo-random number generation"
+date: 2023-04-19
+author:
+  - "Stéfan van der Walt <stefanv@berkeley.edu>"
+  - "Sebastian Berg <sebastianb@nvidia.com>"
+  - Other participants in the discussion <not.yet@named.org>"
+discussion: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/14322
+endorsed-by:
+---
+
+## Description
+
+<!--
+Briefly and clearly describe the proposal.
+Explain the general need and the advantages of this specific proposal.
+If relevant, include examples of how the new functionality would be used,
+intended use-cases, and pseudo-code illustrating its use.
+-->
+
+There is disparity in the APIs libraries provide to seed random number generation.
+This SPEC suggests a single, pragmatic API for the ecosystem, taking into account technical and historical factors.
+Adopting such a uniform API will simplify the user experience, especially for those who rely on multiple projects.
+
+Specifically, we recommend to:
+
+- Deprecate the use of `RandomState` and `np.random.seed`.
+- Standardize usage and interpretation of an `rng` keyword for seeding.
+
+### Concepts
+
+- `BitGenerator`: Generates a stream of pseudo-random bits. The default generator in NumPy (`np.random.default_rng`) uses PCG64.
+- `Generator`: Derives pseudo-random numbers from the bits produced by a `BitGenerator`.
+- `RandomState`: a [legacy object in NumPy](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/index.html), similar to `Generator`, that produces random numbers based on the Mersenne Twister.
+
+### Constraints
+
+NumPy, SciPy, scikit-learn, scikit-image, and NetworkX all implement pseudo-random seeding in slightly different ways.
+Common keyword arguments include `random_state` and `seed`.
+In practice, the seed is unfortunately also often controlled using `np.random.seed`.
+
+## Implementation
+
+<!--
+Discuss how this would be implemented.
+-->
+
+Legacy behavior in packages such as scikit-learn (`sklearn.utils.check_random_state`) typically handle `None` (use the global seed state), an int (convert to `RandomState`), or `RandomState` object.
+
+Two strong motivations for moving over to `Generator`s are:
+
+(1) they avoid naïve seeding strategies, such as using successive integers, via the underlying [SeedSequence](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/parallel.html#seedsequence-spawning);
+(2) they avoid using global state (from `np.random.mtrand._rand`).
+
+Our recommendation here is a deprecation strategy which does not in _all_ cases adhere to the Hinsen[^hinsen] principle.
+
+The [deprecation strategy](https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180#issuecomment-1515248009) is:
+
+1. Accept both `rng` and `random_state` keyword arguments.
+2. If `rng=None`, handle `random_state` as in legacy behavior (see above), except use a compatible Generator instead of RandomState.
+   A DeprecationWarning is raised to warn about a future change in behavior.
+3. After <X time>, use only `rng`, seeding with `default_rng(rng)`.
+   Raise an error if `random_state` is provided.
+4. At a time of the library's choosing, remove any machinery related to `random_state`.
+
+### Impact
+
+The following users will be affected:
+
+1. Those who use `np.random.seed`. The proposal will do away with that global seeding mechanism, meaning that code that relies on it will, after a certain deprecation period, start seeing a different stream of random numbers than before. To ensure that this does not go unnoticed, the library should raise a `FutureWarning` if `np.random.seed` was called earlier (we show how to do that further down).
+
+   Such code will, in effect, go from being seeded to being unseeded.
+   To avoid that from happening, the code will have to be modified to pass in explicitly an `rng` argument on each function call.
+
+2. Those who do not seed. Their code will, after the deprecation period, use the newly proposed default. Since they were already not requesting repeatable sequences, and since the underlying _distributions_ of pseudo-random numbers did not change, they should be unaffected.
+
+3. Users of `random_state=...`. Support for the `random_state` argument may be dropped eventually, but meanwhile we can provide clear guidance, via deprecation warnings and documentation, on how to migrate to the new `rng` keyword.
+
+[^hinsen]: The Hinsen principle states, loosely, that code should, whether executed now or in the future, return the same result, or raise an error.
+
+### Code

```python
from collections.abc import Sequence
from typing import Optional

import numpy as np

# I don't know if an array of integers will typecheck as a `Sequence[int]`, but it's a rarer use case.
SeedLike = int | np.integer | Sequence[int] | np.random.SeedSequence
RNGLike = np.random.Generator | np.random.BitGenerator | np.random.RandomState
RNGOrSeed = RNGLike | SeedLike

def foo(rng: Optional[RNGOrSeed] = None):
    rng = np.random.default_rng(rng)
    ...

+---
+title: "SPEC 7 — Seeding pseudo-random number generation"
+date: 2023-04-19
+author:
+  - "Stéfan van der Walt <stefanv@berkeley.edu>"
+  - "Sebastian Berg <sebastianb@nvidia.com>"
+  - Other participants in the discussion <not.yet@named.org>"
+discussion: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/14322
+endorsed-by:
+---
+
+## Description
+
+<!--
+Briefly and clearly describe the proposal.
+Explain the general need and the advantages of this specific proposal.
+If relevant, include examples of how the new functionality would be used,
+intended use-cases, and pseudo-code illustrating its use.
+-->
+
+There is disparity in the APIs libraries provide to seed random number generation.
+This SPEC suggests a single, pragmatic API for the ecosystem, taking into account technical and historical factors.
+Adopting such a uniform API will simplify the user experience, especially for those who rely on multiple projects.
+
+Specifically, we recommend to:
+
+- Deprecate the use of `RandomState` and `np.random.seed`.
+- Standardize usage and interpretation of an `rng` keyword for seeding.
+
+### Concepts
+
+- `BitGenerator`: Generates a stream of pseudo-random bits. The default generator in NumPy (`np.random.default_rng`) uses PCG64.
+- `Generator`: Derives pseudo-random numbers from the bits produced by a `BitGenerator`.
+- `RandomState`: a [legacy object in NumPy](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/index.html), similar to `Generator`, that produces random numbers based on the Mersenne Twister.
+
+### Constraints
+
+NumPy, SciPy, scikit-learn, scikit-image, and NetworkX all implement pseudo-random seeding in slightly different ways.
+Common keyword arguments include `random_state` and `seed`.
+In practice, the seed is unfortunately also often controlled using `np.random.seed`.
+
+## Implementation
+
+<!--
+Discuss how this would be implemented.
+-->
+
+Legacy behavior in packages such as scikit-learn (`sklearn.utils.check_random_state`) typically handle `None` (use the global seed state), an int (convert to `RandomState`), or `RandomState` object.
+
+Two strong motivations for moving over to `Generator`s are:
+
+(1) they avoid naïve seeding strategies, such as using successive integers, via the underlying [SeedSequence](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/random/parallel.html#seedsequence-spawning);
+(2) they avoid using global state (from `np.random.mtrand._rand`).
+
+Our recommendation here is a deprecation strategy which does not in _all_ cases adhere to the Hinsen[^hinsen] principle.
+
+The [deprecation strategy](https://github.com/scientific-python/specs/pull/180#issuecomment-1515248009) is:
+
+1. Accept both `rng` and `random_state` keyword arguments.
+2. If `rng=None`, handle `random_state` as in legacy behavior (see above), except use a compatible Generator instead of RandomState.
+   A DeprecationWarning is raised to warn about a future change in behavior.
+3. After <X time>, use only `rng`, seeding with `default_rng(rng)`.
+   Raise an error if `random_state` is provided.
+4. At a time of the library's choosing, remove any machinery related to `random_state`.
+
+### Impact
+
+The following users will be affected:
+
+1. Those who use `np.random.seed`. The proposal will do away with that global seeding mechanism, meaning that code that relies on it will, after a certain deprecation period, start seeing a different stream of random numbers than before. To ensure that this does not go unnoticed, the library should raise a `FutureWarning` if `np.random.seed` was called earlier (we show how to do that further down).
+
+   Such code will, in effect, go from being seeded to being unseeded.
+   To avoid that from happening, the code will have to be modified to pass in explicitly an `rng` argument on each function call.
+
+2. Those who do not seed. Their code will, after the deprecation period, use the newly proposed default. Since they were already not requesting repeatable sequences, and since the underlying _distributions_ of pseudo-random numbers did not change, they should be unaffected.
+
+3. Users of `random_state=...`. Support for the `random_state` argument may be dropped eventually, but meanwhile we can provide clear guidance, via deprecation warnings and documentation, on how to migrate to the new `rng` keyword.
+
+[^hinsen]: The Hinsen principle states, loosely, that code should, whether executed now or in the future, return the same result, or raise an error.
+
+### Code

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26916","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26916/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26916/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26916/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26916","id":2404229004,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86PTZ-M","number":26916,"title":"ENH: Forward compatible "shape" argument vs "size"","user":{"login":"jonas-eschle","id":17454848,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3NDU0ODQ4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17454848?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle","html_url":"https://github.com/jonas-eschle","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2024-07-11T22:13:58Z","updated_at":"2024-07-13T23:26:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Willing to contribue this; discussion here before opening a PR**

# TL;DR

The numpy array generating API is currently inconsistent, some functions take `shape`, some `size` as arguments. This requires an unnecessary pain point for any non-power users, as they need to look up the argument everytime.
Not excluding any other changes, the proposed change would add the `shape` argument wherever a `size` argument is expected (backwards compatible) and _may_ does it for the two breaking cases (`gamma`, `standard_gamma`, which take it as the first argument), i.e. adding an alternative name.

The result is a more consistent API inside, less pain for users to think about how to name `shape` in arguments. 

The array API adopted `shape` for some generating arrays and nothing definit yet for others, however, it can be safely said that this will be the standard for all generating methods. _Even if not, in the unlikely case_, numpy should still offer a more consistent API to improve numpy itself.

### Proposed new feature or change:

As briefly discussed at SciPy2024 (with @ngoldbaum), forward compatible version of functions that take a "size" argument vs a "shape" would be welcome. Opening this here to ask for opinions before starting a PR.

# Idea

Some functions take a `shape` argument (`np.zeros`, as in the array API), others take a `size` (as np.random.... does), describing in their docs as `shape`. Other libraries like TensorFlow ([random.normal](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/random/uniform) take consistently a `shape` argument and the array API (afaik) is also tending towards this, albeit the randomness sector is hard to agree on (given the different generator designs) and no such formal decision has been made.
IMHO, `shape` will come and be consistent throughout in the array API and would anyway be the better (more consistent) choice.

# Proposal


Adding an additional `shape` keyword to functions that take a `size` as argument for forward compatibility.
If both are specified, the code errors. No other changes are intended internally (but open to if needed), it's meant to be a zero-cost PR.

## Goal

Provide a consistent naming as an input name for users. Getting this in ASAP means that less legacy code is being written, future upgrades will be easier and it's simply less burden to remember where to use `size` vs `shape` for users.


## No-goals

No deprecation or breaking changes are planned, no behavioral changes either. Simply use a consistent naming.

If there are special cases where `size` maybe fits better, this can be left untouched.

## Future

In the future, and with the API standard adapting this formally, `size` could be depricated, internals could be changed. But that's explicitly not part of the PR.

# Compatibility

This is meant as a forward compatibility feature and should not pose any problems. The only way this could backfire seems to be if another name would be adopted in the array API standard, this seems to me to unlikely to rule out. Even if it were, I would still argue that numpy should allow `shape`. But it seems like an unrealistic scenario.


# Actions

If people agree on this, I'll gladly open a PR and we'll move it there for the more detailed discussion.

Do you remember the reason? From a recent chat I remember that it was rather historical without a fundamental reason.

To avoid confusion with the shape parameters of the various distributions. This was first done in `scipy.stats` back in the super-early days when it was running on Numeric. While mostly not explicitly conflicting with method arguments named `shape`, they do talk about all of the parameters that are not `loc` and `scale` as "shape parameters" (as is standard mathematical practice) and did not want the confusion with having `loc=0, scale=1, shape=None` where `shape` actually meant the shape of the desired array. When I wrote [`mtrand`](https://pypi.org/project/mtrand/), I followed suit so it could be a drop-in replacement for the PRNG code that was in there. Then numpy adopted `mtrand` as `numpy.random`. IIRC, this was while we were still considering it to be `scipy_core`, so `scipy.stats` having made that decision to be somewhat inconsistent with the rest, but a fairly self-contained and *self*-consistent inconsistency withing the one region made some sense.

[I am keenly, *keenly* aware of the tabs in `Makefile`s story.](https://beebo.org/haycorn/2015-04-20_tabs-and-makefiles.html)

> This is a large pain point for anyone not being super familiar with numpy: one always has to look up the docs.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26916","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26916/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26916/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26916/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26916","id":2404229004,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86PTZ-M","number":26916,"title":"ENH: Forward compatible "shape" argument vs "size"","user":{"login":"jonas-eschle","id":17454848,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3NDU0ODQ4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17454848?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle","html_url":"https://github.com/jonas-eschle","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jonas-eschle/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2024-07-11T22:13:58Z","updated_at":"2024-07-14T00:45:06Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"**Willing to contribue this; discussion here before opening a PR**

# TL;DR

The numpy array generating API is currently inconsistent, some functions take `shape`, some `size` as arguments. This requires an unnecessary pain point for any non-power users, as they need to look up the argument everytime.
Not excluding any other changes, the proposed change would add the `shape` argument wherever a `size` argument is expected (backwards compatible) and _may_ does it for the two breaking cases (`gamma`, `standard_gamma`, which take it as the first argument), i.e. adding an alternative name.

The result is a more consistent API inside, less pain for users to think about how to name `shape` in arguments. 

The array API adopted `shape` for some generating arrays and nothing definit yet for others, however, it can be safely said that this will be the standard for all generating methods. _Even if not, in the unlikely case_, numpy should still offer a more consistent API to improve numpy itself.

### Proposed new feature or change:

As briefly discussed at SciPy2024 (with @ngoldbaum), forward compatible version of functions that take a "size" argument vs a "shape" would be welcome. Opening this here to ask for opinions before starting a PR.

# Idea

Some functions take a `shape` argument (`np.zeros`, as in the array API), others take a `size` (as np.random.... does), describing in their docs as `shape`. Other libraries like TensorFlow ([random.normal](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/random/uniform) take consistently a `shape` argument and the array API (afaik) is also tending towards this, albeit the randomness sector is hard to agree on (given the different generator designs) and no such formal decision has been made.
IMHO, `shape` will come and be consistent throughout in the array API and would anyway be the better (more consistent) choice.

# Proposal


Adding an additional `shape` keyword to functions that take a `size` as argument for forward compatibility.
If both are specified, the code errors. No other changes are intended internally (but open to if needed), it's meant to be a zero-cost PR.

## Goal

Provide a consistent naming as an input name for users. Getting this in ASAP means that less legacy code is being written, future upgrades will be easier and it's simply less burden to remember where to use `size` vs `shape` for users.


## No-goals

No deprecation or breaking changes are planned, no behavioral changes either. Simply use a consistent naming.

If there are special cases where `size` maybe fits better, this can be left untouched.

## Future

In the future, and with the API standard adapting this formally, `size` could be depricated, internals could be changed. But that's explicitly not part of the PR.

# Compatibility

This is meant as a forward compatibility feature and should not pose any problems. The only way this could backfire seems to be if another name would be adopted in the array API standard, this seems to me to unlikely to rule out. Even if it were, I would still argue that numpy should allow `shape`. But it seems like an unrealistic scenario.


# Actions

If people agree on this, I'll gladly open a PR and we'll move it there for the more detailed discussion.

But there seems no actual clash to be happening now (which is easy in hindsight of course, but it's what we've learned over time) and we can make the naming more consistent. Or would you disagree with this?

It was not about clashing with another parameter named exactly `shape`, per se. It was about ensuring that people understood that that parameter was not a shape parameter of the distribution.

I am not going to argue that it was an ideal choice; I was not the one who made it. But I will assure you that the inconsistency was done deliberately, knowing full well how `shape` was used elsewhere (said person being the main author of `numpy` as well as the distributions in `scipy.stats`).

> In my experience, any numpy user that I ask (and isn't a super power user) "how do you create an array in numpy, with `shape` or `size`" (I could count dozens of examples with zero exceptions, just being at SciPy conf) will not have an answer and be confused, confessing that he/she has to look it up every time and always forget.

I have my suspicions that `np.random` is not causally related to this. It's a pretty confusing question in its own right given that most places where `shape` is used "to create an array in `numpy`" (`ones()`, `zeros()`, `full()`) usually get that argument passed positionally. If you want to run this experiment for real, have people write code (in an environment with controlled access to docs) where they'd pass said argument as a keyword (the `np.random` methods, or things like `fromstring()`, I guess). I'm willing to be most of the errors will be people using `shape=` for `np.random` methods rather than a general overall confusion.

> I think the _pain_ of this is probably only realized once one uses a library that is consistent (i.e. TensorFlow) and then comes back to numpy.

To be honest, I will stack up the pains I feel every time I use a creaking, old API I designed a couple of decades ago against anyone.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2227152164/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Question","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"Question about NumPy usage or development"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-07-17T23:04:38Z","updated_at":"2024-07-18T02:49:58Z","closed_at":"2024-07-18T02:49:58Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

The numpy.radians function will return different results if it is called while indexing the input array and the array has only integer elements. When acting on an indexed array the radians function returns an integer array, whilst when acting on the same array without indexing it returns a floating point array.
This bug was found using linux for Python 3.11.5 with numpy 1.26.3.


### Reproduce the code example:

```python
# Python 3.11.5 (main, Sep 11 2023, 13:54:46) [GCC 11.2.0] on linux
import numpy as np # np.__version__ = '1.26.3'

# Convert with indexing
v    = np.array([90, 180]) # Angles in degrees
v[:] = np.radians(v[:])    # Angles in radians
print(v)
# >>> [1 3]

# Convert without indexing
v = np.array([90, 180]) # Angles in degrees
v = np.radians(v)       # Angles in radians
print(v)
# >>> [1.57079633 3.14159265]
```


### Error message:

_No response_

### Python and NumPy Versions:

1.26.3
3.11.5 (main, Sep 11 2023, 13:54:46) [GCC 11.2.0]

### Runtime Environment:

_No response_

### Context for the issue:

When applying angle unit conversions on vectors in spherical coordinates, indexing the array is the most efficient way to write the transformation so as to leave the radial component undisturbed. Angles in radians returned as integer values are of no practical value.
I encountered this unexpected behaviour when developing code to model satellite links for use in an academic research environment. The documentation for numpy.random.Generator.uniform states that the output should be over the half-open interval [low, high). 
However, due to the floating point rounding, there are cases in which the right bound is included.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy.random
from math import ulp
while True:
    sample = numpy.random.uniform(1.0, 1.0+ulp(1.0))
    if sample > 1.0:
        break
print("Right bound = \t", 1+ulp(1.0))
print("Sample = \t",sample)
```

{"action":"closed","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26999","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26999/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26999/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26999/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26999","id":2420882886,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86QS73G","number":26999,"title":"ENH: Add numpy.random.disk() for generating uniform random points within a customizable disk or circular sector","user":{"login":"amirh0ss3in","id":59564278,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU5NTY0Mjc4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/59564278?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/amirh0ss3in","html_url":"https://github.com/amirh0ss3in","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amirh0ss3in/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amirh0ss3in/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amirh0ss3in/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amirh0ss3in/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amirh0ss3in/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amirh0ss3in/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amirh0ss3in/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amirh0ss3in/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/amirh0ss3in/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-07-20T12:40:18Z","updated_at":"2024-07-20T13:43:54Z","closed_at":"2024-07-20T13:43:53Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

I'd like to propose adding a new function `disk()` to `numpy.random` for generating random points uniformly distributed within a disk, circular sector, or annulus. This function would be useful for various applications in physics simulations, computer graphics, and statistical modeling.

Proposed function implementation:

```python
import numpy as np

def disk(size=None, angle_range=(0, 2*np.pi), radius=(0, 1)):
    """
    Draw samples from a uniform distribution over a disk or circular sector.
    
    Parameters
    ----------
    size : int or tuple of ints, optional
        Output shape. If the given shape is, e.g., (m, n, k), then
        m * n * k samples are drawn. Default is None, in which case a
        single value is returned.
    angle_range : float or tuple of floats, optional
        Range of angles (in radians) to draw from. If a single value is provided,
        it's interpreted as (0, angle_range). Default is (0, 2*pi).
    radius : float or tuple of floats, optional
        Range of radii to draw from. If a single value is provided,
        it's interpreted as (0, radius). Default is (0, 1).
    
    Returns
    -------
    out : ndarray
        Drawn samples from the parameterized disk distribution.
        The shape of the output is (size, 2) if size is an int or tuple,
        or (2,) if size is None.
    
    Examples
    --------
    >>> np.random.disk()
    array([-0.35795712, -0.10633531])
    
    >>> np.random.disk(size=3, angle_range=np.pi/2, radius=2)
    array([[0.91221391, 1.32474818],
           [1.82670081, 0.77325543],
           [0.04891289, 1.03447163]])
    
    >>> np.random.disk(size=3, angle_range=(np.pi/4, np.pi/2), radius=(0.5, 1))
    array([[0.58382925, 0.58235371],
           [0.49721824, 0.69101054],
           [0.65270298, 0.52740025]])
    """
    if size is None:
        size = 1
    else:
        try:
            np.empty(size)
        except (TypeError, ValueError):
            raise ValueError("size must be a non-negative integer or tuple of non-negative integers")
    
    # Handle angle_range parameter
    if np.isscalar(angle_range):
        angle_range = (0, angle_range)
    theta1, theta2 = angle_range
    
    # Handle radius parameter
    if np.isscalar(radius):
        radius = (0, radius)
    r1, r2 = radius
    
    # Generate random angles and radii
    phi = np.random.uniform(theta1, theta2, size)
    r = np.sqrt(np.random.uniform(r1**2, r2**2, size))
    
    # Convert to Cartesian coordinates
    x = r * np.cos(phi)
    y = r * np.sin(phi)
    
    if np.isscalar(size):
        return np.array([x[0], y[0]]) if size == 1 else np.column_stack((x, y))
    else:
        return np.column_stack((x.ravel(), y.ravel())).reshape(size + (2,))
```

This function complements existing distribution functions in `numpy.random` and provides a convenient way to generate points in a disk, circular sector, or annulus without requiring users to implement these common operations themselves.

Key features:
1. Customizable angle range for generating points in circular sectors
2. Customizable radius range for generating points in annuli or scaled disks
3. Consistent interface with other NumPy random functions (e.g., `size` parameter)
4. Uniform distribution achieved by taking the square root of the random radii

The implementation uses the following approach:
1. Generate uniform random angles (phi) between the specified angle range
2. Generate uniform random radii (r) between the specified radius range, taking the square root to ensure uniform distribution
3. Convert to Cartesian coordinates: x = r * cos(phi), y = r * sin(phi)

Here's a visualization of the function's output for a half-disk annulus:

![Visualization of np.random.disk output](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/bc2f6b90-52dd-421a-97d9-1b3456228b50)

This image was generated using the following code:

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

disk_distribution = disk(size=1000, angle_range=(0, 0.7 * np.pi), radius=(0.5, 1))

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6, 6))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, aspect='equal')
ax.scatter(disk_distribution[:, 0], disk_distribution[:, 1], s=5)
circle1 = plt.Circle((0, 0), 0.5, color='black', fill=False)
circle2 = plt.Circle((0, 0), 1, color='black', fill=False)
ax.add_artist(circle1)
ax.add_artist(circle2)
ax.set_xlim(-1, 1)
ax.set_ylim(-1, 1)
plt.show()
```

I'm willing to contribute the implementation if this proposal is accepted. Please let me know if any additional information or changes are needed.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26999/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26999/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"not_planned"}}

        return np.column_stack((x.ravel(), y.ravel())).reshape(size + (2,))
```

This function complements existing distribution functions in `numpy.random` and provides a convenient way to generate points in a disk, circular sector, or annulus without requiring users to implement these common operations themselves.

Key features:
1. Customizable angle range for generating points in circular sectors
2. Customizable radius range for generating points in annuli or scaled disks
3. Consistent interface with other NumPy random functions (e.g., `size` parameter)
4. Uniform distribution achieved by taking the square root of the random radii

The implementation uses the following approach:
1. Generate uniform random angles (phi) between the specified angle range
2. Generate uniform random radii (r) between the specified radius range, taking the square root to ensure uniform distribution
3. Convert to Cartesian coordinates: x = r * cos(phi), y = r * sin(phi)

Here's a visualization of the function's output for a half-disk annulus:

![Visualization of np.random.disk output](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/bc2f6b90-52dd-421a-97d9-1b3456228b50)

This image was generated using the following code:

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

disk_distribution = disk(size=1000, angle_range=(0, 0.7 * np.pi), radius=(0.5, 1))

fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6, 6))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, aspect='equal')
ax.scatter(disk_distribution[:, 0], disk_distribution[:, 1], s=5)
circle1 = plt.Circle((0, 0), 0.5, color='black', fill=False)
circle2 = plt.Circle((0, 0), 1, color='black', fill=False)
ax.add_artist(circle1)
ax.add_artist(circle2)
ax.set_xlim(-1, 1)
ax.set_ylim(-1, 1)
plt.show()
```

I'm willing to contribute the implementation if this proposal is accepted. Small QOL request. This was removed here https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/24144

I frequently use this pattern in a qtconsole:
```
%pylab qt

# ... do stuff

# look up source for something
source(np.angle)

# possibly open the shown path and poke around
```

This was handy to have available. The function is really simple, just printing source path and the source:
```python
import inspect
print("In file: %s\n" % inspect.getsourcefile(object), file=output)
print(inspect.getsource(object), file=output)
```

The release notes say 
> ``np.source`` has been removed. The preferred replacement is   ``inspect.getsource``.

There's a convenience factor in np.source printing both the path and source, and being available in the namespace. But fair enough if it stays out. Curently, it's allowed to pass some `typing.NamedTuple` instance to e.g. `numpy.empty` or `numpy.zeros`, but somewhere along the way, it gets converted to a vanilla `tuple`:

```pycon
>>> from typing import NamedTuple
>>> import numpy as np
>>> class Shape2D(NamedTuple):
...     height: int
...     width: int
... 
>>> x = np.zeros(Shape2D(width=22, height=7))
>>> type(x.shape)
<class 'tuple'>
```

I think it would be cool if you could have `ndarray.shape` be a named tuple. This way, you wouldn't have to remember which of the shape indices corresponds to the width or height, and use e.g. `x.shape.width` instead.

From a typing perspective, it also makes sense to have the `ShapeType` parameter of `ndarray[ShapeType: tuple[int, ...], DType]` match `ndarray.shape: ShapeType`, in case it isn't exactly a `tuple` but a subtype like `NamedTuple`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27086/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27086/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2260626339","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27086#issuecomment-2260626339","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27086","id":2260626339,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86Gvmuj","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-07-31T14:13:01Z","updated_at":"2024-07-31T14:13:01Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"No, Describe your issue.

Currently, `vonmises.stats()` is very slow and does not compute the circular variance. While there are different definitions of circular kurtosis from a quick literature scan, the circular variance is pretty much standardized. The formula for the circular variance of the von Mises distribution is available: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Von_Mises_distribution#Moments.

Fixing this would be easy to do (see below) but it breaks backwards compatability. Thinking of the general distribution infrastructure at the moment, I would like to gather feedback if this should be done now or later.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
from scipy.stats import vonmises, circvar, circmean
from scipy.special import i0e, i1e

loc, kappa = 3, 10

vonmises.stats(loc=loc, kappa=kappa, moments="mvs")  # takes 2 sec on my machine
# >>> (3.0, 0.10565504392764057, -1.75350482426206e-15)

samples = vonmises.rvs(loc=loc, kappa=kappa, size=100_000)
circmean(samples), circvar(samples)
# >>> (2.9999484119189654, 0.05140615221071776)

# using analytical formulas for the moments
var_analytic = 1 - i1e(kappa)/i0e(kappa)  # takes 1 mu sec
mean = loc
skew = 0  # von mises distribution is symmetric
mean, var_analytic, skew  
# >>> (3, 0.05140017404515407, 0)
```


### Error message

```shell
N/A
```


### SciPy/NumPy/Python version and system information

```shell
N/A
```

calling ``map_coordinates`` with a native/little endian memory-mapped array on Linux, things work fine and are fast:

```python
In [1]: import numpy as np
   ...: from scipy.ndimage import map_coordinates

In [2]: original = np.memmap('test.np', mode='w+', shape=(2000, 2000, 2000), dtype=float)
   ...: array = np.memmap('test.np', mode='r', shape=(2000, 2000, 2000), dtype='<f8')
   ...: coords = np.ones((3, 1))

In [3]: map_coordinates(array, coords, order=1, prefilter=False)
Out[3]: array([0.])
```

However, when passing a big endian memory-mapped array, it looks like a full copy of the array is made:

```python
In [4]: array = np.memmap('test.np', mode='r', shape=(2000, 2000, 2000), dtype='>f8')

In [5]: map_coordinates(array, coords, order=1, prefilter=False)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
MemoryError                               Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[5], line 1
----> 1 map_coordinates(array, coords, order=1, prefilter=False)

File ~/python/dev/lib/python3.11/site-packages/scipy/ndimage/_interpolation.py:472, in map_coordinates(input, coordinates, output, order, mode, cval, prefilter)
    470     filtered = input
    471 mode = _ni_support._extend_mode_to_code(mode)
--> 472 _nd_image.geometric_transform(filtered, None, coordinates, None, None,
    473                               output, order, mode, cval, npad, None, None)
    474 return output

MemoryError: Unable to allocate 59.6 GiB for an array with shape (2000, 2000, 2000) and data type float64
```

This seems like a bug to me but it might also be that it is a hard problem to fix and that it is deliberately not supported?

This is causing issues with large astronomical datasets since they are often stored in FITS which is big-endian.

improvement"},{"id":5432239077,"node_id":"LA_kwDOABZIoc8AAAABQ8lj5Q","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/labels/RFC","name":"RFC","color":"FEF2C0","default":false,"description":"Request
