{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26791","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26791/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26791/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26791/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26791","id":2370431094,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86NSeh2","number":26791,"title":"BUG?: precision issue using np.matmul on macOS 14 with Accelerate","user":{"login":"jorisvandenbossche","id":1020496,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwMjA0OTY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1020496?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorisvandenbossche","html_url":"https://github.com/jorisvandenbossche","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorisvandenbossche/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorisvandenbossche/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorisvandenbossche/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorisvandenbossche/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorisvandenbossche/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorisvandenbossche/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorisvandenbossche/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorisvandenbossche/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jorisvandenbossche/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":10,"created_at":"2024-06-24T14:39:58Z","updated_at":"2024-06-26T02:32:40Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

I don't know if this is a bug (and if it would be, also probably in Accelerate and not NumPy) or rather a wrong expectation about floating point issues in matrix multiplication. 
But, for the downstream issue in shapely (https://github.com/shapely/shapely/issues/2066) this behaviour change _is_ a problem (the array represents coordinates of a ring of a Polygon, and the first and last coordinate pair (first and last row in the array) always have to be exactly equal).

The issue is that we are transformating an array of coordinates using `np.matmul`, where the first and last coordinates are equal. After transforming them, the new first and last coordinates are still equal in general, except that this started to no longer be the case with NumPy 2.0 on MacOS 14 (so probably related to those wheels using Accelerate instead of OpenBLAS).

Should we generally not rely on the `np.matmul` returning identical values for identical values in the input array?

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
# using frombuffer to get the exact numbers at full precision
>>> coords = np.frombuffer(b'\xcb\x92\x87\x89\xfe\xde4@\x92\xce\xf0W\\L,\xc0\xb3\x03b\xffN!3\xc0\xe1y\x97b\x82\xb6+\xc0&ww\x99E\xe52\xc0\xfa\x8e\xbaY\xf7*0@\x04d\x85\xa0|\x1a5@\x14\x14h\xaap\xac/@\xcb\x92\x87\x89\xfe\xde4@\x92\xce\xf0W\\L,\xc0').reshape(5, 2)
>>> coords
array([[ 20.87107143, -14.14914203],
       [-19.13011166, -13.85646351],
       [-18.89559325,  16.16783677],
       [ 21.10346416,  15.83679707],
       [ 20.87107143, -14.14914203]])

>>> A = np.frombuffer(b'7\xb3\xf1\xdd\xc7\xff\xef\xbf\xeb\xe4b\x9b\xf5\xf7}?\xeb\xe4b\x9b\xf5\xf7}\xbf7\xb3\xf1\xdd\xc7\xff\xef\xbf').reshape(2, 2)
>>> A
array([[-0.99997323,  0.00731655],
       [-0.00731655, -0.99997323]])

>>> result = np.matmul(A, coords.T).T
>>> result
array([[-20.9740357 ,  13.99605905],
       [ 19.0282181 ,  13.99605905],
       [ 19.01338028, -16.02915345],
       [-20.98702857, -15.99077774],
       [-20.9740357 ,  13.99605905]])

# first and last row have *exactly* equal values in the input
>>> coords[0, :] == coords[-1, :]
array([ True,  True])

# for me (on linux, with OpenBLAS) this is still the case in the output
>>> result[0, :] == result[-1, :]
array([ True,  True])
```

But from other people that have tested this on MacOS 14, the last line here returns `False`.

### Python and NumPy Versions:

2.0.0
3.12.2 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Feb 16 2024, 20:50:58) [GCC 12.3.0]

(I haven't included my Runtime Environment information, because I cannot actually reproduce it on my system (Linux))","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26791/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26791/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2190424271","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26791#issuecomment-2190424271","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26791","id":2190424271,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86CjzjP","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-06-26T02:32:38Z","updated_at":"2024-06-26T02:32:38Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I find that with computational geometry code, it usually pays to be explicit and granular and avoid higher-level abstractions (from third parties, at least). Computational geometry is rife with invariants that need to be preserved that our typical abstractions for floating point computations rarely preserve.

In this case, you might consider using `matmul()` and fixing up the last point by copying in the result for the first point. You don't need to rely on `matmul()` preserving the invariant since this invariant is luckily relatively trivial to simply enforce at any time.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2190424271/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/pulls/comments/1655379964","pull_request_review_id":2142719381,"id":1655379964,"node_id":"PRRC_kwDOABZIoc5iqxf8","diff_hunk":"@@ -0,0 +1,5217 @@
+import functools

+
+import numpy as np
+

Reference issue
gh-15928
Closes gh-4538
Closes gh-8053
Closes gh-9321
Closes gh-10106 [^1]
Closes gh-10328
Closes gh-11465 [^2]
Closes gh-11746 [^3]
Closes gh-12192 [^3]
Closes gh-13504
Closes gh-13986
Closes gh-14895
Closes gh-16877
Closes gh-16998 [^4]
Closes gh-17307 [^5]
Closes gh-17620
Closes gh-18782 [^6]
Closes gh-19524 [^4]

[^1]: Additional improvements are possible, but random sampling in this infrastructure is still much faster due to vectorization of numerical integration and inversion.
[^2]: Added methods for inverse of the logarithm of the CDF
[^3]: Already closed for the old infrastructure, but addressed in the new infrastructure.
[^4]: Actually we still have an equivalent issue. Because instantiating a `Generator` is very slow, `ContinuousDistribution` does not convert the default `rng=None`into a PRNG until the first time it is used. If `None` were converted to the global `RandomState`, this would avoid the original issue exactly as suggested in gh-19524. However, `None` gets converted to a new `Generator` (`np.random.default_rng()`), which assumes an unpredicatable state. Therefore, if a random variable is copied before `sample` is called, copies will get different `Generator`s, whereas if it is copied *after* `sample` is called, copies will get the same `Generator`. There are several possible solutions; we need to decide which approach to take.
[^5]: It is the _policy_ of this infrastructure that errors will not be raised, but NaNs will be produced. This is more appropriate in a vectorized context: an error due to one bad element of an array should not cause the rest to fail.
[^6]: Infrastructure converts all inputs to consistent, at least float dtype.

#### What does this implement/fix?
See gh-15928. Compared to the branch reviewed in that RFC, there are only a few new commits here, beginning with 5fdb881d6d76d5bddae041a9b3d2f028c5df290f, that respond to some of the comments in the RFC as summarized by @tupui.

#### Additional information
There are many follow-up PRs planned before 1.15.0 (e.g. array API support, various distribution classes, test improvements, accuracy benchmarking and documentatino, tutorials, `fit`, other `TransformedDistrbution` subclasses, `DiscreteDistribution`, etc.). Have to start distribution","user":{"login":"mdhaber","id":6570539,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjY1NzA1Mzk=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/6570539?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber","html_url":"https://github.com/mdhaber","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/mdhaber/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"#### Reference issue
gh-15928
Closes gh-4538
Closes gh-8053
Closes gh-9321
Closes gh-10106 [^1]
Closes gh-10328
Closes gh-11465 [^2]
Closes gh-11746 [^3]
Closes gh-12192 [^3]
Closes gh-13504
Closes gh-13986
Closes gh-14895
Closes gh-16877
Closes gh-16998 [^4]
Closes gh-17307 [^5]
Closes gh-17620
Closes gh-18782 [^6]
Closes gh-19524 [^4]

[^1]: Additional improvements are possible, but random sampling in this infrastructure is still much faster due to vectorization of numerical integration and inversion.
[^2]: Added methods for inverse of the logarithm of the CDF
[^3]: Already closed for the old infrastructure, but addressed in the new infrastructure.
[^4]: Actually we still have an equivalent issue. Because instantiating a `Generator` is very slow, `ContinuousDistribution` does not convert the default `rng=None`into a PRNG until the first time it is used. If `None` were converted to the global `RandomState`, this would avoid the original issue exactly as suggested in gh-19524. However, `None` gets converted to a new `Generator` (`np.random.default_rng()`), which assumes an unpredicatable state. Therefore, if a random variable is copied before `sample` is called, copies will get different `Generator`s, whereas if it is copied *after* `sample` is called, copies will get the same `Generator`. There are several possible solutions; we need to decide which approach to take.
[^5]: It is the _policy_ of this infrastructure that errors will not be raised, but NaNs will be produced. This is more appropriate in a vectorized context: an error due to one bad element of an array should not cause the rest to fail.
[^6]: Infrastructure converts all inputs to consistent, at least float dtype.

#### What does this implement/fix?
See gh-15928. Compared to the branch reviewed in that RFC, there are only a few new commits here, beginning with 5fdb881d6d76d5bddae041a9b3d2f028c5df290f, that respond to some of the comments in the RFC as summarized by @tupui.

#### Additional information
There are many follow-up PRs planned before 1.15.0 (e.g. array API support, various distribution classes, test improvements, accuracy benchmarking and documentatino, tutorials, `fit`, other `TransformedDistrbution` subclasses, `DiscreteDistribution`, etc.). Have to start Reference issue
gh-15928
Closes gh-4538
Closes gh-8053
Closes gh-9321
Closes gh-10106 [^1]
Closes gh-10328
Closes gh-11465 [^2]
Closes gh-11746 [^3]
Closes gh-12192 [^3]
Closes gh-13504
Closes gh-13986
Closes gh-14895
Closes gh-16877
Closes gh-16998 [^4]
Closes gh-17307 [^5]
Closes gh-17620
Closes gh-18782 [^6]
Closes gh-19524 [^4]

[^1]: Additional improvements are possible, but random sampling in this infrastructure is still much faster due to vectorization of numerical integration and inversion.
[^2]: Added methods for inverse of the logarithm of the CDF
[^3]: Already closed for the old infrastructure, but addressed in the new infrastructure.
[^4]: Actually we still have an equivalent issue. Because instantiating a `Generator` is very slow, `ContinuousDistribution` does not convert the default `rng=None`into a PRNG until the first time it is used. If `None` were converted to the global `RandomState`, this would avoid the original issue exactly as suggested in gh-19524. However, `None` gets converted to a new `Generator` (`np.random.default_rng()`), which assumes an unpredicatable state. Therefore, if a random variable is copied before `sample` is called, copies will get different `Generator`s, whereas if it is copied *after* `sample` is called, copies will get the same `Generator`. There are several possible solutions; we need to decide which approach to take.
[^5]: It is the _policy_ of this infrastructure that errors will not be raised, but NaNs will be produced. This is more appropriate in a vectorized context: an error due to one bad element of an array should not cause the rest to fail.
[^6]: Infrastructure converts all inputs to consistent, at least float dtype.

#### What does this implement/fix?
See gh-15928. Compared to the branch reviewed in that RFC, there are only a few new commits here, beginning with 5fdb881d6d76d5bddae041a9b3d2f028c5df290f, that respond to some of the comments in the RFC as summarized by @tupui.

#### Additional information
There are many follow-up PRs planned before 1.15.0 (e.g. array API support, various distribution classes, test improvements, accuracy benchmarking and documentatino, tutorials, `fit`, other `TransformedDistrbution` subclasses, `DiscreteDistribution`, etc.). > But I suppose one solution to this problem is to turn `None` or a user-specified seed into a `Generator` if the user accesses the attribute. It is already a property, so this would be easy.

gh-19524 is an issue report about the old infrastructure; we probably shouldn't do design discussions on the new infrastructure there. Is `dist.sample(100)` sampling 100 realizations of a single RV or a realization each from 100 RVs described the same distribution? It depends on what the user does with the results (it can even be mixed in the case of `dist.sample((m, n))` where one usually intends to get `m` realizations each of `n` RVs). The API is (rightfully) agnostic because all of those variants arise, so I think it makes more sense to think of `ContinuousDistribution` as modeling distributions, if one wants to think in terms of what each thing *is*.

But I think it is usually more dispositive to think of what users want to *do* with each of these things. And when I look at the use cases for sampling from distributions, I see more times where I want to make the decision about the parameters of the distribution separately from when I want to give it the `Generator` I want it to sample from. In a simulation, I want to say that any given `Widget`'s height follows the same `Normal(10, 3)` distribution, but have them evaluate their random samples in different processes. My `Generator` handling follows how I divide up the work among processes, not how I model the `Widget`s. By putting the `rng` choice in the `ContinuousDistribution` constructor (whether actual instantiation is delayed or not), I am forced to make the decision in the modeling phase, which is mixing concerns. I admit that it *is* intuitively compelling to have the distribution own the `Generator` such that it is a complete object that you pass around by itself, which is why the old infrastructure had it, but the years of actual use indicate that it creates more problems than it actually solves.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2192822314/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Because instantiating a `Generator` is very slow, `ContinuousDistribution` does not convert the default `rng=None`into a PRNG until the first time it is used. If `None` were converted to the global `RandomState`, this would avoid the original issue exactly as suggested in gh-19524. However, `None` gets converted to a new `Generator` (`np.random.default_rng()`), which assumes an unpredicatable state. Therefore, if a random variable is copied before `sample` is called, copies will get different `Generator`s, whereas if it is copied *after* `sample` is called, copies will get the same `Generator`. There are several possible solutions; we need to decide which approach to take.

Carrying around a `Generator` instance creates these problems and compromises that just don't have to exist (and no, I don't think there is a way around gh-19524). It's a general strategy to modify nonlinear optimizers that use local approximations. The ODRPACKs implement a trust-region Levenberg-Marquardt. It's not clear to me that the trust region optimizers that we have are directly usable. The key insight of ODRPACK is that the innermost part of the LM algorithm can be applied to the ODR problem (which expands the number of parameters it needs to solve for from `np` to `np+nx`) efficiently by making use of the specific structure of the Jacobian matrix that's used to determine the LM step. It's not clear to me if the trust region methods we have implemented make use of the same Jacobian matrix or that we could intervene that deeply inside of our implementations of them to make use of the special structure.\r\n\r\n[Zwolak2006](https://core.ac.uk/download/pdf/10675932.pdf) has a clear explanation of the ODRPACK95 algorithm.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2093111687/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Backport-Candidate","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"PRs tagged should be backported"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-05-06T09:30:11Z","updated_at":"2024-05-06T15:44:29Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/26388","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26388","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26388.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26388.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Currently, when NumPy saves data using pickle, it forces the protocol version to 3, which was the default value from Python 3.0 to Python 3.7. However, since Python 3.7 has reached its end-of-life (EOL), there are no actively maintained Python versions that default to using pickle protocol 3.

By allowing NumPy to use the default pickle protocol version, objects larger than 4GB can be pickled, resolving the issue described in #26224. Although this new protocol version is incompatible with Python 3.3 and earlier versions, Python 3.3 has long reached its EOL. Therefore, there is no need to force pickle protocol 3 when saving data using pickle.

It is important to note that the pickle module still supports reading pickled objects from protocol 3. As a result, this change will not introduce incompatibilities when reading files saved with an earlier pickle protocol(which is used in current numpy release).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26388/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26388/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2096345395","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/26388#issuecomment-2096345395","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26388","id":2096345395,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP85887Ez","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-06T15:44:27Z","updated_at":"2024-05-06T15:44:27Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"There is a reason that we hardcode the protocol, despite the drawbacks when we forget about it as we drop support for versions of Python. We don't want Python 3.14 to decide that `pickle.DEFAULT_PROTOCOL` is 6, or something, while we're still supporting a version of Python that doesn't have it.

In an ideal world, the policy that we probably want is something like:

* we specify the protocol to be the "best" (probably highest, but only if the features warrant it; I'd probably go with `5` right now) one supported by the versions of Python that we support
* we add "check if we need to change the `np.save()` pickle protocol" to the checklist when we drop EOL Python versions
  - creating such a checklist if we don't have one already
* allow `np.save()` users to override it","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2096345395/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

The policy in that comment is that you are only _allowed_ to bump it if the last version that didn't have it is EOLed. There's nothing in there that states that it _will_ be bumped when that version goes EOL. `5` was introduced in 3.8, and 3.7 EOLed before 3.12, but [3.12 and 3.13 still have `4` as the default](https://docs.python.org/3.13/library/pickle.html#pickle.DEFAULT_PROTOCOL). In any case, Python's EOL schedule and numpy's Python version support are not always in lock-step.

> New features provided by pickle actually need to be adapted by new code (e.g., Out-of-band Buffers for pickle protocol 5).

The `5` format will handle them in-band just fine without needing to modify the `pickle.dump()` call, and AFAICT, [more efficiently than if one was stuck with `4`](https://peps.python.org/pep-0574/#improved-in-band-performance). So if people have objects that support `5` by returning `PickleBuffer`s, it would be good to support it even if we don't do anything out-of-band. People are increasingly sticking weirder things into arrays and passing them about (e.g. variably sized images, text fragments, etc.) for ML purposes, so this isn't entirely theoretical.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2096764481/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"closed","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26408","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26408/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26408/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26408/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26408","id":2287911320,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86IXsGY","number":26408,"title":"ENH: Add context management for np.seterr","user":{"login":"hjenryin","id":72179072,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcyMTc5MDcy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/72179072?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin","html_url":"https://github.com/hjenryin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-05-09T15:20:10Z","updated_at":"2024-05-09T15:26:49Z","closed_at":"2024-05-09T15:26:48Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

The current way (according to [1.26 doc](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.seterr.html)) of setting and resetting error is
```
old_settings = np.seterr(all='ignore')  #seterr to known value
np.seterr(over='raise')
{'divide': 'ignore', 'over': 'ignore', 'under': 'ignore', 'invalid': 'ignore'}
np.seterr(**old_settings)  # reset to default
```
This may be tedious and not elegant when we need to suppress the error for some certain lines, for example, `np.nan_to_num(a/b)` as we need to suppress `divide` here. 

I think it would be way more elegant to use `with` statement here, which should be able to be implemented with some simple changes.

An ideal result would be like:
```
with np.seterr(divide='ignore'):
    np.nan_to_num(a/b) # no warning

a/0 # still warn
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26408/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26408/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26408","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26408/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26408/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26408/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26408","id":2287911320,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86IXsGY","number":26408,"title":"ENH: Add context management for np.seterr","user":{"login":"hjenryin","id":72179072,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjcyMTc5MDcy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/72179072?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin","html_url":"https://github.com/hjenryin","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hjenryin/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-05-09T15:20:10Z","updated_at":"2024-05-09T15:26:49Z","closed_at":"2024-05-09T15:26:48Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

The current way (according to [1.26 doc](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.seterr.html)) of setting and resetting error is
```
old_settings = np.seterr(all='ignore')  #seterr to known value
np.seterr(over='raise')
{'divide': 'ignore', 'over': 'ignore', 'under': 'ignore', 'invalid': 'ignore'}
np.seterr(**old_settings)  # reset to default
```
This may be tedious and not elegant when we need to suppress the error for some certain lines, for example, `np.nan_to_num(a/b)` as we need to suppress `divide` here. 

I think it would be way more elegant to use `with` statement here, which should be able to be implemented with some simple changes.

An ideal result would be like:
```
with np.seterr(divide='ignore'):
    np.nan_to_num(a/b) # no warning

Describe your issue.

It has come to our attention that the `curve_fit` function in `scipy.optimize` produces different fitting results across platforms, even when running identical code and using the same data samples as shown in the sample code below.

The function in question models a trimodal distribution, which is the sum of three Gaussian functions with varying means, standard deviations, and amplitudes (or peak heights).

The fitting results differ across platforms, as detailed in the `Error message` section below.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
'''
Example trimodal fitting code to be reported to SciPy

Code and sample data based on works from Jun
'''

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit

def map_param(param_name_list, param_array):
    '''
    A helper function to map the list of parameters to keyword parameters

    Parameters
    ----------
    param_name_list: list
        List of parameter names
    
    param_array: list or np.ndarray
        array of parameters

    returns
    -------
    param_dict: dict
        keyword parameter
    '''
    param_dict = {}

    for i_param, param_name in enumerate(param_name_list):
        param_dict[param_name] = param_array[i_param]
    
    return param_dict

# Define trimodal function
def gauss(array, mu, sigma, amplitude):
    """
    Generate gaussian distribution with given mean and std

    Parameters
    ----------

    array: np.ndarray
        Input value for the Gaussian function
    
    mu: float
        Mean value of the Gaussian function
    
    sigma: float
        Standard deviation of the Gaussian function

    amplitude: float
        Amplitude (or, "peak height") of the Gaussian function
    
    
    Returns
    -------
    _: np.ndarray
        Output of the Gaussian value
    """
    return amplitude * np.exp(-(array - mu)**2 / (2 * sigma**2))


def trimodal(array, mu1, sigma1, amplitude1,
             mu2, sigma2, amplitude2,
              mu3, sigma3, amplitude3):
    """
    Generate trimodal gaussian distribution with given means and stds

    Parameters
    ----------

    array: np.ndarray
        Input of the trimodal function

    mu1, sigma1, amplitude1: float
        mean value, standard deviation of the 1st Gaussian function
    
    mu2, sigma2, amplitude2: float
        mean value, standard deviation of the 2nd Gaussian function

    mu3, sigma3, amplitude3: float
        mean value, standard deviation of the 3rd Gaussian function
    
    Returns
    -------
    _ : np.ndarray
        Out of the trimodal function
    """
    return gauss(array, mu1, sigma1, amplitude1) + \
        gauss(array, mu2, sigma2, amplitude2) + \
        gauss(array, mu3, sigma3, amplitude3)

Floating point arithmetic can differ between CPUs. You have a degenerate model with a number of symmetries, so the local optimizer's path will end up having a number of branching points in the loss function that differing floating point calculations could send it down, creating large differences in the final result despite the small differences in any given floating point calculation.

You could try to help this by reparameterizing your model and applying constraints so that you don't have as many symmetries. For example, consider representing the means as `mu1` (same as before), `dmu2 = mu2 - mu1` and `dmu3 = mu3 - mu2`. Then optimize the parameter set `mu1, dmu2, dmu3, ... etc.` with `dmu2` and `dmu3` bounded from below by 0. That will break some of the symmetries, though the loss surface may still have a number of those branching points remaining.

I don't think there's much we can do about it on our end. Describe your issue.

It has come to our attention that the `curve_fit` function in `scipy.optimize` produces different fitting results across platforms, even when running identical code and using the same data samples as shown in the sample code below.

The function in question models a trimodal distribution, which is the sum of three Gaussian functions with varying means, standard deviations, and amplitudes (or peak heights).

The fitting results differ across platforms, as detailed in the `Error message` section below.

### Reproducing Code Example

```python
'''
Example trimodal fitting code to be reported to SciPy

Code and sample data based on works from Junkyo Jung (JPL)
'''

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
from scipy.optimize import curve_fit

def map_param(param_name_list, param_array):
    '''
    A helper function to map the list of parameters to keyword parameters

    Parameters
    ----------
    param_name_list: list
        List of parameter names
    
    param_array: list or np.ndarray
        array of parameters

    returns
    -------
    param_dict: dict
        keyword parameter
    '''
    param_dict = {}

    for i_param, param_name in enumerate(param_name_list):
        param_dict[param_name] = param_array[i_param]
    
    return param_dict

# Define trimodal function
def gauss(array, mu, sigma, amplitude):
    """
    Generate gaussian distribution with given mean and std

    Parameters
    ----------

    array: np.ndarray
        Input value for the Gaussian function
    
    mu: float
        Mean value of the Gaussian function
    
    sigma: float
        Standard deviation of the Gaussian function

    amplitude: float
        Amplitude (or, "peak height") of the Gaussian function
    
    
    Returns
    -------
    _: np.ndarray
        Output of the Gaussian value
    """
    return amplitude * np.exp(-(array - mu)**2 / (2 * sigma**2))


def trimodal(array, mu1, sigma1, amplitude1,
             mu2, sigma2, amplitude2,
              mu3, sigma3, amplitude3):
    """
    Generate trimodal gaussian distribution with given means and stds

    Parameters
    ----------

    array: np.ndarray
        Input of the trimodal function

    mu1, sigma1, amplitude1: float
        mean value, standard deviation of the 1st Gaussian function
    
    mu2, sigma2, amplitude2: float
        mean value, standard deviation of the 2nd Gaussian function

    mu3, sigma3, amplitude3: float
        mean value, standard deviation of the 3rd Gaussian function
    
    Returns
    -------
    _ : np.ndarray
        Out of the trimodal function
    """
    return gauss(array, mu1, sigma1, amplitude1) + \
        gauss(array, mu2, sigma2, amplitude2) + \
        gauss(array, mu3, sigma3, amplitude3)

Reference issue
gh-7242

#### What does this implement/fix?
This adds array API support to `scipy.optimize._chandrupatla` and paves the way toward adding array API support to the other elementwise iterative methods (e.g. `_differentiate`, `_tanhsinh`, `_nsum`, `_chandrupatla_minimize`, and the bracket finders). I'll propose making the rootfinder, minimizer, and bracket finders public shortly.

#### Additional information
The performance of this function compared to the existing bracketing rootfinders is worst when function evaluations are inexpensive, so I compared the performance between
- `brentq`
- `_chandrupatla` (NumPy)
- `_chandrupatla` (PyTorch, CPU)
- `_chandrupatla` (CuPy)

when finding the root of `xp.cos(x) - p` for many values of `p`. The bracket is always $[0, \pi]$. `brentq`'s default absolute x-tolerance is `2e-12`, so I set `_chandrupatla` to `1e-12` to be more than fair, and I confirmed that both solvers meet their respective tolerance. `_chandrupatla` finds the roots in a single vectorized call whereas `brentq` loops with a list comprehension.

![image](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/assets/6570539/a2d3c250-d84d-49d4-910d-ec03b63e5a66)

<details>

```
# import time

# import torch

#

# from scipy import optimize
# from scipy.optimize._chandrupatla import _chandrupatla
#
# xp = torch
# n = 10
# n = int(n)
# a = xp.asarray(0)
# b = xp.asarray(np.pi)
# ps = xp.asarray(rng.random(size=n) * 2 - 1, dtype=xp.float64)
#
# def f(x, p):

#
# tic = time.perf_counter()
# res = _chandrupatla(f, a, b, args=(ps,), xatol=1e-12)
# toc = time.perf_counter()

import time
import numpy as np
import cupy as cp
import torch
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1638083107694713882823079058616272161)
from scipy import optimize
from scipy.optimize._chandrupatla import _chandrupatla

ns = np.logspace(0, 6, 30)

times = {np: [], cp: [], torch: [],  'brentq':[]}

for xp in [np, cp, torch, 'brentq']:
    brentq = False
    if xp == 'brentq':
        xp = np
        brentq = True

    for n in ns:
        n = int(n)
        a = xp.asarray(0)
        b = xp.asarray(np.pi)
        ps = xp.asarray(rng.random(size=n) * 2 - 1, dtype=xp.float64)

        def f(x, p):
            return xp.cos(x) - p

        if brentq:
            tic = time.perf_counter()
            ref = [optimize.brentq(f, a, b, args=(p,)) for p in ps]
            toc = time.perf_counter()
            times['brentq'].append(toc - tic)
            np.testing.assert_allclose(ref, np.arccos(ps), atol=2e-12)
            continue

        tic = time.perf_counter()
        res = _chandrupatla(f, a, b, args=(ps,), xatol=1e-12)
        toc = time.perf_counter()
        times[xp].append(toc - tic)
        np.testing.assert_allclose(cp.asnumpy(res.x), np.arccos(cp.asnumpy(ps)), atol=1e-12)

plt.loglog(ns, times[np], label='np')
plt.loglog(ns, times[cp], label='cp')
plt.loglog(ns, times[torch], label='torch')
plt.loglog(ns, times['brentq'], label='brentq')
plt.xlabel('number of roots')
plt.ylabel('execution time (s)')
plt.title('Root of `xp.cos(x) - p`')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

</details>

The function has a lot of overhead due to bells and whistles (e.g. input validation with nice error messages, rich result object, callback function support, etc.). But for solving a lot of equations, the overhead of function calls eventually becomes problematic for `brentq`.

In this case, you could probably get better performance with `cython_optimize`, but for more expensive functions with overhead, like finding the `argus(1)` distribution ppf (https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/17719#issuecomment-1547778146), the advantage is much more pronounced.

![image](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/assets/6570539/712cda1a-77a2-4f1f-b021-c1fc397c7d25)

<details>

```python3
import time
import numpy as np
import cupy as cp
import torch
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1638083107694713882823079058616272161)
from scipy import optimize, stats
from scipy.optimize._chandrupatla import _chandrupatla

ns = np.logspace(0, 4, 30)

times = {np: [], cp: [], torch: [], 'brentq':[]}

for xp in [np, 'brentq']:
    brentq = False
    if xp == 'brentq':
        xp = np
        brentq = True

    for n in ns:
        n = int(n)
        a = 0.001
        b = 0.999
        ps = np.linspace(0.005, 0.995, n)

        dist = stats.argus(1)
        def f(x, p):
            return dist.cdf(x) - p

        if brentq:
            tic = time.perf_counter()
            ref = [optimize.brentq(f, a, b, args=(p,)) for p in ps]
            toc = time.perf_counter()
            times['brentq'].append(toc - tic)
            continue

        tic = time.perf_counter()
        res = _chandrupatla(f, a, b, args=(ps,), xatol=1e-12)
        toc = time.perf_counter()
        times[xp].append(toc - tic)

plt.loglog(ns, times[np], label='np')
# plt.loglog(ns, times[cp], label='cp')
plt.loglog(ns, times['brentq'], label='brentq')
plt.xlabel('number of probabilities')
plt.ylabel('execution time (s)')
plt.title('Root of `argus(1).cdf(x) - p`')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

</details>

`newton` is definitely faster than this function, but it should be - the algorithm converges faster. (The advantage of bracketing methods is that convergence is guaranteed if the bracket is valid.) We can add that as another method with the same framework.

The tests are quite strict; I'm ironing out a few failures with alternative backends. 

The function currently uses fancy indexing assignment. I can investigate working around that for strict array API support Reference issue
gh-7242

#### What does this implement/fix?
This adds array API support to `scipy.optimize._chandrupatla` and paves the way toward adding array API support to the other elementwise iterative methods (e.g. `_differentiate`, `_tanhsinh`, `_nsum`, `_chandrupatla_minimize`, and the bracket finders). I'll propose making the rootfinder, minimizer, and bracket finders public shortly.

#### Additional information
The performance of this function compared to the existing bracketing rootfinders is worst when function evaluations are inexpensive, so I compared the performance between
- `brentq`
- `_chandrupatla` (NumPy)
- `_chandrupatla` (PyTorch, CPU)
- `_chandrupatla` (CuPy)

when finding the root of `xp.cos(x) - p` for many values of `p`. The bracket is always $[0, \pi]$. `brentq`'s default absolute x-tolerance is `2e-12`, so I set `_chandrupatla` to `1e-12` to be more than fair, and I confirmed that both solvers meet their respective tolerance. `_chandrupatla` finds the roots in a single vectorized call whereas `brentq` loops with a list comprehension.

![image](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/assets/6570539/a2d3c250-d84d-49d4-910d-ec03b63e5a66)

<details>

```
# import time
# import numpy as np
# import cupy as cp
# import torch
# import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
#
# rng = np.random.default_rng(1638083107694713882823079058616272161)
# from scipy import optimize
# from scipy.optimize._chandrupatla import _chandrupatla
#
# xp = torch
# n = 10
# n = int(n)
# a = xp.asarray(0)
# b = xp.asarray(np.pi)
# ps = xp.asarray(rng.random(size=n) * 2 - 1, dtype=xp.float64)
#
# def f(x, p):
#     return xp.cos(x) - p
#
# tic = time.perf_counter()
# res = _chandrupatla(f, a, b, args=(ps,), xatol=1e-12)
# toc = time.perf_counter()

import time
import numpy as np
import cupy as cp
import torch
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1638083107694713882823079058616272161)
from scipy import optimize
from scipy.optimize._chandrupatla import _chandrupatla

ns = np.logspace(0, 6, 30)

times = {np: [], cp: [], torch: [],  'brentq':[]}

for xp in [np, cp, torch, 'brentq']:
    brentq = False
    if xp == 'brentq':
        xp = np
        brentq = True

    for n in ns:
        n = int(n)
        a = xp.asarray(0)
        b = xp.asarray(np.pi)
        ps = xp.asarray(rng.random(size=n) * 2 - 1, dtype=xp.float64)

        def f(x, p):
            return xp.cos(x) - p

        if brentq:
            tic = time.perf_counter()
            ref = [optimize.brentq(f, a, b, args=(p,)) for p in ps]
            toc = time.perf_counter()
            times['brentq'].append(toc - tic)
            np.testing.assert_allclose(ref, np.arccos(ps), atol=2e-12)
            continue

        tic = time.perf_counter()
        res = _chandrupatla(f, a, b, args=(ps,), xatol=1e-12)
        toc = time.perf_counter()
        times[xp].append(toc - tic)
        np.testing.assert_allclose(cp.asnumpy(res.x), np.arccos(cp.asnumpy(ps)), atol=1e-12)

plt.loglog(ns, times[np], label='np')
plt.loglog(ns, times[cp], label='cp')
plt.loglog(ns, times[torch], label='torch')
plt.loglog(ns, times['brentq'], label='brentq')
plt.xlabel('number of roots')
plt.ylabel('execution time (s)')
plt.title('Root of `xp.cos(x) - p`')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

</details>

The function has a lot of overhead due to bells and whistles (e.g. input validation with nice error messages, rich result object, callback function support, etc.). But for solving a lot of equations, the overhead of function calls eventually becomes problematic for `brentq`.

In this case, you could probably get better performance with `cython_optimize`, but for more expensive functions with overhead, like finding the `argus(1)` distribution ppf (https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/17719#issuecomment-1547778146), the advantage is much more pronounced.

![image](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/assets/6570539/712cda1a-77a2-4f1f-b021-c1fc397c7d25)

<details>

```python3
import time
import numpy as np
import cupy as cp
import torch
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(1638083107694713882823079058616272161)
from scipy import optimize, stats
from scipy.optimize._chandrupatla import _chandrupatla

ns = np.logspace(0, 4, 30)

times = {np: [], cp: [], torch: [], 'brentq':[]}

for xp in [np, 'brentq']:
    brentq = False
    if xp == 'brentq':
        xp = np
        brentq = True

    for n in ns:
        n = int(n)
        a = 0.001
        b = 0.999
        ps = np.linspace(0.005, 0.995, n)

        dist = stats.argus(1)
        def f(x, p):
            return dist.cdf(x) - p

        if brentq:
            tic = time.perf_counter()
            ref = [optimize.brentq(f, a, b, args=(p,)) for p in ps]
            toc = time.perf_counter()
            times['brentq'].append(toc - tic)
            continue

        tic = time.perf_counter()
        res = _chandrupatla(f, a, b, args=(ps,), xatol=1e-12)
        toc = time.perf_counter()
        times[xp].append(toc - tic)

plt.loglog(ns, times[np], label='np')
# plt.loglog(ns, times[cp], label='cp')
plt.loglog(ns, times['brentq'], label='brentq')
plt.xlabel('number of probabilities')
plt.ylabel('execution time (s)')
plt.title('Root of `argus(1).cdf(x) - p`')
plt.legend()
plt.show()
```

</details>

`newton` is definitely faster than this function, but it should be - the algorithm converges faster. (The advantage of bracketing methods is that convergence is guaranteed if the bracket is valid.) We can add that as another method with the same framework.

The tests are quite strict; I'm ironing out a few failures with alternative backends. 

The function currently uses fancy indexing assignment. I can investigate working around that for strict array API support {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26446","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26446/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26446/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26446/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26446","id":2298724473,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86JA8B5","number":26446,"title":"BUG: Some DTypes have identical hashes but distinct ids","user":{"login":"ngoldbaum","id":3126246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxMjYyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/3126246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum","html_url":"https://github.com/ngoldbaum","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":7,"created_at":"2024-05-15T19:50:13Z","updated_at":"2024-05-16T01:11:05Z","closed_at":"2024-05-15T20:53:36Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Matti originally found this on a 32 bit system, but on 64 bit:

```
>>> hash(np.dtype('uint64'))
-3444929848325097739
>>> hash(np.dtype('ulonglong'))
-3444929848325097739
>>> np.dtype('uint64') is np.dtype('ulonglong')
False
>>> id(np.dtype('uint64')), id(np.dtype('ulonglong'))
(4424700496, 4424700736)
```

I'm guessing this is a problem with the way we're creating these aliases at a low level in C. Somehow python exposes distinct objects, but `tp_hash` sees the exact same object.

_Originally posted by @mattip in https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26439#issuecomment-2112882590_

```python
x = [0, 1]
y = [0, 1]
fit = Polynomial.fit(x, y, deg=1)

print(f"Coefficients for 1-order fit on {x=}, {y=}:")
print(f"Raw: {fit.coef}")
print(f"Converted: {fit.convert().coef}")
```
which prints
```
Coefficients for 1-order fit on x=[0, 1], y=[0, 1]:
Raw: [0.5 0.5]
Converted: [-1.66533454e-16  1.00000000e+00]
```

The result for the raw `fit.coef` is unexpected. The fit is scaled with respect to its current domain, which is by default the endpoints of `x`. The `fit.convert().coef` gives the expected result, but this extra call is somewhat awkward and can easily be overlooked.

### Proposed new feature or change:

I would prefer to set `domain=[-1, 1]` as a default argument of `numpy.polynomial.polynomial.Polynomial.fit`, just as it is for the `numpy.polynomial.polynomial.Polynomial` class. This creates commonality, but also avoids the unexpected outcome as in the above example.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26401/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26401/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2121188391","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26401#issuecomment-2121188391","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26401","id":2121188391,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP85-bsQn","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-05-20T20:52:46Z","updated_at":"2024-05-20T20:52:46Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"The semantics of `.coef` can't change at this time. An error 
```
Argument 1 to "choice" of "Generator" has incompatible type "list[DsAlg]"; expected "Buffer | _SupportsArray[dtype[Any]] | _NestedSequence[_SupportsArray[dtype[Any]]] | bool | int | float | complex | str | bytes | _NestedSequence[bool | int | float | complex | str | bytes]"Mypy[arg-type](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_refs.html#code-arg-type)
```
is raised by mypy when using `choice` (either from `Generator` or direct function) on a list of enums.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import enum
import numpy as np

class Test(enum.Enum):
    A = enum.auto()
    B = enum.auto()
    C = enum.auto()
    D = enum.auto()


np.random.choice([Test.A, Test.B, Test.C], size=100)  # <-- mypy error

_rng = np.random.default_rng()
_rng.choice(list(Test), size=100)  # <-- mypy error
_rng.choice([Test.A, Test.B, Test.C], size=100)  # <-- mypy error
```


### Error message:

```shell
Argument 1 to "choice" of "Generator" has incompatible type "list[DsAlg]"; expected "Buffer | _SupportsArray[dtype[Any]] | _NestedSequence[_SupportsArray[dtype[Any]]] | bool | int | float | complex | str | bytes | _NestedSequence[bool | int | float | complex | str | bytes]"Mypy[arg-type](https://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_refs.html#code-arg-type)
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

1.26.4
3.12.3 (main, Apr 23 2024, 09:16:07) [GCC 13.2.1 20240417]

MyPy version: mypy 1.10.0 (compiled: yes)

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-05-21T15:16:38Z","updated_at":"2024-05-21T19:17:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

The documentation misses how to build & install numpy from source using a custom compiler. There is https://numpy.org/devdocs/building/index.html#building-from-source-to-use-numpy which states the usual workflow `pip install .`

That is missing how to change the compiler, e.g. to use the Intel Fortran compiler instead of the GNU variant.

https://numpy.org/devdocs/f2py/buildtools/distutils.html#extensions-to-distutils states `--fcompiler=<Vendor>` but there is no example on how to use it, especially for installing numpy itself.

We used to do e.g. `python setup.py build --fcompiler=gnu95` but that is deprecated now:
```
  `numpy.distutils` is deprecated since NumPy 1.23.0, as a result
  of the deprecation of `distutils` itself. It will be removed for
  Python >= 3.12.
```

So there doesn't seem to be an option to change the Fortran compiler, when using pip, especially with `--global-option` being ignored by PEP517 builds, see https://github.com/pypa/pip/issues/11859
> WARNING: Ignoring --global-option when building numpy using PEP 517

Additionally the [migration guide](https://numpy.org/devdocs/reference/distutils_status_migration.html#moving-to-setuptools) also mentions that `site.cfg` is only supported by the numpy distutils but we use that a lot, e.g. for specifying FFT & BLAS libraries and e.g. `search_static_first=True`

Is there any option to specify the Fortran compiler to use when building with PIP? If so, is it compatible with earlier numpy versions and which ones?

Where?

When you use `numpy.distutils` and `f2py` to build some other Fortran-using library. numpy basically bundles (or bundled, as they are now essentially deprecated) a Fortran bindings builder and a build system for use _by other projects_. numpy does not need the Fortran components in order to build itself.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2123285862/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

all test code :
```
import numpy as np
import zipfile
from numpy.compat import (
    isfileobj, os_fspath, pickle
    )
def load_from_npz(path, name):
    zf = zipfile.ZipFile(path)
    info = zf.NameToInfo[name + '.npy']
    assert info.compress_type == 0
    zf.fp.seek(info.header_offset + len(info.FileHeader()) + 20)

    encoding='ASCII'
    fix_imports=True
    pickle_kwargs = dict(encoding=encoding, fix_imports=fix_imports)
    array = np.lib.format.read_array(zf.fp, allow_pickle=True, pickle_kwargs=pickle_kwargs)
    return array

# create .npz file

x2 = np.arange(250000000, dtype=np.uint8)
x={"x1":x1,"x2":x2}

xx = np.load("/tmp/test.npz", allow_pickle=True)['row']

Generator.choice with explict size=() fails whereas it works with size=None, when a.ndim > 1

According to the docs this should be a valid operation.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

rng = np.random.default_rng()
rng.choice([[1, 2, 3]], size=())  # ValueError: setting an array element with a sequence.
```


### Error message:

```shell
TypeError: only length-1 arrays can be converted to Python scalars
The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/ricardo/miniconda3/envs/pytensor/lib/python3.12/site-packages/IPython/core/interactiveshell.py", line 3577, in run_code
    exec(code_obj, self.user_global_ns, self.user_ns)
  File "<ipython-input-3-4a79a97119ec>", line 4, in <module>
    rng.choice(np.array([[1, 2, 3]]), size=()) # ValueError: setting an array element with a sequence.
    ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
  File "numpy/random/_generator.pyx", line 938, in numpy.random._generator.Generator.choice
ValueError: setting an array element with a sequence.
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

1.26.4
3.12.2 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Feb 16 2024, 20:50:58) [GCC 12.3.0]

A "sample" in the case of a sequence of shape-`(3,)` arrays is a shape-`(3,)` array. In our other multivariate distributions, the provided `size` tuple gets prepended to the shape of a single sample and is not the shape of the output itself.

```python
>>> rng.multinomial(10, np.full(4, 1/4), size=(10,)).shape
(10, 4)
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2129472658/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I was trying to use a float as a seed for my numpy random number generator but I got the following error saying its the wrong type. I have set my numpy seed as a float before but now its no longer working now.



### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

np.random.seed(0.1)
```


### Error message:

```shell

TypeError: 'float' object cannot be interpreted as an integer

 Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>

Describe your issue.

Greetings, 

I am writing a general infrastructure for the optimization of heat exchangers (basically means relatively complicated objective function) as a part of my phd thesis. In general the problem has arbitrary bounds, constraints and objective functions and I use SLSQP to solve it. 

With 2 different heat exchanger models this works wonderfully and I get optimal solutions that respect all the bounds and constraints. However, with another model i get messages about " Inequality constraints incompatible" when the constraints are correctly calculated and positive. Even if i carry out an optimization without any constraint (that is, I comment the line specificing the constraint to the minimize function) I still get the same error.  Note that I am starting the optimization from a few feasible initial solutions and I get the error right at the beginning, when it is calculating some derivatives. 

Do you happen to have any clue/hint of what is causing this issue? Clearly the "Inequality constraints incompatible" error is misleading.

```python
.
```


### Error message

```shell
Inequality constraints incompatible
```

Describe your issue.

Greetings, 

I am writing a general infrastructure for the optimization of heat exchangers (basically means relatively complicated objective function) as a part of my phd thesis. In general the problem has arbitrary bounds, constraints and objective functions and I use SLSQP to solve it. 

With 2 different heat exchanger models this works wonderfully and I get optimal solutions that respect all the bounds and constraints. However, with another model i get messages about " Inequality constraints incompatible" when the constraints are correctly calculated and positive. Even if i carry out an optimization without any constraint (that is, I comment the line specificing the constraint to the minimize function) I still get the same error.  Note that I am starting the optimization from a few feasible initial solutions and I get the error right at the beginning, when it is calculating some derivatives. 

Do you happen to have any clue/hint of what is causing this issue? Clearly the "Inequality constraints incompatible" error is misleading.



### Reproducing Code Example

```python
.
```


### Error message

```shell
Inequality constraints incompatible
```

{"action":"closed","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/18124","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/18124/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/18124/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/18124/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/18124","id":779322657,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWU3NzkzMjI2NTc=","number":18124,"title":"BUG: Multiprocessing issue for .npz files","user":{"login":"Th-Fo","id":42180212,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQyMTgwMjEy","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/42180212?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo","html_url":"https://github.com/Th-Fo","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Th-Fo/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2021-01-05T17:24:34Z","updated_at":"2024-05-30T14:01:29Z","closed_at":"2024-05-30T14:01:29Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Accessing the entries of a NpzFile object can raise a BadZipFile error (for an intact .npz file) under the following circumstances:
* First, `np.load('my_file.npz')` is called in some process.
* Afterwards, this process is forked, e.g. via multiprocessing module.
* Several of these processes (either multiple child processes, or the parent plus >=1 child processes) access its entries after forking.
The occurrence of this error depends on the .npz file, on the order in which the entries are read out, and apparently also on the machine (on the RAM size?), but seems deterministic for a certain configuration. It happens on all Python and NumPy versions I have tested, including Python 3.9.1 and NumPy 1.19.2 (newest versions available for me). It is not clear to me whether the problem is on the level of Numpy, zipfile, or somewhere else.

Minimal example:

a) Generating the dataset. This part is a bit "tricky" because different .npz files are needed to trigger the bug on different machines; however, for all machines I have tried, I was able to find a .npz file which reproduced the bug (the Python/NumPy version didn't play a role then).

Among the easiest configurations with a high chance to trigger the bug are

```
# especially for machines with <=32 GB RAM
import numpy as np
np.savez(
		'data.npz',
		item_0=np.random.randn(10000),
		item_1=np.random.randn(200),
		item_2=np.random.randn(10000)
)
```

and

```
# especially for machines with >150 GB RAM
import numpy as np
np.savez(
		'data.npz',
		item_0=np.random.randn(100000),
		item_1=np.random.randn(200000)
)
```

If this doesn't work, try

```
import collections
import numpy as np

typical_size = 1000 # also try 10000 or 100000
sizes = np.round(-typical_size*np.log(np.random.rand(20))).astype(int)

np.savez(
		'data.npz',
		**collections.OrderedDict([
			('item_%d'%idx, np.random.randn(size))
			for idx, size in enumerate(sizes)
		])
)
```

b) apply the "recipe" described in the beginning:

```
import multiprocessing
import numpy as np
import time

def func(data):
	for _ in data.values(): # accessing the .npz file from the child processes
		time.sleep(0.2) # to make the access order deterministic

data = np.load('data.npz') # loading the .npz file in the parent process
for _ in range(2):
	# launching child processes inside this loop, passing data as an argument to the target function
	subproc = multiprocessing.Process(target=func, args=[data])
	subproc.start()
	time.sleep(0.1) # to make the access order deterministic
```

result:

```
Process Process-2:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/multiprocessing/process.py", line 258, in _bootstrap
    self.run()
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/multiprocessing/process.py", line 93, in run
    self._target(*self._args, **self._kwargs)
  File "minimal_example.py", line 6, in func
    for _ in data.values():
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/_collections_abc.py", line 762, in __iter__
    yield self._mapping[key]
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/site-packages/numpy/lib/npyio.py", line 256, in __getitem__
    magic = bytes.read(len(format.MAGIC_PREFIX))
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/zipfile.py", line 872, in read
    data = self._read1(n)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/zipfile.py", line 962, in _read1
    self._update_crc(data)
  File "/usr/lib64/python3.6/zipfile.py", line 890, in _update_crc
    raise BadZipFile("Bad CRC-32 for file %r" % self.name)
zipfile.BadZipFile: Bad CRC-32 for file 'item_1.npy'
```

Remarks:
* The error does not occur if np.load is called within each child process, or if the parent process passes an own NpzFile instance to each child process. This might serve as a quick fix, but at least doubles the runtime for calling np.load if both the parent and the child process need to access the considered file, and might be complicated to implement for applications into which multiprocessing is deeply built in.

* Dependent on the .npz file and the machine, slightly different errors can occur, including:

  zipfile.BadZipFile: Bad magic number for file header
  zipfile.BadZipFile: Truncated file header
  zipfile.BadZipFile: File name in directory 'item_2.npy' and header b'it' differ.
  EOFError

* For a fixed order in which the entries are read out, the entries whose readout cause an error are deterministic. If the exception is caught and readout is repeated, accessing an entry succeeds or fails always like it did for the first time (I guess the reason for this is in the caching). This behavior can be seen, for example, by replacing func in the minimal example with

```
import traceback
def func(data):
	for key in 3*list(data.keys()):
		try:
			data[key]
		except Exception:
			traceback.print_exc()
		time.sleep(0.2) # to make access order deterministic
		pass
```

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26221","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26221/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26221/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/26221/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/26221","id":2229872322,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86E6SbC","number":26221,"title":"BUG: Inconsistent behaviour of `SeedSequence.generate_state` inside vs outside of unittests.","user":{"login":"zoj613","id":44142765,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ0MTQyNzY1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/44142765?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/zoj613","html_url":"https://github.com/zoj613","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zoj613/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zoj613/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zoj613/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zoj613/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zoj613/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zoj613/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zoj613/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zoj613/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/zoj613/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2024-04-07T17:52:34Z","updated_at":"2024-04-08T02:13:44Z","closed_at":"2024-04-07T19:15:09Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

I was looking at https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/e59c074842e3f73483afa5ddef031e856b9fd313/numpy/random/tests/test_seed_sequence.py#L7 and noticed that row 5 of the `inputs` list as a seed to `SeedSequence` produces different results that indicated by row 5 of `outputs`. Particularly, row 5 of `outputs` insists that the expected output of `generate_state(4)` is  `[1086200464, 2191331643, 560336446, 3658716651]`, but when the call to the method actually produces `[3633748596, 2539471311, 1066956888, 1033319901]`.

Is this considered a bug? Or am I misunderstanding how `SeedSequence.generate_state` is supposed to work given a fixed seed?

cc @rkern @bashtage 

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

input_row5 = [1864922766, 719732118, 3882010307, 1776744564]
ss = np.random.SeedSequence(input_row5)
ss.generate_state(len(input_row5))
>> array([3633748596, 2539471311, 1066956888, 1033319901], dtype=uint32)
# this is different from [1086200464, 2191331643, 560336446, 3658716651] as indicated by the unit test.
```


### Error message:

_No response_

### Python and NumPy Versions:

1.22.4
3.10.10 | packaged by conda-forge | (main, Mar 24 2023, 20:08:06) [GCC 11.3.0]

### Runtime Environment:

_No response_

### Context for the issue:

I would expect the tests to fail if the output is "wrong" but for some reason the tests pass if ran in that order. Is this behaviour expected? closed"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-04-05T18:22:49Z","updated_at":"2024-04-09T01:40:53Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"Following up on the licensing part of the proposed vendoring of `kokkos/mdspan`, which has an Apache 2.0 + LLVM runtime exception license (see gh-20334, https://github.com/scipy/scipy/pull/20320#pullrequestreview-1969858082).

@steppi opened https://github.com/kokkos/mdspan/issues/321 upstream to inquire about relicensing, which _may_ be possible but isn't a fast decision apparently.

First some context (copied from the review comment linked above):

The Apache 2.0 license in `mdspan.h` seems incompatible with SciPy's BSD-3 license. See:
- http://scipy.github.io/devdocs/dev/core-dev/index.html#licensing
- http://scipy.github.io/devdocs/dev/hacking.html#license-considerations
- https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/8370#issuecomment-363592943

It's not completely impossible to reconsider the current ban on Apache 2.0, as indicated by the comment in the last link above, but it's a major decision with long-term consequences that, even if we drop GPLv2 compatibility, will complicate the project's licensing situation.

* * *

After reviewing the LLVM runtime exception clause, it seems clear that that does address the GPLv2 compatibility issue, so there is no problem there.

The other problem is source-level licensing. Right now we have a BSD-3 license for SciPy as a whole (see [LICENSE.text](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/LICENSE.txt)), and a large-ish set of licenses for vendored code (see [LICENSES_bundled.txt](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/main/LICENSES_bundled.txt)). All of the licenses for bundled code are BSD-3 compatible: various other BSD flavors, MIT, Boost, and Qhull (BSD-like) licenses.

The LLVM runtime exception part of the `kokkos/mdspan` license is only related to linking. So for the license expression one would need to use to describe all of SciPy, that's not very relevant. Introducing Apache 2.0 would complicate describing the license - but perhaps not by that much. Distros tend to already exhaustively search for licensing and copyright info and mash everything together ([example for Debian](https://metadata.ftp-master.debian.org/changelogs//main/s/scipy/scipy_1.10.1-2_copyright)). So while not ideal to add this new license, it _may_ be acceptable.

Let me ask for some input here. @rkern any thoughts? @sandrotosi as a Debian packager, would you see any issues if we'd accept this extra license?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/20396/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":1},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/20396/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/comments/2043991378","html_url":"https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/20396#issuecomment-2043991378","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scipy/scipy/issues/20396","id":2043991378,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABZIoc551NVS","user":{"login":"rkern","id":46135,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjQ2MTM1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/46135?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern","html_url":"https://github.com/rkern","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/rkern/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-04-09T01:40:51Z","updated_at":"2024-04-09T01:40:51Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I think @rgommers covered everything I can think of. The `seed_seq` of a `BitGenerator` that's been round-tripped through pickle dump/load does not match with  the source. This is despite the fact that the `BitGenerator.state` (and hence the sequence of variates generated) *do* round-trip, as does a bare `SeedSequence`.

This also shows up as a deviation of child bitgens from `BitGenerator.spawn()`

I'm assuming this is an oversight, since the pickle bytestream generated from a `BitGenerator` just doesn't mention the `seed_seq` element at all, and I'm assuming it gets generated from the `SeedSequence` default initializer by fetching fresh entropy from the OS, which presumably adds a few us delay to the unpickle path.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
import pickle

bg = np.random.PCG64(np.random.SeedSequence(1))
bg2 = pickle.loads(pickle.dumps(bg))

assert bg.seed_seq == bg2.seed_seq
```
